topics.title: Familiarisation of ON-OFF, proportional, proportional plus integral, and proportional plus integral plus derivative control mode using pressure process station or flow process station and construction of ISA diagram
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL607 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarisation of ON-OFF, proportional, proportional plus integral, and proportional plus integral plus derivative control mode using pressure process station or flow process station and construction of ISA diagram

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Control Modes** are the mathematical algorithms implemented inside an automatic controller (electronic, pneumatic, or digital) that determine *how* the controller output (final control element signal, typically 3–15 psi pneumatic or 4–20 mA current) changes in response to the **Error Signal**, $e(t) = SP - PV$, where $SP$ is the Setpoint and $PV$ is the Process Variable.

For KTU 2024 Scheme Lab Course **PCICL607**, Module Experiment 2 mandates the practical familiarization of the four foundational control laws — **ON-OFF**, **Proportional (P)**, **Proportional-Integral (PI)**, and **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID)** — implemented on either a **Pressure Process Station** (e.g., a closed-loop air pressure vessel) or a **Flow Process Station** (e.g., a centrifugal pump with orifice/rotameter feedback loop). The experiment also mandates the construction of the corresponding **ISA (Instrumentation Society of America) standard diagram** as per **ISA-5.1** symbology.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Home Shower Mixer:**
> - **ON-OFF Control** = A tap that is either fully open or fully closed. The moment water is too hot, you open the cold tap fully; the moment it's too cold, you shut it completely. Result: constant fluctuation between extremes.
> - **Proportional (P)** = You adjust the tap opening *proportional* to how hot the water is. The hotter it gets, the more you close the tap. Smart, but you end up with a small steady offset (slightly warm water forever).
> - **PI Control** = Proportional + you remember the past. If you've been slightly warm for a long time, you keep adjusting until the offset disappears.
> - **PID Control** = Proportional + memory + *anticipation*. You see the temperature rising fast and preemptively close the tap a little to prevent overshoot.

The shower is your **process**, the temperature gauge is the **sensor/transmitter**, your brain is the **controller**, and your hand on the tap is the **final control element (control valve)**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Pneumatic Signal Range:** **3–15 psig** (0.2–1.0 kg/cm²) — the *lingua franca* of legacy process control.
- **Standard Electronic Signal Range:** **4–20 mA DC** (1–5 V DC is also common inside controllers).
- **Process Variable Range (example, Pressure Station):** 0–5 bar or 0–30 psi.
- **Setpoint (SP) in %:** Always expressed as 0–100 % of the controller output span for portability across instruments.
- **Error, $e(t)$:** Dimensionless (in % of span) when normalized; otherwise in engineering units.

## 1.4 The ISA Diagram — Why It Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISA-5.1** is the global standard for instrumentation symbols. In any KTU lab record, the block diagram you draw of your process station **must** use ISA notation. The diagram is a *graphical contract* between the process engineer and the control engineer — it shows every instrument, signal type (pneumatic, electric, capillary, software), and its functional relationship.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Control Modes — Operational Logic

### 2.1.1 ON-OFF Control (Two-Position / Bang-Bang)

The controller output can take **only two discrete states**:
- $u(t) = u_{max}$ when $e(t) > 0$ (process below setpoint)
- $u(t) = u_{min}$ when $e(t) < 0$ (process above setpoint)

A practical controller adds a **differential gap (deadband)** $\Delta$ to prevent the final element from chattering at high frequency around the setpoint.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. Sample error $e(t) = SP - PV$ every cycle.
2. If $e(t) > 0$ → output saturates high (e.g., 15 psi / 20 mA → valve fully open).
3. If $e(t) < 0$ → output saturates low (e.g., 3 psi / 4 mA → valve fully closed).
4. Introduce deadband $\Delta$ so the valve switches only when $|e| > \Delta / 2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Continuous oscillation around the setpoint. The amplitude of oscillation depends on process lag and deadband. ON-OFF control is **never used** where tight regulation is required, but is dominant in domestic thermostats, refrigerator compressors, and limit switches.

### 2.1.2 Proportional Control (P)

The controller output is **linearly proportional** to the instantaneous error.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. Compute $e(t) = SP - PV$.
2. Multiply by the **Proportional Gain**, $K_c$ (also called proportional band inverse, $1/PB$).
3. Add a **bias term**, $u_b$ (also called manual reset), to allow output to operate in mid-range when error is zero.
4. Saturate output at $u_{min}$ and $u_{max}$ if the computed value falls outside the controller output range.

> [!WARNING]
> **Offset** (steady-state error) is the inherent, unavoidable weakness of pure P control. The controller *needs* a non-zero error to maintain a non-bias output. Thus, the process never sits exactly at setpoint under load changes.

### 2.1.3 Proportional-Integral Control (PI)

Adds the **Integral of Error** term, which accumulates the historical error over time and drives the steady-state offset to **zero**.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. Compute $e(t) = SP - PV$.
2. P-action: $u_P = K_c \cdot e(t)$.
3. I-action: $u_I = \dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I} \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau$, where $\tau_I$ is the **Integral Time** (also called *reset time*) in seconds or minutes.
4. Sum: $u(t) = u_b + u_P + u_I$.
5. The integral term **continues to grow** as long as any error exists, so the controller output keeps pushing until the error vanishes. This eliminates offset but slows the response and can cause **integrator windup** if the actuator saturates.

### 2.1.4 Proportional-Integral-Derivative Control (PID)

Adds a **Derivative of Error** term that acts on the *rate of change* of the error, providing anticipatory / damping action.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. Compute $e(t) = SP - PV$.
2. P-action: $u_P = K_c \cdot e(t)$.
3. I-action: $u_I = \dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I} \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau$.
4. D-action: $u_D = K_c \cdot \tau_D \cdot \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$, where $\tau_D$ is the **Derivative Time** constant in seconds.
5. Sum: $u(t) = u_b + u_P + u_I + u_D$.
6. Derivative action is highly sensitive to measurement noise (it amplifies high-frequency noise), so in practice it is applied to the **PV only** (not on error) to avoid **derivative kick** during setpoint changes.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Control Mode** | **Controller Output Equation (Ideal / Academic Form)** | **Key Tuning Parameters** | **Output Range** | **Key Limitation / Behavior** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ON-OFF | $u(t) = u_{max}$ if $e > 0$, else $u_{min}$ | Deadband $\Delta$ | Discrete (2 states) | Sustained oscillation |
| P | $u(t) = u_b + K_c \cdot e(t)$ | $K_c$ (gain) or $PB = 100/K_c$ % | $u_{min}$ to $u_{max}$ | Steady-state **offset** |
| PI | $u(t) = u_b + K_c \left[ e(t) + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau \right]$ | $K_c$, $\tau_I$ (reset time) | $u_{min}$ to $u_{max}$ | **Integrator windup** risk |
| PID (Ideal) | $u(t) = u_b + K_c \left[ e(t) + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + \tau_D \dfrac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$ | $K_c$, $\tau_I$, $\tau_D$ | $u_{min}$ to $u_{max}$ | Noise amplification; setpoint kick |
| **Process Variable** | $e(t) = SP - PV$ | Error in % of span or E.U. | — | — |
| **Pneumatic Span** | 3–15 psig = 12 psi span | — | — | Process industry legacy |
| **Electronic Span** | 4–20 mA DC = 16 mA span | — | — | Modern standard |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **ON-OFF:** Domestic water heaters, air conditioners, refrigerator thermostats, level switches in sumps.
> - **P:** Simple pressure regulators, surge tanks where small offset is acceptable.
> - **PI:** **Workhorse of the process industry** — flow loops (because flow has little dead time), level loops, many pressure loops. ~90% of industrial loops are PI.
> - **PID:** Temperature loops (large dead time, slow dynamics), cascade master loops, pH control, composition control, motion control.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why P Control Produces an Offset

Consider a single-loop pressure control station. The process has steady-state gain $K_p$ (output pressure change per unit change in valve signal). The controller output is $u(t) = u_b + K_c e(t)$. At steady state, the process settles when the manipulated input stops changing the PV.

Step 1 — Define the steady-state condition. At steady state, the PV becomes constant, so the error $e_{ss}$ is constant, and the controller output $u_{ss}$ is also constant.

Step 2 — Express the process steady-state behavior. The process in steady state is $PV_{ss} = K_p \cdot u_{ss}$, assuming a zero-load baseline (linearization around operating point).

Step 3 — Substitute the controller output.

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_{ss} &= K_p \left( u_b + K_c \cdot e_{ss} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Express error at steady state, $e_{ss} = SP - PV_{ss}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_{ss} &= K_p u_b + K_p K_c (SP - PV_{ss}) \\
PV_{ss} + K_p K_c \cdot PV_{ss} &= K_p u_b + K_p K_c \cdot SP \\
PV_{ss} \left( 1 + K_p K_c \right) &= K_p \left( u_b + K_c \cdot SP \right) \\
PV_{ss} &= \frac{K_p \left( u_b + K_c \cdot SP \right)}{1 + K_p K_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 5 — Compute the offset.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offset} = SP - PV_{ss} &= SP - \frac{K_p \left( u_b + K_c \cdot SP \right)}{1 + K_p K_c} \\
&= \frac{SP \left( 1 + K_p K_c \right) - K_p \left( u_b + K_c \cdot SP \right)}{1 + K_p K_c} \\
&= \frac{SP + K_p K_c SP - K_p u_b - K_p K_c SP}{1 + K_p K_c} \\
&= \frac{SP - K_p u_b}{1 + K_p K_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 6 — Interpretation. As long as the bias $u_b$ is *not* perfectly tuned to the process operating point (i.e., $K_p u_b \neq SP$), an **offset persists**. The higher $K_c$ is, the smaller the offset, but the offset never reaches zero for any finite $K_c$. **This is the fundamental motivation for adding the integral term.**

## 3.2 Derivation: How Integral Action Eliminates Offset

In PI control, the controller output is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u(t) = u_b + K_c e(t) + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 1 — At steady state, $e(t) = e_{ss}$ is constant (we don't yet know if it's zero), and the integral term grows linearly with time: $\int_0^t e_{ss} d\tau = e_{ss} \cdot t$.

Step 2 — Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{ss}(t) = u_b + K_c e_{ss} + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I} e_{ss} \cdot t
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — The process cannot sustain an ever-increasing controller output forever — the actuator will saturate. The **only physically stable steady state** is one where the integral term stops growing, which requires $e_{ss} = 0$. Otherwise, the controller output ramps and the process PV keeps moving until the actuator saturates.

Step 4 — Conclusion: $e_{ss} = 0 \Rightarrow PV_{ss} = SP$. **Offset is eliminated by the integral action.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a beautiful piece of control theory: the integral action is essentially a self-adjusting bias. The bias $u_b$ in P control was a *fixed* constant that could not track process changes; the integral term is a *self-adjusting* bias that always converges to the value needed to keep error zero.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Response Characteristics of Each Mode (Lab-Observation Table)

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is what you would fill in your lab record after observing step responses on the pressure/flow station. It is the *core grading rubric* in the KTU lab viva.

| **Performance Metric** | **ON-OFF** | **P** | **PI** | **PID** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Steady-state error (offset) | Oscillates, no steady state | Non-zero offset | **Zero** | **Zero** |
| Peak overshoot | Large (touches limits) | Moderate | Moderate-High | Lowest (well-tuned) |
| Rise time | Fastest (saturates) | Fast | Slower | Slower (D slows it) |
| Settling time | Never settles (oscillates) | Quick but offset remains | Longer due to I action | Fastest *if well-tuned* |
| Stability | Marginally stable (oscillatory) | Stable with offset | Stable, can be oscillatory | Most stable when tuned |
| Sensitivity to noise | Low | Low | Low | **High** (D amplifies noise) |
| Tuning effort | None (one parameter: deadband) | Easy (one knob) | Moderate (two knobs) | Difficult (three knobs) |
| Energy / actuator wear | **Highest** (chattering) | Moderate | Moderate | Lowest |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: PI Tuning via Cohen-Coon Open-Loop Method

A flow process station is open-loop step-tested. The process reaction curve gives:
- Process Gain, $K_p = 0.8$ (%PV / %CO)
- Dead Time, $L = 30$ s
- Time Constant, $T = 120$ s

Step 1 — Compute Cohen-Coon PI tuning parameters:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_c &= \frac{1}{K_p} \cdot \frac{T}{L} \left( 0.9 + \frac{L}{12T} \right) \\
\tau_I &= L \cdot \frac{30 + 3(L/T)}{9 + 20(L/T)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Evaluate $L/T = 30/120 = 0.25$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_c &= \frac{1}{0.8} \cdot \frac{120}{30} \left( 0.9 + \frac{0.25}{12} \right) \\
&= 1.25 \cdot 4 \cdot (0.9 + 0.02083) \\
&= 5 \cdot 0.92083 \\
&= 4.604
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_I &= 30 \cdot \frac{30 + 3(0.25)}{9 + 20(0.25)} \\
&= 30 \cdot \frac{30 + 0.75}{9 + 5} \\
&= 30 \cdot \frac{30.75}{14} \\
&= 30 \cdot 2.1964 \\
&= 65.89 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — The controller is then configured with $K_c = 4.6$ and $\tau_I = 66$ s. The student records the closed-loop step response, observes zero offset, and notes the rise time and overshoot for the lab record.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ISA-5.1 Standard Block Diagram of the Pressure Process Station with PID Control

Below is a complete ISA-5.1 compliant schematic for the lab experiment. Every bubble is tagged with the standard ISA tag-numbering convention: **First Letter = Measured Variable, Subsequent Letters = Function**.

```
ISA-5.1 Reference Tags Used:
  PT  = Pressure Transmitter
  PIC = Pressure Indicating Controller
  PV  = Pressure Valve (control valve)
  F   = Flow (if used)
  I   = Indicator
  C   = Controller
  T   = Transmitter
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint SP]:::inputNode
    PIC["PIC-100\nPressure Indicating Controller\n4-20 mA, PID Mode"]:::controller
    PT["PT-101\nPressure Transmitter\nBourdon / Strain Gauge\n4-20 mA"]:::transmitter
    PV["PV-100\nPneumatic Control Valve\n3-15 psig, Air-to-Close"]:::valve
    VESSEL["Pressure Vessel\nProcess Variable PV\n0-5 bar"]:::process
    PV_DISP["PI-102\nPressure Indicator\nLocal + Panel"]:::indicator

    SP -->|"Setpoint Signal\n4-20 mA"| PIC
    PIC -->|"Controller Output\n4-20 mA or 3-15 psig"| PV
    PV -->|"Air Pressure\nManipulates Inflow"| VESSEL
    VESSEL -->|"Process Fluid\nPressure rises"| PT
    PT -->|"Feedback PV\n4-20 mA"| PIC
    PT -.->|"Tapping for Indication"| PV_DISP

    classDef inputNode fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef controller fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef transmitter fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef valve fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef indicator fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Internal Working of a PID Controller Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[SP Setpoint\n4-20 mA]:::input
    B[PV Process Variable\n4-20 mA from PT]:::input
    C["Summing Junction\ne = SP - PV"]:::junction
    D["P Block\nKc x e(t)"]:::pblock
    E["I Block\nKc / tI x integral of e dt"]:::iblock
    F["D Block\nKc x tD x de/dt"]:::dblock
    G["Summing Junction\nuP + uI + uD + Bias"]:::junction
    H["Output to Valve\n4-20 mA or 3-15 psig"]:::output
    I["Anti-Windup\nClamp + Back-Calc"]:::protect

    A --> C
    B --> C
    C --> D
    C --> E
    C --> F
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H -.->|"Feedback to I-block\npreventing windup"| I
    I -.-> E

    classDef input fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef junction fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pblock fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef iblock fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dblock fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef protect fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Closed-Loop Step Response Comparison Chart (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Step Response Curves Observed in Lab"
    A[Time Axis 0 to T]:::axis
    B["ON-OFF: Square-wave-like\noscillation around SP"]:::oon
    C["P: Fast rise, levels off\nbelow SP, has offset"]:::pctrl
    D["PI: Reaches SP exactly\nslight overshoot possible"]:::pictrl
    E["PID: Fast, minimal overshoot\nreaches SP exactly"]:::pidctrl
    F["Setpoint Line SP"]:::setpt
    end

    A ~~~ B
    A ~~~ C
    A ~~~ D
    A ~~~ E
    A ~~~ F

    classDef axis fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef setpt fill:#FF4500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef oon fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pctrl fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pictrl fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pidctrl fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

> [!NOTE]
> In your lab record, the step response curves must be drawn with axes properly labeled: **X-axis = Time (seconds)**, **Y-axis = PV (in % of span or in E.U.)**, with the setpoint shown as a horizontal dashed line.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, June 2024]
**Define Proportional Band (PB) and state its relationship with Proportional Gain $K_c$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Proportional Band (PB) is defined as the range of error (expressed as a percentage of the controller input span) required to drive the controller output through its full operating range (typically 0–100% or 3–15 psig). The relationship with Proportional Gain $K_c$ is given by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PB (\%) = \frac{100}{K_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

A higher $K_c$ corresponds to a narrower PB, meaning the controller is more sensitive to small errors. Conversely, a low $K_c$ corresponds to a wide PB and a sluggish controller. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023]
**What is Integrator Windup, and how is it prevented?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Integrator Windup is a phenomenon in PI and PID controllers where the integral term continues to accumulate error even after the controller output has saturated at its physical limits ($u_{min}$ or $u_{max}$). When the setpoint or load changes drastically, the error stays large, the integral term grows without bound, and even after the error changes sign, the controller cannot respond until the integral term *unwinds*.

It is prevented by:
1. **Clamping / Conditional Integration:** Stop integrating when the output saturates.
2. **Back-Calculation:** Compute the difference between the limited and unlimited output and feed it back to reduce the integral.
3. **External reset feedback** from the actual valve position.

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern for lab courses, each Part B question carries 14 marks with internal choice. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are fully independent alternatives.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the construction of the **ISA-5.1 standard block diagram** for a pressure process station fitted with a PID controller. Label all instruments with proper ISA tag numbers. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The ISA-5.1 standard diagram for a closed-loop pressure control system is constructed as follows:

**1. Process Identification:** The pressure vessel is the process under control. The **Process Variable (PV)** is the pressure inside the vessel, measured in bar or psi.

**2. Primary Sensing Element & Transmitter:** A Bourdon tube or strain-gauge pressure sensor is fitted on the vessel. The transmitter converts the pressure signal into a standardized **4–20 mA** electronic signal. ISA tag: **PT-101** (Pressure Transmitter, loop number 101).

**3. Controller:** A Pressure Indicating Controller, tagged **PIC-100**, accepts the 4–20 mA feedback from PT-101 and compares it with the local/remote setpoint. The controller implements the PID algorithm and outputs a 4–20 mA (or 3–15 psig if pneumatic) signal to the valve.

**4. Final Control Element:** A pneumatic diaphragm-operated control valve tagged **PV-100** (Pressure Valve) regulates the inflow of air/fluid into the vessel. The valve position is manipulated by the controller output.

**5. Auxiliary Indicator:** A local pressure indicator **PI-102** is connected via a pressure tap to allow the operator to read the PV.

**6. Signal Lines:** Solid lines with arrows denote electronic (4–20 mA) signals; dashed lines denote pneumatic (3–15 psig) signals; per ISA-5.1.

A complete ISA-compliant schematic is given in **Section 4.1** of these notes, with all four bubbles (PT-101, PIC-100, PV-100, PI-102) connected with signal lines and arrows showing the direction of information flow. **(7 marks — full marks for correct tag numbering, signal direction, and bubble symbology)**

---

**(b)** A pressure process station has $K_p = 2.5$, dead time $L = 20$ s, and time constant $T = 90$ s. Using the **Cohen-Coon** open-loop tuning method, compute the PI controller settings $K_c$ and $\tau_I$. Comment on the expected closed-loop response. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Cohen-Coon PI formulae:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_c &= \frac{1}{K_p} \cdot \frac{T}{L} \left( 0.9 + \frac{L}{12T} \right) \\
\tau_I &= L \cdot \frac{30 + 3(L/T)}{9 + 20(L/T)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.** Here $K_p = 2.5$, $T = 90$ s, $L = 20$ s.

Compute the ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{L}{T} &= \frac{20}{90} = 0.2222
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $K_c$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_c &= \frac{1}{2.5} \cdot \frac{90}{20} \left( 0.9 + \frac{0.2222}{12} \right) \\
&= 0.4 \cdot 4.5 \cdot (0.9 + 0.01852) \\
&= 1.8 \cdot 0.91852 \\
&= 1.6533
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\tau_I$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_I &= 20 \cdot \frac{30 + 3(0.2222)}{9 + 20(0.2222)} \\
&= 20 \cdot \frac{30 + 0.6667}{9 + 4.444} \\
&= 20 \cdot \frac{30.6667}{13.444} \\
&= 20 \cdot 2.2812 \\
&= 45.62 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Expected closed-loop response:**
- $K_c = 1.65$ and $\tau_I = 45.6$ s are entered into the PIC-100 controller.
- Step change in setpoint will yield a response with a quarter-amplitude damping ratio (Cohen-Coon target).
- Rise time approximately equal to $L = 20$ s, settling time around $4L = 80$ s.
- Steady-state offset will be **zero** because the integral term is active.
- A small overshoot (~20–25%) is expected; if overshoot is unacceptable, the student may detune by reducing $K_c$ by 10%.

**(7 marks — 1 mark for formula, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for numerical result, 2 marks for expected response comment)**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Compare the **ON-OFF, P, PI, and PID** control modes with respect to: (i) steady-state error, (ii) overshoot, (iii) stability, and (iv) typical industrial applications. Tabulate your answer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| **Mode** | **(i) Steady-State Error** | **(ii) Overshoot** | **(iii) Stability** | **(iv) Typical Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ON-OFF** | Sustained oscillation, no true steady state | 100% (touches limits) | Marginally stable (limit cycle) | Refrigerators, thermostats, level switches |
| **P** | Non-zero offset | Moderate | Stable with offset | Simple pressure regulators, surge tanks |
| **PI** | **Zero offset** | Moderate–High | Stable but may oscillate if mistuned | **Flow loops, level loops, ~90% of industrial processes** |
| **PID** | **Zero offset** | Lowest (well-tuned) | Most stable when properly tuned | Temperature loops, cascade master, motion control |

**Key additional points (for full marks):**
- ON-OFF is the simplest and cheapest; PID is the most complex.
- Derivative action is never used alone (always combined with P or PI).
- The derivative term **amplifies measurement noise**; hence it is not preferred for noisy processes like flow.
- The integral term is the reason PI and PID achieve zero offset, but it slows down the response. **(7 marks — full table plus 2-3 explanatory points)**

---

**(b)** With a clear block diagram, derive the **transfer function of an ideal PID controller** and show how the **proportional, integral, and derivative** terms individually act on the error signal $e(t) = SP - PV$. Also explain why in practice the derivative is taken on PV only, not on error. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — The ideal (academic) PID control law** in the time domain is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u(t) = K_c \, e(t) + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I} \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_c \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} + u_b
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $K_c$ = proportional gain
- $\tau_I$ = integral time (reset time) in seconds
- $\tau_D$ = derivative time in seconds
- $u_b$ = controller bias (manual reset)

**Step 2 — Laplace Transform to obtain the transfer function.** Taking the Laplace transform (assuming zero initial conditions and ignoring bias):

$$
\begin{aligned}
U(s) &= K_c \, E(s) + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I} \cdot \frac{E(s)}{s} + K_c \tau_D \cdot s E(s) \\
U(s) &= K_c \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s \right] E(s)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Ideal PID transfer function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_c(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_c \left( 1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Individual term action (block diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes):**
- **Proportional block:** produces a control effort proportional to *current* error.
- **Integral block:** produces a control effort proportional to the *accumulated past* error; eliminates offset.
- **Derivative block:** produces a control effort proportional to the *rate of change* of error; provides anticipation and damping.

**Step 5 — Why derivative is taken on PV only:** When a sudden setpoint change occurs, the error $e(t) = SP - PV$ shows an **instantaneous step**. The derivative of a step is an **impulse (delta function)**, which causes a huge, brief spike in the controller output. This is called **"derivative kick"** and can damage the actuator and disturb the process.

By contrast, the PV cannot change instantaneously (process has inertia), so $d(PV)/dt$ is bounded, and the derivative action is well-behaved. In practice, the controller computes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u(t) = K_c \left[ (SP - PV) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int e\, dt - \tau_D \frac{d(PV)}{dt} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7 marks — 1 mark PID equation, 2 marks Laplace derivation, 2 marks individual term explanation, 2 marks derivative-on-PV reasoning)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in PCICL607 Lab Exam Records:**
> 1. **Wrong ISA Tag Numbering:** Tag must be of the form *[Function Letters]-[Loop Number]*, e.g., `PIC-100`, NOT `P-100` or `PressureIndicator1`. The loop number ties all instruments on the same loop.
> 2. **Forgetting the Bias Term:** When writing the P-control equation, students often omit the bias $u_b$. This costs a full mark.
> 3. **Confusing $K_c$ with $K_p$:** $K_c$ is the *controller* gain; $K_p$ is the *process* gain. Mixing them up makes the offset derivation invalid.
> 4. **Drawing PV Curves without axes labels:** The lab graph must have **Time (s) on X-axis** and **PV (% of span or E.U.) on Y-axis**, with the setpoint shown as a dashed horizontal line. Without this, the graph carries zero credit.
> 5. **Skipping the deadband in ON-OFF:** Always mention the differential gap / deadband $\Delta$ and explain its role in preventing relay chattering.
> 6. **Derivative on error vs. on PV:** This is a 2-mark differentiator. Always state that industrial controllers apply derivative on PV to avoid *derivative kick*.
> 7. **Confusing reset rate with reset time:** $\tau_I$ is in **seconds per repeat** (reset time); $1/\tau_I$ is in **repeats per second** (reset rate). Examiners expect this distinction.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU PCICL607 Module Experiment 2:**

- **ON-OFF:** Two-position output, deadband $\Delta$ prevents chattering, sustained oscillation around SP, used in domestic thermostats.
- **P Control:** $u(t) = u_b + K_c e(t)$, **offset always present**, gain $K_c = 100/PB$ (%), high $K_c$ = narrow band = aggressive.
- **PI Control:** $u(t) = u_b + K_c e(t) + (K_c/\tau_I) \int e\, dt$, **eliminates offset**, risk of **integrator windup** (use anti-windup / clamping / back-calculation).
- **PID Control:** Adds derivative $K_c \tau_D (de/dt)$, derivative applied to **PV only** in practice to avoid derivative kick, sensitive to noise (low-pass filter the PV).
- **ISA-5.1 Tag Convention:** Function-Letters-LoopNumber, e.g., **PIC-100** (Pressure Indicating Controller in loop 100), **PT-101** (Pressure Transmitter in loop 101), **PV-100** (Pressure Valve), **PI-102** (Pressure Indicator).
- **Standard Signals:** Electronic = **4–20 mA DC**; Pneumatic = **3–15 psig**; Computer / Fieldbus = digital packets.
- **Tuning Methods (must know for viva):** Cohen-Coon (open-loop), Ziegler-Nichols (both open-loop reaction curve and closed-loop ultimate cycle).
- **Cohen-Coon PI formulas:**
  - $K_c = (1/K_p)(T/L)(0.9 + L/12T)$
  - $\tau_I = L \cdot (30 + 3L/T)/(9 + 20L/T)$
- **Performance Metrics to Tabulate in Lab Record:** Rise time, peak overshoot, settling time, steady-state error, IAE / ISE (integral of absolute / squared error).
- **Practical Industrial Rule of Thumb:** ~90% of control loops in a typical refinery/chemical plant are **PI**, ~5% are PID (mostly temperature), and ~5% are ON-OFF / specialized.
- **Lab Procedure Order:** (1) Open-loop step test to identify $K_p$, $L$, $T$. (2) Switch to P, increase $K_c$ until offset is small but stable. (3) Add I, reduce $K_c$ by 10%, increase $\tau_I$ gradually. (4) If needed, add D, tune $\tau_D$ for minimal overshoot. (5) Record closed-loop step response and tabulate metrics.
- **The Big Picture:** P acts on *now*, I acts on *the past*, D acts on *the future*. Master all three and you master classical process control.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarisation of ON-OFF, proportional, proportional plus integral, and proportional plus integral plus derivative control mode using level process station or temperature process station and construction of ISA diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL607 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarisation of ON-OFF, proportional, proportional plus integral, and proportional plus integral plus derivative control mode using level process station or temperature process station and construction of ISA diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Process Control Station** is a pilot-scale laboratory setup that emulates an industrial process (level, temperature, flow, or pressure) and allows the user to study the closed-loop dynamic behavior of the system under different controller modes. The four fundamental control modes examined in this experiment are:

1. **ON-OFF Control Mode** – A discontinuous (two-position) control action where the final control element is either fully ON or fully OFF depending on the sign and magnitude of the error signal.
2. **Proportional (P) Control Mode** – A continuous control action where the controller output is linearly proportional to the instantaneous error between the measured process variable (PV) and the setpoint (SP).
3. **Proportional-Integral (PI) Control Mode** – Combines proportional action with integral action to eliminate the steady-state (offset) error inherent in pure P-control.
4. **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) Control Mode** – Augments the PI action with derivative action to anticipate future error based on its rate of change, thereby improving transient response and stability.

The **ISA Diagram** (per **ISA 5.1 Standard – Instrumentation Symbols and Identification**) is a standardized schematic representation of the measurement and control loop, depicting field instruments, signal interconnections, control valves, and control room architecture using circles (bubbles), lines, and alphanumeric tag identifiers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PCICL607):** Students are expected to (a) experimentally observe the response of a level/temperature process under each of the four controller modes, (b) record performance metrics (rise time, peak overshoot, settling time, steady-state error, IAE/ISE), and (c) draw the **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)** or **Instrumentation Loop Diagram** using **ISA 5.1** symbology.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Shower-Mixer Story"

Imagine you are taking a shower and the water temperature is your **Process Variable (PV)**.

- **ON-OFF Mode** = A person with only two hands, one completely on the cold tap, one completely on the hot tap. They keep flipping back and forth — you either freeze or scald. There is a small **dead band** (hysteresis) so they don't flip too fast.
- **Proportional (P) Mode** = You adjust the *mixer knob* in proportion to how far the temperature is from your comfort setpoint. If the water is *very* cold, you turn the knob a *lot*. If it is *slightly* cold, you turn it a *little*. But the water is *never* exactly at your setpoint — there's always a small residual "lukewarm" offset.
- **PI Mode** = Now imagine you **also** keep a mental timer: if the water has been slightly off for a long time, you automatically keep nudging the knob. This eliminates the lingering offset completely.
- **PID Mode** = You additionally *predict*: if the temperature is dropping fast (rate of change), you preemptively turn the hot water up even before the error becomes large. This anticipates the disturbance and gives a smoother, faster response.

### 1.3 Standard Process Station Hardware

A typical **Level Process Station** consists of:
- A **transparent acrylic/perspex tank** (process tank)
- A **reservoir tank** (supply)
- A **centrifugal pump** or **pneumatic supply**
- A **pneumatic control valve** (or motorized) on the outlet/inlet
- A **level transmitter** (DPT, capacitance probe, or ultrasonic)
- A **digital PID controller** (software-based or hardware DCS module)
- A **SCADA/PC interface** for data logging

A **Temperature Process Station** consists of:
- A **heated tank/vessel** with an electrical heater
- A **temperature sensor** (RTD PT100 or Type-K thermocouple)
- A **temperature transmitter**
- A **cooling water/cooling coil system**
- A **solid-state relay (SSR)** or **SCR power controller** as the final control element
- A **PID controller** with auto-tuning

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Reference:** All instrumentation symbology in this experiment follows **ANSI/ISA-5.1-2009 (R2014) – Instrumentation Symbols and Identification**. Letters inside the circle denote the measured variable (e.g., **L** = Level, **T** = Temperature, **F** = Flow, **P** = Pressure) and the function (e.g., **T** = Transmitter, **C** = Controller, **I** = Indicator, **CV** = Control Valve).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 ON-OFF Control Mode — Operational Logic

**Step-by-step logic:**

- Compute error: $e(t) = SP - PV(t)$
- If $e(t) > 0$ (PV below SP) → Output = **100%** (fully ON)
- If $e(t) < 0$ (PV above SP) → Output = **0%** (fully OFF)
- If $\vert e(t) \vert \leq \Delta$ (inside dead band) → Output retains last state (hysteresis prevents chattering)

**Why it works:** ON-OFF is the simplest and cheapest controller (used in domestic thermostats, refrigerators, iron boxes). Its limitation is the inherent **limit cycle oscillation** around the setpoint.

**Key Characteristics:**
- No tuning parameters (other than dead band)
- Continuous oscillation (cycling) around SP
- Large amplitude oscillation for slow processes
- Suitable for non-critical applications

### 2.2 Proportional (P) Control Mode

**Step-by-step logic:**

- Compute error: $e(t) = SP - PV(t)$
- Output: $u(t) = K_c \cdot e(t) + u_{bias}$
- Where $K_c$ is the **proportional gain** (dimensionless) and $u_{bias}$ is the controller output when error = 0 (also called the **setpoint of the valve**, often 50%)

**Why it works:** The bigger the error, the harder the controller "pushes" the final control element. This produces a faster, smoother response than ON-OFF.

**Limitation:** Because the controller needs a non-zero error to produce a non-zero output (it can only "hold" the valve at one position), it always leaves a **residual steady-state error (offset)**.

**Proportional Band (PB):** The percentage change in PV required to drive the output from 0% to 100%.

$$PB(\%) = \frac{100}{K_c}$$

### 2.3 Proportional-Integral (PI) Control Mode

**Step-by-step logic:**

- Compute error: $e(t) = SP - PV(t)$
- Output: $u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau \right] + u_{bias}$
- The integral term **accumulates** the error over time and produces an output that grows as long as any error exists.

**Why it works:** Even if the proportional action alone would leave a small offset, the integral term keeps adding to the output until the error is driven to zero. Thus **PI control eliminates steady-state error**.

**Key Tuning Parameter:**
- $T_i$ = **Integral (Reset) Time** in minutes/seconds — the time required for the integral action to repeat (duplicate) the proportional action.
- If $T_i$ is too small → integral action is too aggressive → oscillations
- If $T_i$ is too large → slow elimination of offset

### 2.4 Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) Control Mode

**Step-by-step logic:**

- Compute error: $e(t) = SP - PV(t)$
- Compute rate of change: $\frac{de(t)}{dt}$
- Output: $u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right] + u_{bias}$

**Why it works:** The derivative term **anticipates** future error. If the error is growing rapidly (process is moving away from setpoint quickly), the derivative action immediately applies a strong corrective force — acting like a "predictor" or "damper".

**Key Tuning Parameter:**
- $T_d$ = **Derivative Time** — the time by which the derivative action advances the controller output relative to a pure P-action.
- $T_d$ is in minutes/seconds.
- If $T_d$ is too large → excessive damping, very slow response
- If $T_d$ is too small → noisy derivative action, instability

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the exam-ready formula bank for Module Experiment 2.**

| **#** | **Control Mode** | **Time-Domain Equation** | **Laplace-Domain Transfer Function** | **Key Parameter** | **Tuning Units** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | ON-OFF | $u(t) = U_{max}$ if $e>+\Delta$, else $U_{min}$ | $G_c(s) = \dfrac{U_{max}}{\Delta}$ (approx.) | Dead band $\Delta$ | % of span |
| 2 | P | $u(t) = K_c e(t) + u_b$ | $G_c(s) = K_c$ | $K_c$ (gain) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | PI | $u(t) = K_c\!\left[e + \dfrac{1}{T_i}\!\int e\,dt\right] + u_b$ | $G_c(s) = K_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s}\right)$ | $K_c$, $T_i$ | $K_c$ dimensionless; $T_i$ in min or s |
| 4 | PID | $u(t) = K_c\!\left[e + \dfrac{1}{T_i}\!\int e\,dt + T_d\dfrac{de}{dt}\right] + u_b$ | $G_c(s) = K_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$ | $K_c$, $T_i$, $T_d$ | $T_d$ in min or s |
| 5 | Proportional Band | $PB(\%) = \dfrac{100}{K_c}$ | — | — | % |
| 6 | Process Variable Error | $e(t) = SP - PV(t)$ | $E(s) = R(s) - Y(s)$ | — | Engineering units |
| 7 | Performance — Overshoot | $M_p(\%) = \dfrac{y_{peak} - y_{ss}}{y_{ss}}\times 100$ | — | — | % |
| 8 | Performance — Settling Time | $t_s$ at $\pm 2\%$ or $\pm 5\%$ band | — | — | seconds |
| 9 | Performance — Steady-State Error | $e_{ss} = \lim_{t\to\infty} e(t)$ | $e_{ss} = \lim_{s\to 0} sE(s)$ | — | Engineering units |
| 10 | Performance — IAE | $IAE = \int_0^{\infty} \vert e(t)\vert\,dt$ | — | — | unit·seconds |
| 11 | Performance — ISE | $ISE = \int_0^{\infty} e(t)^2\,dt$ | — | — | (unit)²·s |
| 12 | Performance — ITAE | $ITAE = \int_0^{\infty} t\,\vert e(t)\vert\,dt$ | — | — | unit·s² |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **Typical Application** | **Mode Used** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Domestic Refrigerator | Compressor on/off | ON-OFF |
| Air-conditioner (older) | Compressor cycling | ON-OFF with dead band |
| Boiler drum level | Level control | P / PI |
| Chemical reactor temperature | Exothermic reaction cooling | PI / PID |
| pH neutralization | Wastewater treatment | PI / PID |
| HVAC zone control | Room temperature | PI |
| Cruise control (automobile) | Speed regulation | PID |
| Robotic arm positioning | Motor servo | P / PD / PID |

### 2.7 Ziegler-Nichols Tuning Rules (Open-Loop Method)

Used in the lab to find initial $K_c$, $T_i$, $T_d$ values from a **step-response test** on the open-loop process:

| **Controller Type** | $K_c$ | $T_i$ | $T_d$ |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| P | $\dfrac{T}{L \cdot K}$ | — | — |
| PI | $\dfrac{0.9\,T}{L \cdot K}$ | $3.33\,L$ | — |
| PID | $\dfrac{1.2\,T}{L \cdot K}$ | $2.0\,L$ | $0.5\,L$ |

Where $K$ is the process gain, $L$ is the apparent dead time, and $T$ is the time constant — all obtained from the open-loop step response.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Proportional Band Relationship

**Step 1 — Define Proportional Gain.** The proportional gain $K_c$ is defined as the ratio of change in controller output to change in error:

$$K_c = \frac{\Delta u}{\Delta e}$$

**Step 2 — Express in Percentage Form.** Let the controller output be normalized over its full range (0% to 100%) and the error over the measurement span (0% to 100% of PV range):

$$K_c = \frac{\Delta u (\%)}{\Delta e (\%)} = \frac{100\% - 0\%}{100\% - 0\% \text{ of span required for } 100\% \text{ output change}}$$

**Step 3 — Define Proportional Band (PB).** The proportional band is the percentage of the measurement span over which the error must change to drive the output through its entire range (0% to 100%):

$$PB = \frac{1}{K_c} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Final Result.**

$$\boxed{PB(\%) = \frac{100}{K_c}}$$

> **Logic:** A small PB (large $K_c$) means the controller reacts aggressively to small errors. A large PB (small $K_c$) means the controller reacts sluggishly.

### 3.2 Derivation of Steady-State Error in Pure P-Control

**Step 1 — Open-Loop Process Model.** Assume a first-order plus dead-time (FOPDT) process:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}\,e^{-Ls}$$

**Step 2 — Closed-Loop Transfer Function for Setpoint Tracking.** With a proportional controller $G_c(s) = K_c$:

$$T(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{K_c\,G_p(s)}{1 + K_c\,G_p(s)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Steady-State Output for Unit Step Setpoint.** Apply the Final Value Theorem (assuming the closed-loop is stable):

$$y_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1}{s} \cdot \frac{K_c\,G_p(s)}{1 + K_c\,G_p(s)}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Limit.** Substitute $s = 0$ in $G_p(s)$ (the dead-time term evaluates to 1):

$$y_{ss} = \frac{K_c \cdot K}{1 + K_c \cdot K}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Steady-State Error.**

$$e_{ss} = r_{ss} - y_{ss} = 1 - \frac{K_c K}{1 + K_c K} = \frac{1}{1 + K_c K}$$

**Step 6 — Final Result.**

$$\boxed{e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_c K} \neq 0 \text{ for finite } K_c, K}$$

> **Logic:** A pure proportional controller **always** leaves an offset. To eliminate it, one must add the **integral mode** which drives the denominator to infinity.

### 3.3 Why PI Control Eliminates Offset

**Step 1 — Add Integral Action.** Now $G_c(s) = K_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s}\right) = K_c \cdot \dfrac{T_i s + 1}{T_i s}$

**Step 2 — Reapply the Final Value Theorem.**

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1}{s} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)}$$

**Step 3 — Examine the Integrator Pole.** As $s \to 0$, the term $\dfrac{1}{T_i s} \to \infty$, so the open-loop gain $G_c G_p \to \infty$:

$$1 + G_c(s) G_p(s) \to \infty$$

**Step 4 — Result.**

$$\boxed{e_{ss} = 0 \text{ (offset is eliminated)}}$$

### 3.4 Tuning Calculation — Worked Example

**Given:** Open-loop step test on a level process yields:
- Process gain $K = 0.8$ (% level / % valve opening)
- Dead time $L = 30$ s
- Time constant $\tau = 120$ s

**Required:** Ziegler-Nichols (Open-Loop) tuning parameters for **PI** and **PID** controllers.

#### Solution for PI Controller

**Step 1 — Apply the Z-N formula for $K_c$:**

$$K_c = \frac{0.9 \cdot \tau}{K \cdot L} = \frac{0.9 \times 120}{0.8 \times 30} = \frac{108}{24} = 4.5$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Z-N formula for $T_i$:**

$$T_i = 3.33 \cdot L = 3.33 \times 30 = 99.9 \text{ s} \approx 100 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert $K_c$ to Proportional Band:**

$$PB = \frac{100}{K_c} = \frac{100}{4.5} = 22.22\%$$

**Result:** $K_c = 4.5$, $T_i \approx 100$ s, $PB \approx 22.2\%$.

#### Solution for PID Controller

**Step 1 — Compute $K_c$:**

$$K_c = \frac{1.2 \cdot \tau}{K \cdot L} = \frac{1.2 \times 120}{0.8 \times 30} = \frac{144}{24} = 6.0$$

**Step 2 — Compute $T_i$:**

$$T_i = 2.0 \cdot L = 2.0 \times 30 = 60 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $T_d$:**

$$T_d = 0.5 \cdot L = 0.5 \times 30 = 15 \text{ s}$$

**Result:** $K_c = 6.0$, $T_i = 60$ s, $T_d = 15$ s.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Performance Comparison

| **Performance Metric** | **ON-OFF** | **P** | **PI** | **PID** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Steady-State Error (Offset) | Oscillates around SP | Non-zero (offset exists) | **Zero** | **Zero** |
| Peak Overshoot | High (limit cycle) | Moderate | Moderate-High | **Lowest (if well tuned)** |
| Settling Time | ∞ (continuous cycle) | Fast | Moderate | **Fastest** |
| Rise Time | Fastest (saturates) | Fast | Moderate | **Fast** |
| Stability | Marginally stable | Stable | Stable | **Stable (if tuned)** |
| Disturbance Rejection | Poor | Moderate | Good | **Best** |
| Noise Sensitivity | Low | Low | Low | **High (derivative amplifies noise)** |
| Tuning Complexity | None | 1 parameter | 2 parameters | 3 parameters |
| Implementation Cost | **Lowest** | Low | Moderate | **Highest** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ISA 5.1 Instrumentation Symbology Legend

The following Mermaid block presents a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of a complete level/temperature control loop with the corresponding ISA tag identifiers and signal interconnections.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph field["FIELD AREA (Plant)"]
        TT["TT-101\nTemperature\nTransmitter"]
        PT["PT-102\nPressure/DPT\nLevel Transmitter"]
        E1["E-101\nHeater Element"]
        V1["V-101\nControl Valve"]
        T1["T-101\nProcess Tank"]
    end

    subgraph control["CONTROL ROOM (DCS / Software PID)"]
        TIC["TIC-101\nTemperature\nIndicating Controller"]
        LIC["LIC-102\nLevel\nIndicating Controller"]
        HMI["HMI / SCADA\nOperator Station"]
    end

    TT -->|4-20 mA| TIC
    PT -->|4-20 mA| LIC
    TIC -->|3-15 psi / 4-20 mA| E1
    LIC -->|3-15 psi / 4-20 mA| V1
    TIC <-->|Ethernet / Fieldbus| HMI
    LIC <-->|Ethernet / Fieldbus| HMI

    style field fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,stroke-width:2px
    style control fill:#e2ecfd,stroke:#33c,stroke-width:2px
    style TT fill:#fff,stroke:#333
    style PT fill:#fff,stroke:#333
    style E1 fill:#fff,stroke:#333
    style V1 fill:#fff,stroke:#333
    style TIC fill:#fff,stroke:#333
    style LIC fill:#fff,stroke:#333
    style HMI fill:#fff,stroke:#333
```

### 4.2 ISA 5.1 Symbol Conventions Used in This Experiment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISA Tag Identification Format:** `XY-NNN` where **X** is the measured variable letter, **Y** is the function letter, and **NNN** is the loop number.

| **Tag Letter (X)** | **Measured Variable** | **Tag Letter (Y)** | **Function** |

| **L** | Level | **C** | Controller |
