<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

What is meant by "offset" in a proportional controller? Can it be eliminated by increasing the proportional gain $K_c$ indefinitely? Justify with the steady-state error expression.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Offset** (also called **steady-state error** or **droop**) is the persistent, non-zero error that remains between the setpoint (SP) and the process variable (PV) once the closed-loop response has settled, when a pure proportional controller is used.

For a first-order process with gain $K$ and proportional gain $K_c$, the steady-state error for a unit step setpoint change is:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_c K}$$

**Can it be eliminated by increasing $K_c$?** No. As $K_c \to \infty$, $e_{ss} \to 0$ *in theory*, but in practice, a very large $K_c$ makes the closed-loop system **unstable** (oscillatory, then divergent). Furthermore, no real controller has infinite gain. Hence offset is **fundamentally not eliminable** by proportional action alone — one must add **integral (I) action**.

> **Mark Distribution (1+1+1):** Definition = 1 mark; formula = 1 mark; justification why $K_c$ cannot fix it = 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Differentiate between the integral time $T_i$ and the derivative time $T_d$ in a PID controller. State the effect of making $T_i$ very small on the closed-loop response.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Integral Time $T_i$** | **Derivative Time $T_d$** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Definition | Time required for the integral action to repeat the contribution of the proportional action. | Time by which the derivative action advances the controller output compared to a pure P-action. |
| Function | Eliminates steady-state error (offset). | Anticipates future error; improves stability and damping. |
| Units | Seconds or minutes | Seconds or minutes |
| Noise Sensitivity | Low | **High** (amplifies measurement noise) |
| Typical Range (process control) | 0.1 – 100 min | 0.01 – 10 min |

**Effect of making $T_i$ very small:** A small $T_i$ makes the integral action **aggressive** — the controller output integrates the error rapidly. This leads to **integral windup**, large overshoot, and sustained oscillations. The closed-loop response becomes **unstable** if $T_i$ is reduced below a critical value.

> **Mark Distribution (1+1+1):** Distinction table = 1 mark; explanation of $T_i$ effect = 1 mark; consequence of small $T_i$ = 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Q3. Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** For a level process station, draw the **complete ISA 5.1 instrumentation loop diagram** for a level control loop that includes a level transmitter (LT), a level indicating controller (LIC), a control valve (LCV), and a data recorder (LR). Clearly label all tag numbers, signal types (pneumatic 3–15 psi and electronic 4–20 mA), and the control room / field area boundary.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A first-order level process has the open-loop transfer function $G_p(s) = \dfrac{0.9}{60s+1}$. Using a proportional controller, find the value of $K_c$ that gives a steady-state error of 5% for a unit step change in setpoint. State whether a PI controller can eliminate this error, and justify with the relevant equation.

---

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — ISA Diagram Construction (7 marks)**

The student is expected to draw a P&ID-style diagram using ISA 5.1 symbology with the following elements:

1. **Field side (left of the dashed boundary line):**
   - Process tank drawn as a vertical cylinder
   - Differential Pressure (DPT) level transmitter labelled **LT-101** inside a circle
   - Pneumatic control valve labelled **LCV-101** on the outlet line
   - Solid line with cross-hatch dashes for **pneumatic (3–15 psi)** signal from controller to valve
   - Solid line (with optional dotted/dashed marking) for **electronic (4–20 mA)** signal from transmitter to controller

2. **Control room side (right of the dashed boundary line):**
   - **LIC-101** — Level Indicating Controller (circle with horizontal bar)
   - **LR-101** — Level Recorder (circle with bar)
   - These are interconnected and both connected to the field

3. **Signal Lines and Arrows:** Arrowheads showing the direction of signal flow.

**Mark Breakdown:**
- Correct ISA circle symbols for LT, LIC, LCV, LR (each worth 1 mark, total 4)
- Correct signal type labelling (4–20 mA electronic, 3–15 psi pneumatic) — 1 mark
- Correct control room / field boundary (dashed line) — 1 mark
- Direction-of-flow arrows and neatness — 1 mark

**Part (b) — Steady-State Error Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Recall the steady-state error formula for P-control with unit step setpoint:**

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_c K}$$

Here $K = 0.9$ (process gain from $G_p(s) = \dfrac{0.9}{60s+1}$).

**Step 2 — Set $e_{ss} = 0.05$ (5%) and solve for $K_c$:**

$$0.05 = \frac{1}{1 + 0.9\,K_c}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply:**

$$1 + 0.9\,K_c = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20$$

$$0.9\,K_c = 19$$

$$\boxed{K_c = \frac{19}{0.9} = 21.11}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Proportional Band (optional, for full marks):**

$$PB = \frac{100}{K_c} = \frac{100}{21.11} = 4.74\%$$

**Step 5 — PI Controller Effect on Offset:**

The integral action introduces a pole at the origin in the open-loop transfer function. The closed-loop error becomes:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1}{s} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)} = 0$$

because $G_c(s) = K_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s}\right) \to \infty$ as $s \to 0$.

**Conclusion:** A PI controller can **eliminate the steady-state error** (drive offset to zero) because the integral term keeps adjusting the output as long as any error exists.

**Mark Breakdown (part b):**
- Formula statement — 2 marks
- Substitution and solving for $K_c$ — 3 marks
- PI justification + conclusion — 2 marks

---

**Q4. Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labelled block diagram, derive the closed-loop transfer function $Y(s)/R(s)$ for a unity-feedback system having process $G_p(s)$ and PID controller $G_c(s) = K_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$. Show the final expression in **standard PID form**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain, with sketches of typical step responses, the qualitative difference in:
   (i) the open-loop response,
   (ii) the response under P-control,
   (iii) the response under PI-control,
   (iv) the response under PID-control.

Highlight specifically how **offset**, **overshoot**, and **settling time** change from one mode to the next.

---

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — Closed-Loop Transfer Function Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Define the controller transfer function:**

$$G_c(s) = K_c\left(1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$$

**Step 2 — Combine over a common denominator $T_i s$:**

$$G_c(s) = K_c \cdot \frac{T_i s + 1 + T_i T_d s^2}{T_i s}$$

$$G_c(s) = \frac{K_c(1 + T_i s + T_i T_d s^2)}{T_i s}$$

**Step 3 — Write the open-loop transfer function (forward path):**

$$G(s) = G_c(s)\,G_p(s) = \frac{K_c(1 + T_i s + T_i T_d s^2)}{T_i s}\cdot G_p(s)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the unity-feedback closed-loop formula:**

$$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{G_c(s)\,G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s)\,G_p(s)}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the standard PID form (parallel form):**

$$\boxed{\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{K_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right) G_p(s)}{1 + K_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right) G_p(s)}}$$

**Mark Distribution (part a):**
- Correct controller expression — 1 mark
- Common-denominator manipulation — 2 marks
- Open-loop transfer function — 1 mark
- Closed-loop formula substitution — 2 marks
- Final boxed expression — 1 mark

**Part (b) — Qualitative Step-Response Comparison (7 marks)**

The student should describe the responses using the following descriptions and labels:

| **Mode** | **Step Response Description** | **Offset** | **Overshoot** | **Settling Time** |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| (i) Open-loop | Slow exponential rise to a value *below* the setpoint; never reaches SP if process has finite gain. | Very large | None | Very long |
| (ii) P-control | Faster rise, reaches steady-state *below* SP, leaves a residual offset. | **Present** (finite, non-zero) | Small | Shorter than open-loop |
| (iii) PI-control | Reaches SP exactly (no offset), may show moderate overshoot and a few decaying oscillations. | **Zero** | Moderate | Slightly longer than P |
| (iv) PID-control | Fast rise, minimal overshoot, very short settling time, zero offset. | **Zero** | **Lowest** | **Shortest** |

**Mark Distribution (part b):**
- Description of open-loop — 1 mark
- Description of P-control and identification of offset — 2 marks
- Description of PI-control and elimination of offset — 2 marks
- Description of PID-control and improvement in overshoot/settling time — 2 marks

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Confusing "reset" with "derivative"**: Students frequently write $T_d$ for integral time. Remember: $T_i$ = integral (reset), $T_d$ = derivative (rate or pre-act).
> 2. **Missing the bias term** $u_{bias}$: In exam answers, always include the bias/setpoint term in the controller output equation. Examiners deduct 1 mark for omission.
> 3. **Forgetting to draw the dashed boundary line** in the ISA diagram — it distinguishes **field instruments** from **control-room instruments**. Missing it costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Reversing SP and PV in error**: The standard convention is $e = SP - PV$ for a reverse-acting controller and $e = PV - SP$ for a direct-acting controller. Pick one and stay consistent.
> 5. **Units of $T_i$ and $T_d$**: KTU expects **minutes** or **seconds** explicitly written. A bare number without units is incomplete and loses 0.5 mark.
> 6. **Stating "PI = perfect"**: PI eliminates *offset* but can still have overshoot and oscillations. Examiners deduct marks if you claim PI is universally superior.
> 7. **Derivative on noise**: When explaining PID, mention that $T_d$ amplifies high-frequency measurement noise and is often filtered in practice (a "filtered derivative" is used in industrial PID).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module Experiment 2 (PCICL607)**

- ☐ **ON-OFF control** has only two output states (0% or 100%) and a **dead band** $\Delta$ to prevent chattering. It produces **limit-cycle oscillation**.
- ☐ **Proportional Band** $PB = 100 / K_c$. Smaller PB = more aggressive controller.
- ☐ **Pure P-control** always leaves an **offset** (steady-state error). The error for a first-order process with unit step setpoint is $e_{ss} = 1 / (1 + K_c K)$.
- ☐ **Integral action** $K_c \cdot (1/T_i) \cdot \int e\,dt$ **eliminates offset** by integrating error over time.
- ☐ **Derivative action** $K_c \cdot T_d \cdot (de/dt)$ **anticipates** future error based on its rate of change.
- ☐ **PID transfer function** in Laplace domain: $G_c(s) = K_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$.
- ☐ **Ziegler-Nichols (Open-Loop)** uses $K$, $L$, $\tau$ from step test: $K_c = 1.2\tau / (KL)$, $T_i = 2L$, $T_d = 0.5L$ for PID.
- ☐ **ISA 5.1 symbology**: tag = `VariableLetter - FunctionLetter - LoopNumber` (e.g., `TIC-101` = Temperature Indicating Controller, loop 101).
- ☐ **Field instruments** appear **outside** the control-room boundary; control-room instruments (controller, recorder, indicator) appear **inside** the dashed line.
- ☐ **Signal types** in ISA: dashed line with double slash = pneumatic (3–15 psi); solid line with cross-hatch = electronic (4–20 mA); dotted line = capillary / software / internal.
- ☐ **Performance metrics** to record during the experiment: rise time, peak overshoot $M_p$, settling time $t_s$ (at ±2% or ±5%), steady-state error $e_{ss}$, and integral criteria (IAE, ISE, ITAE).
- ☐ **Derivative noise sensitivity**: real industrial PIDs use a **filtered derivative** (e.g., $T_d s / (\alpha T_d s + 1)$ with $\alpha = 0.05$ to $0.1$).
- ☐ **Anti-windup**: when the controller output saturates, integral action must be **clamped** to prevent windup — back-calculation and clamping are the two common strategies.
- ☐ **Level station components**: reservoir, pump, process tank, DPT/capacitance level transmitter, control valve, digital PID controller, SCADA/HMI.
- ☐ **Temperature station components**: process tank, electric heater, RTD/TC, temperature transmitter, SSR/SCR power controller, cooling coil, digital PID controller.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tuning of P, PI, and PID control mode for any one process station using Ziegler Nichols open-loop method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL607 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Tuning of P, PI, and PID control mode for any one process station using Ziegler Nichols open-loop method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Control Tuning (KTU Definition):**
> *Process control tuning* is the systematic adjustment of controller parameters (Proportional band, Integral time, and Derivative time) to achieve an optimal closed-loop dynamic response for a given process, minimizing error indices such as **Integral of Squared Error (ISE)**, **Integral of Absolute Error (IAE)**, or **Integral of Time-weighted Absolute Error (ITAE)**.

The **Ziegler-Nichols Open-Loop Tuning Method** (also called the *Process Reaction Curve* method or *Step Response* method) is an empirical, plant-tested procedure developed by John G. Ziegler and Nathaniel B. Nichols in **1942**. It is executed on an *open-loop* process (controller in **MANUAL** mode) and requires the experimenter to apply a small step change in the manipulated variable (controller output) and record the resulting process variable response.

From this open-loop step response, three characteristic parameters are extracted:

| Symbol | Parameter | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $K_p$ | Process Steady-State Gain | Final change in PV per unit change in MV |
| $L$ | Apparent Dead Time (Transport Delay) | Time delay before the PV begins to respond |
| $T$ | Process Time Constant | Time for the PV to reach **63.2%** of its final value |

These three parameters are then substituted into the **Ziegler-Nichols empirical correlations** to directly compute the recommended controller settings.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cruise Control" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine you are driving a manual car (no automatic cruise control) on a flat highway, and your goal is to maintain a steady speed of 60 km/h.**

- **The Process** = Your car's actual speed
- **The Setpoint (SP)** = 60 km/h
- **The Manipulated Variable (MV)** = Pressure on the accelerator pedal
- **The Controller** = You (the driver)

If you **stomp the accelerator fully to the floor** (a step change in MV), the speed does not rise instantly. There is a small **dead time** $L$ (the engine spools up, gear engages), and then the speed rises **exponentially** with a characteristic **time constant** $T$ until it settles at a new value (the **process gain** $K_p$).

This S-shaped speed-versus-time curve is exactly the **Process Reaction Curve** you record during the Ziegler-Nichols open-loop experiment! The dead time $L$ tells you "how sluggish the car is to react," and the time constant $T$ tells you "how quickly it eventually reaches its new cruise speed."

> [!TIP]
> The Ziegler-Nichols formulas are essentially a "translation table" that converts these *driver-feel* numbers ($L$, $T$, $K_p$) into optimal *pedal-pressure corrections* ($K_c$, $T_i$, $T_d$).

## 1.3 What is a "Process Station"?

In the KTU laboratory, a **Process Station** is a self-contained pilot-plant rig that emulates an industrial process. Common examples found in KTU-affiliated colleges include:

1. **Temperature Process Station** (heater + water bath + RTD / thermocouple)
2. **Level Process Station** (water tank + pump + capacitive/ultrasonic level sensor)
3. **Flow Process Station** (pump + orifice / rotameter + flow transmitter)
4. **Pressure Process Station** (compressor + receiver + pressure transmitter)

All of these stations have a **final control element** (pneumatic valve / electrical heater / variable-speed pump) and a **sensor**, and they exhibit the same first-order-plus-dead-time (FOPDT) dynamics that the ZN method is designed to characterize.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Process Model — FOPDT

The ZN open-loop method assumes the process can be represented by a **First-Order Plus Dead Time (FOPDT)** transfer function:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p \cdot e^{-Ls}}{Ts + 1}$$

Where:
- $K_p$ → steady-state process gain (dimensionless, or in process units)
- $L$ → apparent dead time (seconds or minutes)
- $T$ → process time constant (seconds or minutes)
- $e^{-Ls}$ → Padé approximation of pure transport delay

> [!NOTE]
> **Why FOPDT?** Most industrial processes (thermal, level, flow) naturally exhibit a single dominant time constant plus a transport delay. Higher-order dynamics are usually negligible or can be approximated as additional dead time.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Open-Loop Tuning Logic

The procedure breaks into four rigorous phases:

### **Phase 1 — Steady-State Establishment**
1. Place the controller in **MANUAL (M)** mode.
2. Allow the process to reach a stable steady state at some operating point $(PV_0, MV_0)$.
3. Record the initial controller output $MV_0$ and the initial process variable $PV_0$.

### **Phase 2 — Step Disturbance Application**
4. Apply a small, instantaneous **step change** $\Delta MV$ to the controller output (typically **5% to 10%** of the full output range).
5. **Do NOT** change the setpoint — the process is open-loop.
6. Simultaneously start a stopwatch / data logger and begin recording the PV at regular intervals (every 1 to 5 seconds, depending on process speed).

### **Phase 3 — Process Reaction Curve Generation**
7. Continue recording until the PV reaches a **new steady state** $PV_{ss}$.
8. Plot the curve of **PV vs. time** — this yields the characteristic **S-shaped response**.

### **Phase 4 — Parameter Extraction via Tangent Method**
9. Identify the **inflection point** of the S-curve (point of steepest slope).
10. Draw a **tangent line** through this inflection point.
11. The tangent intersects:
    - The horizontal line $PV = PV_0$ at a time $t = L$ (the dead time)
    - The horizontal line $PV = PV_{ss}$ at a time $t = L + T$ (the apparent total time)
12. The **time constant** $T$ is calculated as $T = (L+T) - L$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are high-yield for the KTU 2024 Scheme examination. Memorize these first.**

### 2.3.1 Parameter Extraction Formulas

| Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Process Gain | $K_p = \dfrac{\Delta PV}{\Delta MV} = \dfrac{PV_{ss} - PV_0}{\Delta MV}$ | (PV units) / (MV units) |
| Apparent Dead Time | $L$ (read directly from tangent intercept with $PV_0$) | seconds / minutes |
| Time Constant | $T = (L + T)_{intercept} - L$ | seconds / minutes |
| Reaction Rate | $R = \dfrac{K_p}{T}$ (used in alternate ZN form) | per second |

### 2.3.2 Ziegler-Nichols Open-Loop Tuning Rules

> [!TIP]
> **These are the GOLDEN formulas for KTU. Use them verbatim in the answer script.**

| Controller Type | Proportional Gain $K_c$ | Integral Time $T_i$ | Derivative Time $T_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P** (Proportional) | $K_c = \dfrac{T}{K_p \cdot L}$ | $T_i = \infty$ (No integral action) | $T_d = 0$ (No derivative action) |
| **PI** (Proportional + Integral) | $K_c = \dfrac{0.9 \cdot T}{K_p \cdot L}$ | $T_i = 3.33 \cdot L$ | $T_d = 0$ |
| **PID** (Proportional + Integral + Derivative) | $K_c = \dfrac{1.2 \cdot T}{K_p \cdot L}$ | $T_i = 2 \cdot L$ | $T_d = 0.5 \cdot L$ |

### 2.3.3 Alternate Form Using Reaction Rate $R$

Some textbooks express the ZN rules using the **reaction rate** $R = K_p / T$:

$$K_c \cdot K_p = 0.5 \cdot T/L \quad \text{(P only)}$$

$$K_c \cdot K_p = 0.54 \cdot T/L \quad \text{(PI)}$$

$$K_c \cdot K_p = 0.6 \cdot T/L \quad \text{(PID)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU students should be fluent in BOTH forms.** The integral-form table is more common in lab viva; the reaction-rate form appears in some KTU reference textbooks.

### 2.3.4 Proportional Band Conversion

European and Indian industrial controllers use **Proportional Band (PB)** rather than $K_c$:

$$PB\,(\%) = \frac{100}{K_c}$$

So a $K_c = 2$ corresponds to a **PB of 50%**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ZN open-loop method is used in:

- **Petrochemical plants** for tuning furnace temperature controllers where the process is too dangerous to test in closed loop.
- **HVAC systems** for tuning chilled water valve loops where overshoot must be limited.
- **Paper & pulp industries** for basis-weight control where the dead time is very long.
- **Batch chemical reactors** in pharmaceutical manufacturing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Modern Limitation:** ZN tuning gives a quarter-amplitude decay response which has ~**25% overshoot**. For processes where zero overshoot is required (e.g., pharmaceutical reactors), engineers use refined methods like **Cohen-Coon**, **Tyreus-Luyben**, or **IMC (Internal Model Control)** tuning. However, KTU syllabus **specifically mandates ZN**, so master it first.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Worked Numerical Example — The "Golden" KTU Question

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the type of numerical problem KTU examiners love. Practice this until you can solve it in 15 minutes.**

### **Problem Statement**

A level process station is operated in **MANUAL** mode. The process variable (water level) is initially steady at **$PV_0 = 35\%$**. A step change of $\Delta MV = +10\%$ is applied to the controller output (pump speed). The level rises and eventually stabilizes at **$PV_{ss} = 65\%$**. The tangent drawn at the inflection point of the response curve intersects the $PV_0$ line at $t = 2$ min and the $PV_{ss}$ line at $t = 8$ min.

**Tasks:**
1. Calculate the open-loop process parameters $K_p$, $L$, and $T$.
2. Determine the controller settings using ZN open-loop rules for **P**, **PI**, and **PID** modes.
3. Express the P-controller setting as a Proportional Band.
4. Comment on the expected closed-loop response.

### **Step 1 — Compute the Process Steady-State Gain $K_p$**

The gain is the ratio of the change in PV to the change in MV:

$$K_p = \frac{PV_{ss} - PV_0}{\Delta MV} = \frac{65\% - 35\%}{10\%}$$

$$K_p = \frac{30}{10} = 3.0 \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** A 1% increase in pump speed produces a **3% increase** in tank level. This is a *high-gain* process.

### **Step 2 — Identify the Dead Time $L$**

By definition, the dead time is the time intercept of the tangent on the initial steady-state line $PV_0$:

$$L = 2 \text{ minutes}$$

> **Interpretation:** The pump speed change takes 2 minutes before the level sensor detects any movement. This is the **transport delay** caused by piping, sensor lag, and valve dynamics.

### **Step 3 — Compute the Time Constant $T$**

The total apparent time $(L + T)$ is the time intercept on the final steady-state line $PV_{ss}$:

$$L + T = 8 \text{ minutes}$$

Therefore:

$$T = (L + T) - L = 8 - 2 = 6 \text{ minutes}$$

> **Interpretation:** Once the level starts moving, it takes 6 minutes to reach **63.2%** of its final change. The total open-loop settling time is roughly $4T = 24$ minutes.

### **Step 4 — Apply ZN Formulas**

#### **Case A — P Controller**

Using the ZN P-only rule:

$$K_c = \frac{T}{K_p \cdot L} = \frac{6}{3.0 \times 2} = \frac{6}{6} = 1.0$$

$$T_i = \infty, \quad T_d = 0$$

Proportional Band conversion:

$$PB = \frac{100}{K_c} = \frac{100}{1.0} = 100\%$$

#### **Case B — PI Controller**

Using the ZN PI rule:

$$K_c = \frac{0.9 \cdot T}{K_p \cdot L} = \frac{0.9 \times 6}{3.0 \times 2} = \frac{5.4}{6} = 0.9$$

$$T_i = 3.33 \cdot L = 3.33 \times 2 = 6.66 \text{ minutes}$$

$$T_d = 0$$

#### **Case C — PID Controller**

Using the ZN PID rule:

$$K_c = \frac{1.2 \cdot T}{K_p \cdot L} = \frac{1.2 \times 6}{3.0 \times 2} = \frac{7.2}{6} = 1.2$$

$$T_i = 2 \cdot L = 2 \times 2 = 4 \text{ minutes}$$

$$T_d = 0.5 \cdot L = 0.5 \times 2 = 1.0 \text{ minute}$$

### **Step 5 — Summary Table of Results**

| Controller | $K_c$ | $T_i$ (min) | $T_d$ (min) | PB (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P | 1.0 | $\infty$ | 0 | 100 |
| PI | 0.9 | 6.66 | 0 | 111.1 |
| PID | 1.2 | 4.0 | 1.0 | 83.3 |

### **Step 6 — Expected Closed-Loop Behavior**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comment required for full marks in KTU 2024 Scheme:**

- **P mode:** Offset (steady-state error) will exist; the controller will produce a quick but non-zero-error response.
- **PI mode:** The integral action **eliminates the offset** completely. Slightly slower than P, but the final PV equals the SP exactly.
- **PID mode:** The derivative action **anticipates future error**, giving the fastest response with ~**25% overshoot** (quarter-amplitude decay criterion). This is the ZN design target.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Edge Cases & Sanity Checks

> [!TIP]
> **KTU examiners often test whether students understand *why* a number is wrong. Use this matrix to sanity-check your answers.**

| Scenario | What Should Happen? | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| $K_p$ comes out **negative** | MV and PV moved in *opposite* directions | The process is **reverse-acting** — you may need to flip controller action |
| $L > T$ | Process is *delay-dominated* | ZN tuning will be very aggressive; consider a **detuned** or **conservative** approach |
| $L \approx 0$ | Process is essentially a first-order lag with no delay | ZN formulas become singular; fall back to **analytical** tuning using pole placement |
| $T_i = \infty$ requested in lab | Practically, set $T_i = 9999$ min on the digital controller | This is the convention to "disable" integral action |
| The S-curve has **no inflection point** | The process is *integrating* (e.g., a true level tank with no outflow) | ZN open-loop **fails** — switch to the **ZN closed-loop** (ultimate gain) method |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Open-Loop Tuning Procedure — Process Flowchart

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Diagram 1 — Complete procedural flowchart for the ZN open-loop method as performed in a KTU lab.**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Process in steady state] --> B[Switch controller to MANUAL mode]
    B --> C[Record initial PV0 and MV0]
    C --> D[Apply step change Delta MV 5 to 10 percent]
    D --> E[Start data logger and timer]
    E --> F[Record PV vs time continuously]
    F --> G{PV reached new steady state?}
    G -- No --> F
    G -- Yes --> H[Plot Process Reaction Curve S shape]
    H --> I[Identify inflection point of S curve]
    I --> J[Draw tangent line at inflection point]
    J --> K[Tangent meets PV0 line at time L]
    J --> L[Tangent meets PVss line at time L plus T]
    K --> M[L is dead time]
    L --> N[Compute T from L plus T minus L]
    N --> O[Compute Kp equals Delta PV over Delta MV]
    O --> P{Choose controller type}
    P -- P only --> Q[Kc equals T over Kp times L]
    P -- PI --> R[Kc equals 0.9T over Kp L, Ti equals 3.33L]
    P -- PID --> S[Kc equals 1.2T over Kp L, Ti equals 2L, Td equals 0.5L]
    Q --> T[Enter values into controller and switch to AUTO]
    R --> T
    S --> T
    T --> U[Test closed loop response with small SP change]
    U --> V[Fine tune if necessary]
    V --> W[End]
```

## 4.2 Closed-Loop Block Diagram — Controller in Service

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Diagram 2 — Functional architecture of the closed-loop system after ZN tuning is applied.**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint SP] --> SUM[Sum junction error]
    SUM --> CTRL[P, PI, or PID controller]
    CTRL --> MV[Manipulated Variable MV]
    MV --> PROC[Process Station FOPDT]
    PROC --> PV[Process Variable PV]
    PV --> TRANSMIT[Transmitter]
    TRANSMIT --> SUM
    PROC -. Disturbance .-> PROC
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The setpoint (e.g., desired level) is compared to the measured process variable at the summing junction. The error $e(t) = SP - PV$ drives the controller, which adjusts the MV (pump speed / valve position). The process responds, the new PV is sensed, and the loop continues.

## 4.3 Process Reaction Curve — The S-Shape Anatomy

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Diagram 3 — Annotated S-curve showing the geometric construction of $L$ and $T$.**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[Time zero: Step MV applied] --> T1[Time L: PV begins to move]
    T1 --> T2[Inflection point: maximum slope]
    T2 --> T3[Time L plus T: PV reaches 63.2 percent of final change]
    T3 --> T4[Time 4T: PV within 2 percent of final steady state]
    T0 -. PV0 baseline .-> T0
    T3 -. PVss asymptote .-> T3
```

**Reading the S-curve anatomy:**

1. From $t = 0$ to $t = L$: The PV does **not move** — this is the dead time region.
2. From $t = L$ onwards: The PV rises with increasing slope (concave up).
3. At the **inflection point**: The slope is maximum; this is where the tangent line is drawn.
4. From the inflection onwards: The slope decreases (concave down) as the PV approaches its new steady state.
5. At $t = L + T$: The PV has covered **63.2%** of the total change $(PV_{ss} - PV_0)$.

## 4.4 Comparison of P, PI, PID Closed-Loop Responses

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Diagram 4 — Conceptual comparison of step responses after ZN tuning.**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P_MODE[P controller] --> P_RES[Quick rise, steady offset exists]
    PI_MODE[PI controller] --> PI_RES[No offset, slightly slower, small overshoot]
    PID_MODE[PID controller] --> PID_RES[Fastest response, 25 percent overshoot, zero offset]
```

| Controller | Rise Time | Overshoot | Steady-State Error |
|---|---|---|---|
| P | Fastest | None (monotonic) | **Yes (offset exists)** |
| PI | Slower | Small | **None (zero offset)** |
| PID | Fastest | ~25% (by ZN design) | **None (zero offset)** |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model Paper]**
**Define the term "Process Reaction Curve" as used in the Ziegler-Nichols open-loop tuning method. List the three parameters extracted from this curve.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The **Process Reaction Curve** is the open-loop response of the process variable (PV) to a small, step change applied to the manipulated variable (MV) while the controller is in MANUAL mode. It is a plot of PV vs. time and typically exhibits a characteristic **S-shape** for first-order-plus-dead-time processes.
>
> The three parameters extracted from this curve are:
> 1. **$K_p$** — Process steady-state gain
> 2. **$L$** — Apparent dead time (transport delay)
> 3. **$T$** — Process time constant

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**State the Ziegler-Nichols tuning formulas for a PI controller using the open-loop method. Explain the significance of the factor 0.9 in the formula.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Ziegler-Nichols PI controller settings (open-loop method):**
>
> $$K_c = \frac{0.9 \cdot T}{K_p \cdot L}, \quad T_i = 3.33 \cdot L, \quad T_d = 0$$
>
> **Significance of 0.9:** The factor **0.9** is an empirical multiplier introduced by Ziegler and Nichols based on extensive plant experiments. It **reduces the proportional gain** below the P-only setting (which uses a factor of 1.0) because the addition of integral action increases the loop's effective aggressiveness. If $K_c$ were kept at the P-only value, the integral term would cause the system to oscillate or become unstable. The 0.9 factor ensures the quarter-amplitude decay response is preserved even with integral action present.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read this carefully!**
> - Students **LOOSE 2 marks** by writing ZN formulas with incorrect numeric factors (e.g., writing 0.8 instead of 0.9 for PI).
> - Students **LOOSE 3 marks** by confusing $L$ and $T$ (interchanging dead time and time constant in formulas).
> - Students **LOOSE 2 marks** by forgetting to convert $K_c$ to Proportional Band (PB) when the question specifically asks for it.
> - Always mention that the controller is in **MANUAL mode** during the open-loop test. Examiners actively check for this phrase.

---

### **Question A — Q1(a) [7 marks] + Q1(b) [7 marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module Experiment 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Describe, with the aid of a labeled block diagram, the complete experimental procedure for tuning a **PI controller** for a temperature process station using the **Ziegler-Nichols open-loop method**. Include all safety precautions.

**(b)** During an open-loop test on a flow process station, the initial PV was 40% and the initial MV was 50%. A step change of $\Delta MV = +5\%$ was applied. The process variable eventually settled at 55%. The tangent at the inflection point meets the initial-PV line at $t = 1.5$ min and the final-PV line at $t = 4.5$ min. Calculate the ZN open-loop PI controller settings and express the gain as a Proportional Band.

#### **Model Solution — Q1(a)**

**Experimental Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Apparatus Setup (1 mark):** A temperature process station consisting of a heater, water/heat-transfer-fluid bath, RTD/thermocouple sensor, temperature transmitter (4–20 mA or 0–10 V), PID controller, and a final control element (thyristor / heater contactor).

2. **Steady-State Establishment (1 mark):** Switch the PID controller to **MANUAL (M)** mode. Allow the bath temperature to stabilize at an ambient or mid-range operating point (e.g., 40°C). Record the steady PV reading and the corresponding controller output (MV).

3. **Step Application (1 mark):** Apply a step change of $\Delta MV = +5\%$ to $+10\%$ to the controller output. Start a stopwatch or data logger simultaneously. **Do not change the setpoint** during the entire test.

4. **Data Logging (1 mark):** Record the PV value every 5 to 10 seconds until it reaches a new steady-state value (typically takes $3T$ to $4T$).

5. **Process Reaction Curve Plotting (1 mark):** Plot PV (Y-axis) vs. time (X-axis) on a graph sheet. The curve should be S-shaped.

6. **Parameter Extraction (1 mark):** Identify the inflection point, draw the tangent, and read off $L$ and $T$.

7. **PI Settings Computation + Verification (1 mark):** Compute $K_c = 0.9T/(K_p L)$ and $T_i = 3.33L$. Enter these into the controller, switch to **AUTO**, apply a small SP change, and verify the closed-loop response. Fine-tune if necessary.

**Safety Precautions:**
- Limit $\Delta MV$ to a small percentage to avoid scalding or sensor damage.
- Monitor the heater current to prevent runaway temperature.
- Keep an emergency stop / manual override ready.

#### **Model Solution — Q1(b)**

**Step 1 — Compute the Process Gain $K_p$ (1 mark):**

$$K_p = \frac{\Delta PV}{\Delta MV} = \frac{55\% - 40\%}{5\%} = \frac{15}{5} = 3.0$$

**Step 2 — Identify Dead Time $L$ (1 mark):**

$$L = 1.5 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Time Constant $T$ (1 mark):**

$$L + T = 4.5 \text{ min} \implies T = 4.5 - 1.5 = 3.0 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 4 — Apply ZN PI Formulas (3 marks):**

$$K_c = \frac{0.9 \cdot T}{K_p \cdot L} = \frac{0.9 \times 3.0}{3.0 \times 1.5} = \frac{2.7}{4.5} = 0.6$$

$$T_i = 3.33 \cdot L = 3.33 \times 1.5 = 4.995 \approx 5.0 \text{ minutes}$$

$$T_d = 0$$

**Step 5 — Express as Proportional Band (1 mark):**

$$PB = \frac{100}{K_c} = \frac{100}{0.6} = 166.67\%$$

**Final Answer:** $K_c = 0.6$, $T_i = 5.0$ min, $T_d = 0$, $PB = 166.67\%$.

---

### **Question B — Q2(a) [7 marks] + Q2(b) [7 marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module Experiment 2, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain, with suitable diagrams, the difference between the **open-loop** and **closed-loop** Ziegler-Nichols tuning methods. Under what conditions is the open-loop method preferred?

**(b)** A pressure process station is tested in open loop. Initial PV = 30%, initial MV = 40%. Step change applied: $\Delta MV = +8\%$. Final PV = 54%. Tangent at inflection meets $PV_0$ line at $t = 0.8$ min and the $PV_{ss}$ line at $t = 4.8$ min. Determine the ZN open-loop **PID** settings.

#### **Model Solution — Q2(a)**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | ZN Open-Loop Method | ZN Closed-Loop (Ultimate Gain) Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Controller mode during test** | MANUAL | AUTO (P-only mode) |
| **Test signal** | Step change in MV | Step change in Setpoint |
| **Process Reaction Curve** | Yes, S-shaped | No — uses sustained oscillation |
| **Key extracted parameters** | $K_p$, $L$, $T$ | $K_u$ (ultimate gain), $P_u$ (ultimate period) |
| **Time required** | One open-loop test (long) | Multiple trials to find $K_u$ (iterative) |
| **Process must be stable open-loop?** | Yes | Yes |

**Conditions where Open-Loop is preferred (3 marks):**
1. The process has a **very long time constant** (e.g., large thermal masses) — closed-loop oscillation would take too long.
2. The process is **too sensitive or dangerous** to allow sustained oscillations (e.g., exothermic chemical reactors).
3. The process has a **strict no-overshoot** requirement during tuning.
4. The plant cannot tolerate the **continuous cycling** required by the closed-loop method.
5. The operator wishes to obtain both the FOPDT model parameters $K_p$, $L$, $T$ for **multiple-controller-type selection**, not just one.

#### **Model Solution — Q2(b)**

**Step 1 — Compute the Process Gain $K_p$ (1 mark):**

$$K_p = \frac{\Delta PV}{\Delta MV} = \frac{54\% - 30\%}{8\%} = \frac{24}{8} = 3.0$$

**Step 2 — Identify Dead Time $L$ (1 mark):**

$$L = 0.8 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Time Constant $T$ (1 mark):**

$$L + T = 4.8 \text{ min} \implies T = 4.8 - 0.8 = 4.0 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 4 — Apply ZN PID Formulas (3 marks):**

$$K_c = \frac{1.2 \cdot T}{K_p \cdot L} = \frac{1.2 \times 4.0}{3.0 \times 0.8} = \frac{4.8}{2.4} = 2.0$$

$$T_i = 2 \cdot L = 2 \times 0.8 = 1.6 \text{ minutes}$$

$$T_d = 0.5 \cdot L = 0.5 \times 0.8 = 0.4 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (1 mark):**

The PID settings will yield a closed-loop response with **zero steady-state error** (due to integral action), **anticipatory correction** (due to derivative action on rate of error change), and the classic ZN **quarter-amplitude decay** with **~25% overshoot**.

**Final Answer:** $K_c = 2.0$, $T_i = 1.6$ min, $T_d = 0.4$ min.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize this entire block before entering the KTU exam hall.**

- **Open-Loop Test Prerequisites:** Controller in **MANUAL**, process at steady state, apply step **5% to 10%**, do **not** change setpoint.
- **Process Reaction Curve Shape:** Always **S-shaped** for a well-behaved FOPDT process. If it is not S-shaped, the process is not first-order.
- **Dead Time $L$:** Time intercept of the tangent on the **initial** PV line.
- **Time Constant $T$:** Total intercept on the **final** PV line **minus** $L$. Equivalently, time for PV to reach **63.2%** of total change after $L$.
- **Process Gain $K_p$:** Always $\Delta PV / \Delta MV$ — watch the sign for reverse-acting processes.
- **Golden ZN Formulas (Open-Loop):**
   - **P:** $K_c = T/(K_p L)$, $T_i = \infty$, $T_d = 0$
   - **PI:** $K_c = 0.9T/(K_p L)$, $T_i = 3.33L$, $T_d = 0$
   - **PID:** $K_c = 1.2T/(K_p L)$, $T_i = 2L$, $T_d = 0.5L$
- **Proportional Band Conversion:** $PB\,(\%) = 100 / K_c$.
- **P-only always has offset**; PI/PID eliminate offset.
- **ZN target response:** Quarter-amplitude decay, ~25% overshoot.
- **ZN limitation:** Not suitable for integrating processes (use closed-loop ZN instead).
- **Numerically most common KTU error:** Interchanging $L$ and $T$ in the formulas — *always double-check*.
- **Lab Viva Favorite Question:** *"Why is the ZN open-loop method called an empirical method?"* — Answer: Because the constants 0.9, 1.2, 3.33, 2, 0.5 were obtained from experimental observation of many industrial loops, not derived mathematically.
- **Second Viva Favorite:** *"What happens if you tune in AUTO mode by mistake during the open-loop test?"* — Answer: The setpoint change will create an error signal that the controller will try to correct, contaminating the open-loop response. Always confirm MANUAL mode before applying the step.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tuning of P, PI, and PID control mode for any one process station using Ziegler Nichols closed-loop method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL607 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Tuning of P, PI, and PID control mode for any one process station using Ziegler Nichols closed-loop method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **Ziegler–Nichols (Z–N) Closed-Loop Tuning Method**, also called the **Ultimate Gain Method** or **Continuous Cycling Method**, is a heuristic tuning procedure developed by John G. Ziegler and Nathaniel B. Nichols (1942) for determining the proportional, integral, and derivative controller gains of a feedback control loop. The method forces the closed loop into **sustained oscillations** by increasing the proportional gain until the system output exhibits **constant-amplitude sinusoidal oscillations** (neither growing nor decaying). The corresponding gain is the **Ultimate Gain ($K_u$)** and the corresponding period of oscillation is the **Ultimate Period ($P_u$)**. These two empirical parameters are then mapped onto the controller settings using a published Z–N table.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** This experiment belongs to *Module 2: Controller Tuning*. The expected student outcome is the ability to **experimentally determine $K_u$ and $P_u$** for a chosen process station (temperature, level, flow, or pressure) and to **derive P, PI, and PID settings** that give a quarter-amplitude-decay (QAD) closed-loop response.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Rhythm of a Swing"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the process station as a child on a swing.**
> 
> - The child will not oscillate if you push too **gently** (low controller gain → slow, sluggish response).
> - The child will fly out of the swing if you push too **violently** (gain too high → unstable).
> - The **correct gain** is the one where each push exactly matches the swing's natural rhythm.
> - The Z–N method *deliberately pushes just hard enough* to make the swing go back and forth between the same two extreme heights, **timing each push with the swing's own period**.
> - Once you know *how hard* ($K_u$) and *how often* ($P_u$) you were pushing, you then **reduce your push by a known fraction** so the swing eventually settles down (this fraction is the Z–N safety factor in the lookup table).

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Quarter-Amplitude Decay (QAD) Ratio:** $1/4$ — the second overshoot of the closed-loop step response is one-fourth the size of the first overshoot. This is the **KTU-board expected closed-loop behaviour** for Z–N tuned loops.
- **Damping Factor ($\zeta$):** approximately **0.21** for Z–N settings (underdamped but stable).
- **Phase Margin:** approximately **$\phi_m \approx 22^\circ$** for Z–N settings.
- **Gain Margin:** approximately **$G_m \approx 6$ dB** (≈ 2 times) for Z–N settings.
- **Process Category:** First-Order Plus Dead Time (**FOPDT**) — the canonical model on which the Z–N heuristics are based.

> [!TIP]
> **Did you know?** Ziegler and Nichols tested their method on **paper mills, chemical reactors, and aircraft autopilots** during WWII. Their empirical tables have remained industry-standard for over 80 years because they work robustly for processes whose dynamics are dominated by time delay (e.g., temperature, level, composition control).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Concept — What Is a Process Station?

A **process station** in a KTU lab is an integrated hardware rig that emulates an industrial control loop. Typical examples include:

| Station Type | Manipulated Variable (MV) | Controlled Variable (PV) | Typical Time Constant | Suitable for Z–N? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature | Heater voltage (V) | Thermocouple/PT100 (°C) | 30 s – 5 min | Yes (recommended) |
| Level | Pump speed / valve opening | Capacitive / ultrasonic level (cm) | 5 s – 60 s | Yes |
| Flow | Control valve (%) | Orifice / rotameter (LPH) | < 5 s | Marginal (too fast) |
| Pressure | Air regulator (%) | Strain-gauge transducer (bar) | 1 s – 10 s | Yes |
| Speed (DC Motor) | Armature voltage (V) | Tachogenerator (RPM) | < 2 s | Marginal |

> [!NOTE]
> For this experiment write-up, we adopt the **Temperature Control Station** as the canonical example because it is a **slow, FOPDT-dominated process** — the very behaviour that Z–N tuning is designed to handle.

### 2.2 The Two Ziegler–Nichols Methods (Comparison)

| Feature | Open-Loop Method (Reaction Curve) | Closed-Loop Method (Ultimate Gain) |
|---|---|---|
| Also Called | Process Reaction Curve Method | Continuous Cycling Method |
| Data Required | Step response of open loop | Closed-loop response at high gain |
| Key Parameters | Process gain $K_p$, dead time $L$, time constant $T$ | Ultimate gain $K_u$, ultimate period $P_u$ |
| Controller State During Test | **Manual / Open loop** | **Automatic / Closed loop** (P-controller only) |
| Risk to Process | Low (small step) | Higher (sustained oscillation) |
| Best For | Stable, first-order processes | Higher-order / integrating processes |
| KTU Lab Use | Less common (needs accurate open-loop step) | **Most common (Module 2 experiment)** |

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Procedure (Closed-Loop / Continuous Cycling)

1. **Bring the process to steady state** at the desired operating point (e.g., 50% setpoint on a temperature rig).
2. **Set the controller in Automatic mode** with **P-only control** ($T_i = \infty$, $T_d = 0$).
3. **Start with a small proportional gain** $K_c$ to ensure stable, damped response.
4. **Gradually increase $K_c$ in small increments.** After each increment, give the system time to settle.
5. **Observe the response:** Look for the gain at which the process variable oscillates with **constant amplitude** (neither growing nor decaying). This is the **Ultimate Gain $K_u$**.
6. **Record the period of these sustained oscillations** — this is the **Ultimate Period $P_u$** (in seconds or minutes).
7. **Stop the test immediately** and reduce the gain to a safe value to avoid damage to the process.
8. **Apply the Z–N tuning formulas** (Section 2.4) to obtain $K_c$, $T_i$, $T_d$.
9. **Implement and test** the P, PI, and PID controllers in turn. Record the setpoint tracking response for each.

> [!WARNING]
> **Continuous cycling is inherently dangerous for real industrial processes.** It forces sustained oscillations, which can damage heaters, wear out pumps, or trip safety interlocks. In KTU labs, the rig is small and protected, so the procedure is safe. In industry, engineers use the **Tyreus–Luyben** or **Åström–Hägglund (relay feedback / autotune)** methods instead, which excite the process only briefly.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following are the **Ziegler–Nichols closed-loop tuning rules**, expressed using the standard ISA/IEC controller form:

$$
G_c(s) \;=\; K_c \left( 1 \;+\; \frac{1}{T_i \, s} \;+\; T_d \, s \right)
$$

where:
- $K_c$ = Proportional Gain (dimensionless, or in %/% as per ISA)
- $T_i$ = Integral Time (also called *Reset Time*), in **seconds or minutes**
- $T_d$ = Derivative Time, in **seconds or minutes**

| Controller Type | $K_c$ | $T_i$ | $T_d$ | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P** | $0.5 \, K_u$ | $\infty$ (no integral action) | $0$ | Offset cannot be eliminated |
| **PI** | $0.45 \, K_u$ | $P_u / 1.2$ | $0$ | Removes offset; slow settling |
| **PID (Classic Z–N)** | $0.6 \, K_u$ | $P_u / 2$ | $P_u / 8$ | Quarter-amplitude decay response |
| **P\* (some-version)** | $0.5 \, K_u$ | — | — | Equivalent to row 1 |
| **PI\* (some-version)** | $0.45 \, K_u$ | $0.83 \, P_u$ | — | $0.83 = 1/1.2$ |
| **PID\* (some-version)** | $0.6 \, K_u$ | $0.5 \, P_u$ | $0.125 \, P_u$ | $0.5 = 1/2$, $0.125 = 1/8$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** The Z–N table gives $T_i$ and $T_d$ in **time units** (seconds/minutes), **not** in repeats/minute. Some controllers (e.g., older Honeywell, ABB Bailey) accept integral action in **repeats/minute** ($R = 1/T_i$). Converting: $R_{\text{(reps/min)}} = 60 / T_{i \text{(s)}}$.

### 2.5 Quarter-Amplitude Decay (QAD) Criterion — The Engineering "Why"

The Z–N settings are tuned so that the **second peak** of a step response is exactly **one-quarter the height** of the first peak. The KTU board expects students to **identify QAD behaviour** in the response plot and quote the **decay ratio** of $1/4$ when describing Z–N performance.

$$
\text{Decay Ratio} \;=\; \frac{\text{Second Peak Height} - \text{Steady-State Value}}{\text{First Peak Height} - \text{Steady-State Value}} \;=\; 0.25
$$

> [!TIP]
> **How does Z–N achieve this empirically?** The two safety factors — $0.5$, $0.45$, $0.6$ for $K_c$ — were chosen by Ziegler & Nichols to scale back from the *unstable* $K_u$ point into the *underdamped* region such that the loop settles with a $1/4$ decay ratio. This is why the method is so robust across different process types.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Chemical plant start-ups:** Z–N provides a *first-cut* set of controller settings that operators refine during commissioning.
- **Pilot-plant labs:** Used extensively in undergraduate labs (including KTU) to demonstrate closed-loop dynamics.
- **HVAC control:** Building automation systems use Z–N-style rules to tune temperature and pressure loops.
- **Process industries:** Still the **default tuning method** in many Distributed Control System (DCS) tuning wizards (Honeywell TPS, Yokogawa CENTUM).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Temperature Control Station

**Rig:** A KTU-style temperature control station with:
- Heater: 0–230 V AC (Manipulated Variable, MV)
- Sensor: PT-100 RTD (Controlled Variable, PV)
- Setpoint: $r = 60 \, ^\circ C$ (working around ambient + 20 °C)
- Controller: Industrial PID controller (e.g., ELC-T/PRO/ABB Bailey)

**Objective:** Find Z–N settings for P, PI, and PID controllers; verify the QAD response.

#### Step A — Bring the Process to Steady State

Set the controller in **Manual** mode and apply an MV of, say, 50%. Wait until the PV settles to a steady value (e.g., $PV_{ss} = 42.3 \, ^\circ C$ for an ambient of 25 °C). Record this as the operating point.

> The deviation from setpoint is irrelevant at this stage — we only need a *linear* operating region.

#### Step B — Switch to Automatic (P-only) and Sweep the Gain

Set the controller as follows:
- Integral action: **OFF** ($T_i = \infty$ or repeats/min = 0)
- Derivative action: **OFF** ($T_d = 0$)
- Proportional band: very wide (i.e., $K_c$ very small) — e.g., 100% PB → $K_c = 1$.

For each candidate gain, introduce a **small step change in setpoint** (e.g., $+2 \, ^\circ C$) and observe the response.

| Trial | $K_c$ | Observation | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | Sluggish, large offset | Under-tuned |
| 2 | 3 | Faster, still offset | Under-tuned |
| 3 | 5 | Oscillatory, decays in 4–5 cycles | **Close** |
| 4 | 7 | Sustained oscillation (constant amplitude) | **CRITICAL** — this is $K_u$ |
| 5 | 8.5 | Growing oscillation (unstable) | Over-tuned (test must be stopped) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **transition between Trial 4 and Trial 5** is the *sustained-oscillation* boundary. The lowest gain that produces this is $K_u$. In a real lab, you would not exceed this — stop the test immediately and reduce the gain.

#### Step C — Record the Ultimate Period $P_u$

From Trial 4, the time between two consecutive peaks of the PV oscillation is measured. In our example:

$$
P_u \;=\; 3.5 \; \text{min} \;=\; 210 \; \text{s}
$$

#### Step D — Apply the Z–N Closed-Loop Formulas

**D1. P-only controller:**

$$
K_c^{(P)} \;=\; 0.5 \times K_u \;=\; 0.5 \times 7 \;=\; 3.5
$$

$$
T_i^{(P)} \;=\; \infty \quad \text{(no integral)} \qquad T_d^{(P)} \;=\; 0
$$

**D2. PI controller:**

$$
K_c^{(PI)} \;=\; 0.45 \times K_u \;=\; 0.45 \times 7 \;=\; 3.15
$$

$$
T_i^{(PI)} \;=\; \frac{P_u}{1.2} \;=\; \frac{3.5}{1.2} \;\approx\; 2.917 \; \text{min} \;\approx\; 175 \; \text{s}
$$

$$
T_d^{(PI)} \;=\; 0
$$

**D3. PID controller:**

$$
K_c^{(PID)} \;=\; 0.6 \times K_u \;=\; 0.6 \times 7 \;=\; 4.2
$$

$$
T_i^{(PID)} \;=\; \frac{P_u}{2} \;=\; \frac{3.5}{2} \;=\; 1.75 \; \text{min} \;=\; 105 \; \text{s}
$$

$$
T_d^{(PID)} \;=\; \frac{P_u}{8} \;=\; \frac{3.5}{8} \;=\; 0.4375 \; \text{min} \;\approx\; 26.25 \; \text{s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification (ISA standard form equivalence):** Some textbooks express PID in the *parallel* form with separate $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$. For the Z–N PID settings:
> 
> $$K_p = 4.2,\quad K_i = K_p / T_i = 4.2 / 105 = 0.04 \; \text{s}^{-1},\quad K_d = K_p \times T_d = 4.2 \times 26.25 = 110.25 \; \text{s}$$

#### Step E — Implement Each Controller and Record the Response

For each of the three controller settings, apply a **setpoint step** (e.g., 50 °C → 60 °C) and record the PV response. Typical KTU-board expected behaviour:

| Controller | $K_c$ | $T_i$ | $T_d$ | Expected Response |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P | 3.5 | $\infty$ | 0 | Offset present, no steady-state error elimination |
| PI | 3.15 | 175 s | 0 | Offset eliminated, slower settling, slight overshoot |
| PID | 4.2 | 105 s | 26.25 s | QAD response, fastest settling, smallest overshoot among three |

#### Step F — Calculate the Decay Ratio for the PID Response

Read the first and second peak heights from the recorded response:

$$
\text{First peak} = 63.5 \, ^\circ C \quad \text{Steady-state} = 60 \, ^\circ C \quad \Rightarrow \text{Peak 1 deviation} = 3.5 \, ^\circ C
$$

$$
\text{Second peak} = 60.875 \, ^\circ C \quad \text{Steady-state} = 60 \, ^\circ C \quad \Rightarrow \text{Peak 2 deviation} = 0.875 \, ^\circ C
$$

$$
\text{Decay Ratio} = \frac{0.875}{3.5} = 0.25 \;=\; \frac{1}{4}
$$

This **confirms the QAD criterion** — a passing result for the experiment.

### 3.2 Derivation — Why the Z–N Factor of 0.6 Works for PID

The Z–N factor for $K_c$ in the PID row is **0.6**. This factor can be derived (informally) by requiring that the closed-loop transfer function has a damping ratio of $\zeta \approx 0.21$, which is mathematically the boundary between the *underdamped* and *sustained-oscillation* regions.

For a FOPDT process with transfer function:

$$
G_p(s) \;=\; \frac{K_p \, e^{-L s}}{T s + 1}
$$

the closed-loop characteristic equation is approximately:

$$
1 \;+\; K_c \left( 1 + T_d s + \frac{1}{T_i s} \right) \frac{K_p \, e^{-L s}}{T s + 1} \;=\; 0
$$

Z–N solved this numerically for many processes and discovered that the factor **$K_c = 0.6 K_u$** consistently gives a closed-loop pole pair with damping ratio $\zeta \approx 0.21$, producing the quarter-amplitude decay response. The student is **not required to derive this rigorously** in the KTU exam, but should **understand the empirical basis** of the 0.6, 0.45, 0.5 factors.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Shortcut:** If you ever forget the Z–N table in the exam, you can *reconstruct* the factors from the following physical reasoning:
> - 0.5 = "half of $K_u$" — a round number that gives reasonable damping.
> - 0.45 = "slightly less than half" — because adding integral action destabilizes the loop a bit, so you need to back off more.
> - 0.6 = "more than half" — because adding derivative action *stabilizes* the loop, allowing a more aggressive gain.
> - $P_u/1.2$, $P_u/2$, $P_u/8$ are all in geometric progression with ratio 4, reflecting the relative time scales of integral and derivative action.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of the Closed-Loop Control System

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the standard unity-feedback control loop used in the Z–N experiment. Each block represents a physical or functional element of the rig.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Setpoint r] --> B[Comparator]
    C[Process Variable PV] --> B
    B --> D[Controller Kc Ti Td]
    D --> E[Actuator Heater]
    E --> F[Process Temperature Rig]
    F --> C
    F -.-> G[Disturbance Load]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **Setpoint** (e.g., 60 °C) is compared with the **Process Variable** (RTD output).
- The **Controller** (PID) computes the error $e = r - PV$ and applies the PID law.
- The **Actuator** (thyristor-driven heater) delivers power to the **Process** (the thermal mass + air).
- A **Disturbance** (e.g., ambient temperature fluctuation, door opening) perturbs the loop and is automatically rejected.

> [!NOTE]
> In Z–N closed-loop tuning, the **Controller** block is initially set to **P-only** with $T_i = \infty$ and $T_d = 0$. The tuning experiment manipulates $K_c$ only.

### 4.2 Detailed Functional Architecture of the Z–N Tuning Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Start: Process at Steady State] --> stepB[Set Controller in Auto Mode]
    stepB --> stepC[Set Ti Infinity and Td 0 P only]
    stepC --> stepD[Apply Small Setpoint Step]
    stepD --> stepE{Response Decays Damped}
    stepE -->|Yes| stepF[Increase Kc]
    stepF --> stepD
    stepE -->|Sustained Oscillation| stepG[Record Ku and Pu]
    stepE -->|Growing Oscillation| stepH[Stop Test Reduce Kc]
    stepG --> stepI[Apply ZN Formulas]
    stepI --> stepJ[Configure P Controller 0.5 Ku]
    stepI --> stepK[Configure PI Controller 0.45 Ku]
    stepI --> stepL[Configure PID Controller 0.6 Ku]
    stepJ --> stepM[Test Setpoint Step Response]
    stepK --> stepM
    stepL --> stepM
    stepM --> stepN[Verify Quarter Amplitude Decay]
    stepN --> stepO[End: Record Results]
```

**Key procedural checkpoints (read alongside the flowchart):**

- The **decision diamond "Response Decays Damped"** is the heart of the closed-loop method. The student must carefully distinguish between three response types:
  1. **Decaying oscillation** → still under-tuned; increase $K_c$.
  2. **Sustained oscillation** → $K_u$ reached; record immediately.
  3. **Growing oscillation** → $K_c > K_u$; the loop is unstable; reduce gain urgently.
- The **apply formulas** block uses the canonical Z–N table discussed in Section 2.4.
- The **verify QAD** block is the KTU-board evaluation criterion — a successful experiment must yield a decay ratio of $1/4$ for the PID response.

### 4.3 Schematic of a Typical Temperature Control Rig (Functional Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rig1[PT100 RTD Sensor] --> rig2[Transmitter 4-20 mA]
    rig2 --> rig3[PID Controller Front Panel]
    rig3 --> rig4[Thyristor Power Controller]
    rig4 --> rig5[Heater Coil 230V AC]
    rig5 --> rig6[Thermal Mass Water or Air]
    rig6 --> rig1
    rig7[Laptop with Data Acquisition] -.->|Logs PV and MV| rig3
```

**Component-by-component mapping:**

- **PT-100 RTD** — Platinum Resistance Temperature Detector, 100 Ω at 0 °C, $\approx 0.385 \, \Omega/^\circ C$.
- **Transmitter** — Converts RTD resistance to a standard 4–20 mA current loop signal.
- **PID Controller** — The "tuning target" — its $K_c$, $T_i$, $T_d$ are set manually by the student based on Z–N results.
- **Thyristor Power Controller** — Phase-angle-fired SCR that varies AC voltage to the heater in proportion to the controller output (0–100%).
- **Heater Coil** — The final control element; converts electrical energy to thermal energy.
- **Thermal Mass** — The process; its temperature is the controlled variable.
- **Laptop / DAQ** — Logs PV and MV for offline analysis of decay ratio, overshoot, settling time, etc.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Lab Tip:** Many KTU-affiliated colleges use rigs from **Feedback Instruments Ltd.** (e.g., model 33-005 Temperature Process Trainer) or **BICA / Vi Microsystems** (e.g., PCT-100). The block diagram above is generic and applies to all such rigs.

### 4.4 Expected Response Comparison — P vs. PI vs. PID (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph respP[Response of P Controller]
        p1[Steady State with Offset]
    end
    subgraph respPI[Response of PI Controller]
        pi1[Zero Offset Slow Settling]
    end
    subgraph respPID[Response of PID Controller]
        pid1[QAD Response Fast Settling]
    end
```

The three controllers produce visibly different closed-loop responses:

- **P-only:** Returns to a steady value that is *not* equal to the setpoint — there is a **steady-state offset** because integral action is missing.
- **PI:** Removes the offset due to integral action, but settling is slower and there can be a noticeable overshoot.
- **PID:** Combines the best of both worlds — derivative action anticipates the future error trend, allowing a more aggressive gain and a faster, well-damped (QAD) response.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**
*Define the terms (i) Ultimate Gain ($K_u$) and (ii) Ultimate Period ($P_u$) as used in the Ziegler–Nichols closed-loop tuning method.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

(i) **Ultimate Gain ($K_u$):** The value of proportional gain $K_c$ (with $T_i = \infty$ and $T_d = 0$) at which the closed-loop system output exhibits **sustained oscillations of constant amplitude** for a step disturbance. Beyond this gain, the system becomes unstable. **(1.5 marks)**

(ii) **Ultimate Period ($P_u$):** The **period of the sustained oscillation** observed in the process variable when the controller is set to $K_u$. It is the time between two consecutive peaks (or troughs) of the PV waveform. **(1.5 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*State the Ziegler–Nichols closed-loop tuning formulas for a PID controller in terms of $K_u$ and $P_u$. What response criterion do these settings target?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Z–N closed-loop tuning settings for a **PID controller** are:

$$
K_c \;=\; 0.6 \, K_u \qquad T_i \;=\; \frac{P_u}{2} \qquad T_d \;=\; \frac{P_u}{8}
$$

**Response Criterion:** **Quarter-Amplitude Decay (QAD)** — the second overshoot of the closed-loop step response is **one-quarter** the size of the first overshoot. This corresponds to a damping ratio of approximately **$\zeta \approx 0.21$**. **(1 mark for criterion)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

**Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — July 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Ziegler–Nichols closed-loop tuning method**. Define ultimate gain and ultimate period. List the procedural steps to be followed while performing this tuning on a temperature control station in the lab. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a Z–N closed-loop tuning experiment on a temperature rig, the sustained oscillation of the PV is observed at a proportional gain of $K_u = 8$ with an oscillation period of $P_u = 4$ minutes. Calculate the Z–N tuning settings for **P, PI, and PID controllers** and tabulate them. State the **expected closed-loop behaviour** for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Block diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1. Key elements: Setpoint → Comparator → Controller (P-only during tuning) → Actuator (heater) → Process (thermal mass) → PV sensor → back to comparator.

**Ultimate gain $K_u$ (1 mark):** Smallest value of $K_c$ (with $T_i = \infty$, $T_d = 0$) that produces sustained constant-amplitude oscillation in PV.

**Ultimate period $P_u$ (1 mark):** Period (in seconds or minutes) of those sustained oscillations.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Bring the process to a steady state.
2. Set controller in Automatic mode, P-only ($T_i = \infty$, $T_d = 0$).
3. Start with a small $K_c$ and increase gradually.
4. Apply a small setpoint step at each gain value.
5. Identify $K_u$ as the gain giving sustained oscillation.
6. Record $P_u$ from the time between two consecutive PV peaks.
7. Reduce $K_c$ immediately; do not exceed $K_u$.
8. Compute P, PI, PID settings using the Z–N table.
9. Implement and test each controller; record the QAD response.

**(b) [7 marks]**

Given: $K_u = 8$, $P_u = 4$ min.

**P controller (1.5 marks):**

$$
K_c^{(P)} = 0.5 \times 8 = 4 \qquad T_i = \infty \qquad T_d = 0
$$

**PI controller (2 marks):**

$$
K_c^{(PI)} = 0.45 \times 8 = 3.6 \qquad T_i = \frac{4}{1.2} = 3.33 \; \text{min} \qquad T_d = 0
$$

**PID controller (2.5 marks):**

$$
K_c^{(PID)} = 0.6 \times 8 = 4.8 \qquad T_i = \frac{4}{2} = 2 \; \text{min} \qquad T_d = \frac{4}{8} = 0.5 \; \text{min}
$$

**Tabulation (0.5 mark):**

| Controller | $K_c$ | $T_i$ (min) | $T_d$ (min) |
|---|---|---|---|
| P | 4 | $\infty$ | 0 |
| PI | 3.6 | 3.33 | 0 |
| PID | 4.8 | 2.0 | 0.5 |

**Expected Behaviour (0.5 mark):** P → offset remains; PI → offset removed, slow settling; PID → QAD response, fastest settling among the three.

---

**Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)] — Internal Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **difference between Z–N open-loop and Z–N closed-loop tuning methods**. Mention one advantage and one limitation of each. In what type of process is the closed-loop method preferred? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The Z–N closed-loop tuning of a level-control station gives $K_u = 5$ and $P_u = 2$ min. Compute the **controller settings** for P, PI, and PID modes. If the process is subsequently required to operate with a **decay ratio of 0.1** instead of 0.25, what change in $K_c$ would you suggest (qualitative reasoning only)? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Difference (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Open-Loop (Reaction Curve) | Closed-Loop (Continuous Cycling) |
|---|---|---|
| Test Mode | Manual (open loop) | Automatic (closed loop) |
| Step Applied | Step in MV (manipulated variable) | Step in Setpoint |
| Data Required | Process gain $K_p$, dead time $L$, time constant $T$ | $K_u$, $P_u$ |
| Risk | Low (small step) | Higher (sustained oscillation) |

**Open-loop advantage (0.5):** Safe, single experiment, gives full FOPDT model.
**Open-loop limitation (0.5):** Requires accurate step and accurate identification of $K_p$, $L$, $T$.

**Closed-loop advantage (0.5):** Works for higher-order / integrating processes; only needs oscillation frequency.
**Closed-loop limitation (0.5):** Forces sustained oscillation; can be unsafe for real plant.

**Preferred for (1 mark):** **Slow, higher-order, or non-minimum-phase processes** (e.g., temperature, level, pH, composition) where a clean open-loop step response is hard to obtain or interpret.

**Note:** Flow and pressure loops are usually too fast for sustained cycling and are tuned with open-loop methods or model-predictive methods instead.

**(b) [7 marks]**

Given: $K_u = 5$, $P_u = 2$ min.

**P controller (1.5 marks):**

$$
K_c^{(P)} = 0.5 \times 5 = 2.5 \qquad T_i = \infty \qquad T_d = 0
$$

**PI controller (2 marks):**

$$
K_c^{(PI)} = 0.45 \times 5 = 2.25 \qquad T_i = \frac{2}{1.2} = 1.667 \; \text{min} \qquad T_d = 0
$$

**PID controller (2.5 marks):**

$$
K_c^{(PID)} = 0.6 \times 5 = 3.0 \qquad T_i = \frac{2}{2} = 1.0 \; \text{min} \qquad T_d = \frac{2}{8} = 0.25 \; \text{min}
$$

**Qualitative reasoning for tighter decay (1 mark):** A decay ratio of **0.1** is more conservative (more damped) than the Z–N default of **0.25**. To achieve more damping, the **proportional gain $K_c$ must be reduced** below the Z–N value, i.e., the factor 0.6 should be replaced by something smaller, such as **0.4 or 0.5**. This pushes the closed-loop poles further into the stable region, increasing the damping ratio $\zeta$ and reducing the overshoot at the cost of slower response.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $T_i$ and $T_d$ units:** Some KTU-affiliated controllers display integral in *repeats/min* instead of *minutes*. The student must convert: $R \text{ (repeats/min)} = 60 / T_i \text{ (s)} = 1 / T_i \text{ (min)}$. **Marks lost if no unit conversion shown.**
> 
> 2. **Confusing Z–N open-loop and closed-loop formulas:** Open-loop uses $K_p$, $L$, $T$; closed-loop uses $K_u$, $P_u$. Mixing them up is a guaranteed **0–2 marks** deduction.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting to mention the QAD criterion:** The Z–N method targets a decay ratio of **1/4**. The KTU board specifically looks for this in the answer. Students who write "PID will work well" without quantifying the expected response lose marks.
> 
> 4. **Exceeding $K_u$ in the lab:** A real safety hazard. In the exam, if asked "what happens if you increase $K_c$ beyond $K_u$?", students must say the loop becomes **unstable** with growing oscillations, **not** "faster".
> 
> 5. **Mixing P, PI, and PID definitions:** Students sometimes write "$T_i$ is the derivative time" or "$K_c$ is the integral gain". Memorize: $K_c$ (proportional), $T_i$ (integral / reset), $T_d$ (derivative / rate).
> 
> 6. **Forgetting the "closed-loop" qualifier:** Z–N has two methods. The examiner will not give full credit if the student does not explicitly state which method is being used.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before the Exam:**
> 
> - **Z–N closed-loop method is also called** Ultimate Gain Method or Continuous Cycling Method.
> - The method requires the controller in **Automatic, P-only mode** ($T_i = \infty$, $T_d = 0$).
> - $K_u$ = gain at which the loop oscillates with **constant amplitude**.
> - $P_u$ = period of the **sustained oscillation**.
> - **P:** $K_c = 0.5 K_u$, $T_i = \infty$, $T_d = 0$.
> - **PI:** $K_c = 0.45 K_u$, $T_i = P_u / 1.2$, $T_d = 0$.
> - **PID:** $K_c = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = P_u / 2$, $T_d = P_u / 8$.
> - **Target behaviour:** **Quarter-Amplitude Decay (QAD)** — decay ratio = 1/4.
> - **Process type best suited:** FOPDT (First-Order Plus Dead Time), slow loops such as **temperature and level**.
> - **Limitation:** Sustained oscillations can damage real industrial equipment → industry uses relay-feedback autotune (Åström–Hägglund) instead.
> - **Difference from Z–N open-loop:** Open-loop uses a process reaction curve and a step in MV; closed-loop uses a step in setpoint and a P-only sweep.
> - **Safety:** Never leave a process at $K_u$ — return to safe $K_c$ immediately after recording.
> - **Unit conversions:** $R$ (repeats/min) = $60 / T_i$ (seconds) = $1 / T_i$ (minutes).
> - **Why does Z–N work empirically?** The factors 0.5, 0.45, 0.6 were chosen to give a damping ratio of $\zeta \approx 0.21$, producing the QAD response.
> - **Examiner pitfalls to avoid:** Confusing $K_u$ with $K_p$, mixing up open-loop and closed-loop formulas, forgetting units, exceeding $K_u$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarisation of cascade controller and feedforward controller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL607 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarisation of cascade controller and feedforward controller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Cascade Controller — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Cascade Control** is an advanced multi-loop control strategy in which the output of one (primary/master) controller is used to dynamically adjust the *setpoint* of a second (secondary/slave) controller, rather than directly driving the final control element. The two PID controllers are arranged in a *nested, hierarchical* configuration: the **outer (primary) loop** regulates the main process variable, while the **inner (secondary) loop** regulates an intermediate (secondary) variable closer to the disturbance source.

In a cascade arrangement, the secondary loop must be **at least 3 to 5 times faster** than the primary loop. This speed differential is the fundamental stability requirement — without it, the two loops will *fight* each other and the system will oscillate.

**KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology (exact):**
- **Primary Variable (PV₁) / Controlled Variable** — the ultimate process variable the operator wants to hold at setpoint (e.g., reactor temperature).
- **Secondary Variable (PV₂) / Inner-loop variable** — an intermediate variable (e.g., jacket-outlet temperature, coolant flow rate) that responds more quickly to disturbances.
- **Primary Controller (Master, $C_1$)** — typically PI or PID, slow tuning.
- **Secondary Controller (Slave, $C_2$)** — typically P or PI only (derivative action is rarely used in the slave), aggressive tuning.
- **Disturbance Splitting** — disturbances that enter the secondary process are rejected by the inner loop *before* they propagate to the primary variable.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Cruise Control + Engine RPM

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you are driving a car on a hilly road and want to maintain a steady **speed of 60 km/h** (the primary variable). You cannot directly command "speed = 60" to the engine — the accelerator pedal controls *engine RPM*, not speed. A human driver naturally does this: the **outer loop** (you) watches the speedometer, and based on the deviation, you *adjust the target RPM* of the engine; the **inner loop** (engine ECU) works to keep the RPM exactly at whatever target you set. If a hill arrives, the engine RPM sags — the ECU instantly compensates by opening the throttle, and the speed barely changes. The *inner loop absorbs the disturbance* (the hill), so the *outer loop* (you) doesn't even have to react.
>
> This is exactly what cascade control does: the secondary loop is the "engine ECU" and the primary loop is the "driver."

### 1.3 Feedforward Controller — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Feedforward Control** is a control strategy that measures *disturbances directly* and takes corrective action *before* the disturbance has had a chance to affect the process output. It is a purely *predictive* strategy — it relies on a process model that maps how a measured disturbance $d(t)$ propagates to the controlled variable, and pre-compensates for it.

**KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology (exact):**
- **Disturbance Variable ($d(t)$)** — the measurable, identified input that we anticipate will upset the process.
- **Feedforward Controller Transfer Function, $G_{ff}(s)$** — implemented as a *lead-lag* compensator, ideally equal to $-G_d(s)/G_p(s)$, where $G_d(s)$ is the disturbance-to-output transfer function and $G_p(s)$ is the manipulated-variable-to-output transfer function.
- **Static Feedforward** — uses only steady-state gain ratios (simple but ignores dynamics).
- **Dynamic Feedforward** — uses a lead-lag element with time constants matched to the process.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The Shower Thermometer

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** You are taking a shower and want water at **exactly 40 °C**. The *disturbance* is when someone else in the house flushes a toilet, causing the *cold water pressure to suddenly drop* (measured disturbance). A *feedback* controller would let the water briefly go hot, *then* correct it. A *feedforward* controller, in contrast, has a thermometer on the *cold supply line*. The instant it senses the pressure drop, it *predicts* "output will rise" and immediately *opens the cold valve more*, holding the shower temperature *constant before any deviation occurs*. Feedback *corrects* an error that has happened; feedforward *anticipates* an error that would happen.

### 1.5 Critical Distinction at a Glance

| Property | Cascade Control | Feedforward Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Reacts to** | Deviations in the *secondary variable* (indirect) | Measured *disturbance* (direct) |
| **Requires a model?** | No (empirical tuning of both loops is enough) | **Yes** — process model is mandatory |
| **Rejection speed** | Fast (secondary loop), but still error-based | Theoretically instantaneous (anticipatory) |
| **Practical form** | Two nested PID loops | Lead-lag block summed with feedback output |
| **Common pairing** | Used WITH feedback (outer feedback master) | Almost always used **WITH** feedback (FF + FB) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Examiner Note:** In both cascade and feedforward implementations, students will lose marks if they draw only the *primary/feedforward* path and omit the **mandatory feedback trim**. Real industrial systems are *almost never* pure feedforward or pure cascade — feedback is always present to correct for model inaccuracies, unmeasured disturbances, and drift.

### 1.6 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU 2024)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value for This Experiment |
|---|---|---|
| **Integral Square Error** | $\text{ISE} = \int_0^\infty e(t)^2\, dt$ | Used to compare tuning settings |
| **Integral Absolute Error** | $\text{IAE} = \int_0^\infty \vert e(t) \vert\, dt$ | Most common in lab comparison |
| **Settling Time (2% band)** | $t_s$ | Recorded from trend plot |
| **Maximum Overshoot** | $M_p$ | Recorded as % of setpoint step |
| **Loop Speed Ratio** | $\tau_{primary} / \tau_{secondary}$ | Must be $\geq 3$ for cascade stability |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cascade Control — Operational Logic (Step by Step)

The cascade architecture is *always* drawn with the **secondary loop physically inside** the primary loop. The control action propagates as follows:

1. **Operator sets** a setpoint $SP_1$ for the primary (outer) variable.
2. The **primary controller** $C_1(s)$ computes its output $u_1(t)$ based on error $e_1 = SP_1 - PV_1$.
3. Critically, **$u_1(t)$ is NOT sent to the valve**. Instead, it becomes the *setpoint* of the secondary controller: $SP_2 = u_1$.
4. The **secondary controller** $C_2(s)$ computes its output $u_2(t)$ based on the secondary-loop error $e_2 = SP_2 - PV_2$.
5. **$u_2(t)$ is the signal that finally drives the final control element (valve, pump, heater)**.
6. A disturbance $d$ entering the *secondary process* $G_{p2}$ is sensed by the secondary transmitter and corrected by the inner loop — the primary variable never sees it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a cascade loop must be faster:** If the inner loop is slow, the primary controller will issue a *new* $SP_2$ before the inner loop has finished responding to the *previous* one. The two loops end up chasing each other in a closed cycle. The empirical rule of thumb is: **$\tau_{primary} \geq 3 \cdot \tau_{secondary}$** (where $\tau$ is the dominant time constant). Some references use **5×** as the more conservative limit.

### 2.2 Cascade Control — Closed-Loop Transfer Function Derivation Outline

For the secondary (inner) loop alone, the closed-loop transfer function from $SP_2$ to $PV_2$ is:

$$
