$$L_{EU} = \left( \dfrac{N_{raw} - N_{min}}{N_{max} - N_{min}} \right) \times (L_{max} - L_{min}) + L_{min}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$L_{EU} = \left( \dfrac{2867 - 819}{4095 - 819} \right) \times (5 - 0) + 0$$

$$L_{EU} = \left( \dfrac{2048}{3276} \right) \times 5$$

$$L_{EU} = 0.6251 \times 5 = 3.1255 \approx 3.13\,m$$

**Verification:** 4 mA → 0 m; 12 mA → mid-scale → 2.5 m; 20 mA → 5 m. A count of 2867 (≈14 mA) should be 3.125 m, which matches. ✓

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing PLC and SCADA roles:** SCADA does *not* directly drive actuators; it sends *setpoints* to the PLC, which then drives outputs. Writing "SCADA controls the motor" loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong ADC formula:** Using $2^n$ instead of $2^n - 1$ for full-scale count. The maximum count for a 12-bit ADC is **4095**, not **4096**.
> 3. **Forgetting the scan cycle's image-table concept:** Writing "PLC continuously monitors inputs" loses a mark; the correct statement is "PLC samples inputs once per scan and stores them in the IIT."
> 4. **Omitting the live-zero:** A 4–20 mA loop has **4 mA = 0%**, not **0 mA**. A break in the wire (0 mA) is detected as a *fault*, not as 0% process value.
> 5. **No diagram in (a)-parts:** KTU examiners *expect* a labelled block diagram in any 7-mark descriptive sub-question. Skipping the diagram costs 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] **PLC = Local, fast, deterministic control** (1–20 ms); **SCADA = Supervisory, slow, plant-wide monitoring** (1–10 s).
- [ ] **PLC scan cycle** has 4 phases: Input Scan → Program Scan → Output Scan → Housekeeping → Loop.
- [ ] **Scan time** = $T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{hsk}$ — keep below the application's required response time.
- [ ] **IIT and OIT** are *snapshots*; this is what makes PLC behavior **deterministic and predictable**.
- [ ] **SCADA architecture = 4 layers:** Field → Control → Supervisory → Management.
- [ ] **Historian** is a time-series database for *all* tag values; used for trending, compliance, and predictive maintenance.
- [ ] **PLC–SCADA interface** uses **Modbus TCP** (legacy, polling) or **OPC UA** (modern, event-driven, secure).
- [ ] **Tag mapping** = declaring a SCADA-side variable bound to a specific PLC memory address (e.g., `%DB1.DBX0.0`).
- [ ] **4–20 mA** standard: **4 mA = 0% (live-zero)**, **20 mA = 100%**. Wire break (0 mA) = **fault**, not 0%.
- [ ] **ADC count** for $n$ bits: range is **$0$ to $2^n - 1$**, not $0$ to $2^n$.
- [ ] **Analog scaling formula:** $EU = \dfrac{(N_{raw} - N_{min}) \times (EU_{max} - EU_{min})}{N_{max} - N_{min}} + EU_{min}$.
- [ ] **Communication rule:** Always ensure $T_{poll} \geq T_{scan}$ to avoid stale SCADA data.
- [ ] **Common protocols** (in order of KTU relevance): **Modbus RTU/TCP → Profibus/Profinet → Ethernet/IP → OPC UA → IEC 60870-5-104 → DNP3**.
- [ ] **Lab record essentials:** Hardware identification, software familiarisation, simple ladder program, tag-mapping table, screenshot of live HMI, alarm log printout.
- [ ] **Determinism** = worst-case bounded response time; **real-time** ≠ *fast*, it means *guaranteed on time*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control of process stations using DCS and construction of ISA diagram
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL607 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Control of process stations using DCS and construction of ISA diagram

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Distributed Control System (DCS) — Formal Definition

A **Distributed Control System (DCS)** is a digitally networked, computer-based supervisory control architecture in which autonomous controllers are geographically distributed across the plant floor, yet integrated under a central human–machine interface (HMI) for unified process monitoring, control, and data historian functions. It replaces the legacy single-loop pneumatic / analog electronic controllers by distributing intelligence into **process stations (controllers, I/O stations)** and aggregating supervision into **operator and engineering stations**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from syllabus):**
> A DCS is a system that divides process plant functions into **subsystems**, each controlled by one or more controllers, with supervisory control, data acquisition, and centralized operator interaction enabled by high-speed digital communication buses (e.g., FOUNDATION Fieldbus, Profibus, Ethernet/IP).

## 1.2 ISA Diagram — Formal Definition

An **ISA Diagram** is a process and instrumentation schematic drawn strictly in accordance with the **ANSI/ISA-5.1 Instrumentation Symbols and Identification** standard. It uses standardized graphic symbols and a structured alphanumeric tag-numbering convention to depict the **measurement, control, and interlock philosophy** of an entire process loop on a single drawing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ISA?** Before ISA-5.1, every company used proprietary symbols, making cross-plant engineering impossible. ISA-5.1 is the **global lingua franca** of instrumentation — the same way a circuit schematic uses IEEE symbols.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

| Engineering Concept | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **DCS as a whole** | The brain + nervous system of the human body |
| **Process Stations (Controllers)** | Reflex centers at the spinal cord — make fast local decisions |
| **Field Instruments (PT, TT, FT)** | Sensory receptors on skin (touch, heat, pain) |
| **Operator Station (HMI)** | The conscious mind — sees the big picture |
| **Communication Network (Bus)** | The spinal cord — carries signals between body and brain |
| **Historian Server** | The long-term memory (hippocampus) — stores all past events |
| **ISA Diagram** | A road map of the nervous system drawn for a doctor |

> [!TIP]
> When the experiment asks you to "control a process station using DCS," picture: the teacher gives you an **industrial pilot plant** (say, a level-control rig). The DCS software (e.g., Honeywell Experion, Yokogawa CENTUM VP, or DeltaV) acts as the **digital brain** that you program to keep the level at the desired setpoint, while you simultaneously draw the **ISA P&ID** showing exactly how the field instruments are wired into the control loop.

## 1.4 Standards and Physical Constants

- **ISA-5.1** — Instrumentation Symbols and Identification (current revision uses **SAMA** loop numbering).
- **ISA-5.2** — Binary Logic Diagrams.
- **ISA-5.3** — Graphic Symbols for Distributed Control / Shared Display Instrumentation.
- **Communication Speed:** Modern DCS buses operate at **100 Mbps to 1 Gbps (Ethernet)**; legacy fieldbus at **31.25 kbps**.
- **Scan Time:** Typical process station I/O scan is **100 ms – 1 s**, fast enough for 99% of process loops (flow, level, pressure, temperature).
- **Redundancy:** Industrial-grade DCS uses **1oo2D** (1 out of 2 voted, with diagnostics) or **2oo2** redundant controllers for SIL compliance.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners often mark **"DCS = SCADA"** as **wrong**. DCS is **control-centric, plant-wide, integrated**; SCADA is **supervisory, geographically dispersed, telemetry-based** (e.g., water distribution, power grid). They are not synonymous.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 DCS — Hierarchical Architecture (Per ISO / IEC 62264 & ISA-95)

The DCS is structured in **four logical levels**, often called the **Purdue Model**:

| Level | Name | Function | Typical Hardware |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 0** | Field / Process | Sensing and final actuation | RTD, thermocouple, orifice plate, control valve, I/P |
| **Level 1** | Direct Control (Basic) | PID, interlocks, sequencing | Process Station (Controller + I/O) |
| **Level 2** | Supervisory Control | HMI, alarms, trending, recipes | Operator Station, Engineering Station |
| **Level 3** | MES / Operations | Production planning, batch records, KPI | Plant Information / MES Server |
| **Level 4** | ERP | Business, supply chain, finance | ERP system (SAP) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **DCS in a typical KTU lab** simulates only **Level 0 → Level 2**. The PC running the DCS software is acting as both the **Process Station** and the **Operator/Engineering Station**.

## 2.2 Components of a Process Station

A **Process Station (also called a Controller Station or Field Control Station — FCS)** is the heart of a DCS. It performs:

1. **Analog Input (AI) processing** — 4–20 mA / 1–5 V / RTD / TC scaling, linearization, square-root extraction (for DP flow), filtering.
2. **Analog Output (AO) generation** — 4–20 mA signal to I/P converter → pneumatic actuator.
3. **Digital Input (DI)** — limit switches, push-buttons, solenoid feedbacks.
4. **Digital Output (DO)** — solenoid valves, motor starters, alarm annunciators.
5. **PID computation** — executed in the controller function block at the configured scan period.
6. **Communication** — to operator station via fieldbus / Ethernet.

## 2.3 Types of Control Strategies Implemented in DCS

| Strategy | Loop Architecture | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-loop PID (Feedback)** | SP → PID → CV → Process → PV → PT → PID | Most generic (level, temp, pressure) |
| **Cascade** | Primary PID → Setpoint of Secondary PID → Valve | Temp. control with flow inner loop |
| **Feedforward** | Disturbance measured → FF controller → Valve | Inlet temp. disturbance compensation |
| **Ratio Control** | Flow A × K = Flow B setpoint | Blending, fuel-air ratio |
| **Split Range** | One controller output split into 2+ valve segments | Heating/cooling with two valves |
| **Override / Select** | High/Low signal selector picks controlling PV | Surge protection in compressor |
| **Auto–Manual (A/M) station** | Operator can take over output | Maintenance, start-up |

## 2.4 ISA-5.1 Symbol Vocabulary (Most Tested in KTU)

The ISA standard classifies symbols into **two planes**: **Field-Mounted** and **Control-Room (DCS-Mounted)**, plus **Shared Display**.

### 2.4.1 Instrument Bubble Convention

- A **circle** = a **discrete / field-mounted** instrument.
- A **circle inside a square** = **DCS / shared-display** (computer) instrument.
- A **square with horizontal line through the middle** = PLC.
- A **hexagon** = a **function block** (mathematical / computational).
- A **diamond** = a **hand-actuated** valve (or solenoid on some references).
- A **balloon with cross** = **instrument inside the same primary field location** as the process.

### 2.4.2 Line Symbols (Signal Types)

| Line Type | Symbol | Carries |
|---|---|---|
| **Solid line with cross-hatch** | ─×─ | Continuous process piping |
| **Solid line with arrowhead** | ─→─ | Continuous instrument signal (pneumatic / electric) |
| **Dashed line** | - - - - | Optional / interlock signal |
| **Line with double crossbar** | ─H─ | Hydraulic signal |
| **Line with double slash and dots** | ≡≡≡ | Capillary (filled thermal system) |
| **Line with circles (bubbles)** | ─◯◯◯─ | Electromagnetic / sonic / guided wave |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU lab viva, memorize: **solid with arrow = 4–20 mA signal line** (most common); **dashed = optional / interlock**. If a drawing is silent, assume 4–20 mA unless labeled.

### 2.4.3 Function Identification Letters (Tag Prefix)

Each instrument is tagged with a **3–4 letter code**. The **first letter = measured variable**; **subsequent letters = function, modifier, readout**.

| Letter | Variable | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **T** | Temperature | Transmit |
| **P** | Pressure | Indicate / Record |
| **F** | Flow | Transmit |
| **L** | Level | Indicate / Record |
| **A** | Analysis (pH, O₂) | Alarm |
| **C** | Conductivity | Control |
| **D** | Density / Differential | — |
| **E** | Voltage | Element (sensor) |
| **H** | Hand | High |
| **I** | Current | Indicate |
| **S** | Speed / Switch | Switch |
| **V** | Viscosity / Vibration | Valve |
| **Z** | Position | Drive |
| **Q** | Quantity | Integrate / Totalize |
| **R** | Radiation / Recorder | Record |
| **X** | Unclassified | Unclassified |
| **Y** | Event / State | — |
| **K** | Time / Time-schedule | Control station |

> [!NOTE]
> **Suffix letters**: **I** = Indicate, **R** = Record, **C** = Control, **T** = Transmit, **A** = Alarm, **S** = Switch, **V** = Valve, **E** = Element, **H** = High, **L** = Low.

### 2.4.4 Tag Number Structure

A complete ISA tag has the **format: `XX-NNN` or `XX-NNN-SS`**

- **XX** → ISA function letters (e.g., **TIC** = Temperature Indicating Controller).
- **NNN** → Loop number (e.g., 100, 101, 200 — typically thousands group instruments by area).
- **SS** → Optional suffix for repeated instruments in the same loop (e.g., `TT-100A`, `TT-100B` for dual-element RTD).

**Example:** `FIC-101` = Flow Indicating Controller, Loop 101.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 4–20 mA → Engineering units scaling | $E = \dfrac{I - 4}{20-4} \times (E_{max} - E_{min}) + E_{min}$ | $I$ in mA; $E$ in engineering units |
| Current-to-Pressure (I/P) conversion | $P_{out}(\text{psi}) = \dfrac{I - 4}{16} \times 15 + 3$ | Standard 3–15 psi pneumatic output |
| Standard PID (ISA) algorithm (Series / Parallel) | $u(t) = K_c\!\left[e(t) + \dfrac{1}{T_i}\!\int\! e(t)\,dt + T_d\dfrac{de(t)}{dt}\right]$ | $K_c$ = proportional gain (dimensionless), $T_i$ = integral time (min/repeat), $T_d$ = derivative time (min) |
| PID discrete (velocity) form used in DCS | $\Delta u_n = K_c\!\left[(e_n - e_{n-1}) + \dfrac{T_s}{T_i} e_n + \dfrac{T_d}{T_s}(e_n - 2e_{n-1} + e_{n-2})\right]$ | $T_s$ = scan period (s) |
| Orifice flow (Bernoulli) | $q_m = C_d A \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$ | $\Delta P$ from DPT |
| RTD (Pt100) linearization (Callendar–Van Dusen) | $R_T = R_0\!\left[1 + AT + BT^2 + C(T-100)T^3\right]$ | $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$ °C⁻¹, $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$ °C⁻², $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}$ °C⁻⁴ (for $T<0$ °C) |
| Control valve characteristic (Equal % ) | $C_v(\alpha) = C_{v,rated}\,\alpha^{\alpha - 1}$ for inherent, $\alpha = f(\text{travel})$ | Linear trim vs. equal-percentage trim |
| Loop scan time vs. process time-constant | $T_s \le 0.1 \tau_p$ | Rule of thumb for digital control |
| Signal Selector (Auto Override) | $Y = \max(\text{or } \min)(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$ | High/Low signal selector in DCS |
| DCS Communication update (FOUNDATION Fieldbus) | $\Delta t = \dfrac{N \times 32 \text{ bytes}}{31.25 \text{ kbps}}$ | $N$ = number of function blocks |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU pitfall:** In tables, **never use `|`** as absolute value (e.g., `|e|`) — it will break the markdown parser. Use `\vert e \vert` or `\lvert e \rvert` instead.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Standard 4–20 mA Linearization Equation (Universally Tested in KTU Labs)

In any DCS, the analog input card receives a **current signal $I$** in the range **4–20 mA**, which must be converted to the corresponding **engineering value $E$** (e.g., 0–200 °C, 0–10 bar, 0–500 L/min). We must therefore derive a linear mapping.

### Step 1 — State the two known end-point pairs

When $I = 4$ mA, the engineering value equals $E_{min}$.
When $I = 20$ mA, the engineering value equals $E_{max}$.

$$
(I_1, E_1) = (4,\, E_{min}) \qquad
(I_2, E_2) = (20,\, E_{max})
$$

### Step 2 — Write the two-point form of a straight line

$$
E - E_1 = \dfrac{E_2 - E_1}{I_2 - I_1}\,(I - I_1)
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the values

$$
E - E_{min} = \dfrac{E_{max} - E_{min}}{20 - 4}\,(I - 4)
$$

### Step 4 — Rearrange to get the KTU-formula form

$$
E = E_{min} + \dfrac{E_{max} - E_{min}}{16}\,(I - 4)
$$

This is the **engineering linearization equation** used in the DCS scaling block.

> [!NOTE]
> The **range** of the loop is $R = E_{max} - E_{min}$. The percentage of range is $\%R = \dfrac{E - E_{min}}{R}\times 100$. So $I = 4 + 0.16 \times \%R$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 (Level Loop)

A differential pressure transmitter (DPT) is ranged **0–500 mmH₂O** mapped to **4–20 mA**. The process level is reading **I = 12 mA**.

**Step 1 — Apply the linearization formula**

$$
E = 0 + \dfrac{500 - 0}{16}\,(12 - 4)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the difference**

$$
I - 4 = 12 - 4 = 8 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 3 — Divide by the span**

$$
\dfrac{8}{16} = 0.5 \;\;(50\% \text{ of range})
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by the engineering span**

$$
E = 0.5 \times 500 = 250 \text{ mmH}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to a useful unit if required**

$$
E = 250 \text{ mmH}_2\text{O} = 0.25 \text{ mH}_2\text{O} = 0.00245 \text{ bar (gauge)}
$$

using $\rho_{H_2O} = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Discrete (Velocity) PID Form Used in DCS

A DCS executes the PID algorithm **digitally at each scan instant $n$**, where $n$ denotes the current scan, $n-1$ the previous scan, and $n-2$ two scans ago.

### Step 1 — Start with the continuous-time ISA (parallel) form

$$
u(t) = K_c e(t) + \dfrac{K_c}{T_i}\!\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_c T_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

### Step 2 — Replace the integral with a backward-difference (rectangular) sum

$$
\int_0^{nT_s} e(\tau)\,d\tau \;\approx\; T_s \sum_{k=1}^{n} e_k
$$

### Step 3 — Replace the derivative with a backward difference

$$
\dfrac{de(t)}{dt}\bigg|_{t=nT_s} \;\approx\; \dfrac{e_n - e_{n-1}}{T_s}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute into the PID expression at scan $n$

$$
u_n = K_c e_n + \dfrac{K_c T_s}{T_i}\sum_{k=1}^{n} e_k + \dfrac{K_c T_d}{T_s}(e_n - e_{n-1})
$$

### Step 5 — Form the same expression at scan $n-1$ and subtract

$$
u_{n-1} = K_c e_{n-1} + \dfrac{K_c T_s}{T_i}\sum_{k=1}^{n-1} e_k + \dfrac{K_c T_d}{T_s}(e_{n-1} - e_{n-2})
$$

### Step 6 — Subtract to obtain the **velocity (incremental) form**

$$
u_n - u_{n-1} = K_c(e_n - e_{n-1}) + \dfrac{K_c T_s}{T_i}e_n + \dfrac{K_c T_d}{T_s}(e_n - 2e_{n-1} + e_{n-2})
$$

### Step 7 — Solve for the **incremental change in controller output**

$$
\Delta u_n = K_c \!\left[(e_n - e_{n-1}) + \dfrac{T_s}{T_i}e_n + \dfrac{T_d}{T_s}(e_n - 2e_{n-1} + e_{n-2})\right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **velocity form** is the form actually implemented in DCS function blocks (e.g., Honeywell's `PIDFF` block, Yokogawa's `PID` block, Emerson DeltaV's `PID` block) because:
> - **Bumpless transfer** from manual to auto is automatic.
> - The output is incremental, so a sensor or transmitter failure cannot drive the valve to a dangerous extreme (output saturates only at the configured limits).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How to Recognize a Tag in an ISA Drawing

| Drawing shows | Tag is | What it does |
|---|---|---|
| Circle (or square-bubble) with **TT-100** | Temperature **T**rans**T**ransmit | RTD/TC sensor on the field, transmits a signal |
| Square-bubble with **TIC-100** | Temperature **I**ndicating **C**ontroller | DCS block: reads PV, indicates, computes PID, outputs CV |
| Square-bubble with **PI-101** | Pressure **I**ndicate (DCS) | DCS reads pressure, displays it on HMI |
| Hexagon with **FY-100** | Function block **Y** (math) | Performs square-root extraction / linearization |
| Diamond with **PCV-101** | Pressure **C**ontrol **V**alve | Field pneumatic valve actuator |
| Square with cross + **XV-101** | On/Off (Solenoid) Valve | Discrete output from DCS |
| Square-bubble with **LAH-100** | Level **A**larm **H**igh | DCS triggers an alarm when level exceeds high limit |
| Square with horizontal line + **PCS-200** | Programmable Controller Station (PLC) | Used when DCS is not suited; PLC handles discrete logic |

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Construction of a Cascade-Control ISA Diagram

**Cascade control** is the most-tested configuration in KTU viva. Below is the methodical procedure.

### Step 1 — Identify the **Primary (Master) and Secondary (Slave) variables**.

- Example: **Reactor temperature (primary) → coolant flow (secondary)**.

### Step 2 — Identify the **Primary transmitter** and **Secondary transmitter**.

- Primary transmitter: `TT-100` (RTD/TC on reactor shell).
- Secondary transmitter: `FT-101` (orifice + DPT on coolant line).

### Step 3 — Identify the **Primary controller and Secondary controller**.

- Primary: `TIC-100` (master).
- Secondary: `FIC-101` (slave).

### Step 4 — Identify the **Final Control Element** for the secondary loop.

- `FCV-101` (flow control valve on coolant line).

### Step 5 — Wire the cascade:

- Output of `TIC-100` becomes the **remote setpoint (RSP)** of `FIC-101`.
- PV of `FIC-101` is `FT-101`'s signal.
- Output of `FIC-101` drives `FCV-101`.

### Step 6 — Draw the ISA diagram.

- Use circles/bubbles for transmitters, square-bubbles for DCS controllers, diamond for the valve, arrows for 4–20 mA signals.

### Step 7 — Add the **loop tag suffix** for the inner loop:

- `FT-101` is the inner-loop transmitter.
- `FIC-101` is the inner-loop controller.
- `FCV-101` is the inner-loop final element.
- The OUTER loop is `TT-100` → `TIC-100` → RSP of `FIC-101`.

### Step 8 — Verify on DCS:

- In DCS configuration, set `FIC-101.SP` source = `TIC-100.MV` (mode = "Cascade").
- Run secondary loop first in auto, then switch to cascade for the primary.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]
**Differentiate between DCS and SCADA. List any four major components of a DCS.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | DCS | SCADA |
|---|---|---|
| **Geographic span** | Local, single plant | Wide, multi-site |
| **Primary function** | Continuous regulatory control | Supervisory / data acquisition |
| **Architecture** | Distributed controllers, central HMI | Master–slave RTUs, central MTU |
| **Application** | Refinery, chemical plant | Power grid, water distribution |
| **Redundancy** | Built-in (1oo2, 2oo3) | Often minimal |

**Four major components of a DCS:**

1. **Process / Controller Station** — contains PID function blocks and I/O.
2. **Operator / Engineering Station** — HMI for monitoring and configuration.
3. **Communication Network (Fieldbus / Ethernet)** — links stations.
4. **Historian / Data Server** — long-term time-series storage and trending.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]
**Explain the significance of ISA-5.1 standard. What is the meaning of the tag `TICAHL-200`?**

**Model Answer:**

**Significance of ISA-5.1:** It is the universally accepted standard that defines (a) the **graphic symbols** used to depict instruments, controllers and valves; (b) the **letter codes** for process variables and instrument functions; and (c) the **tag-numbering convention** for unique loop identification. It enables cross-plant engineering communication and unambiguous design documentation.

**Decoding `TICAHL-200`:**
- **First letter T** → measured variable is **Temperature**.
- **Suffix I** → instrument has **Indication** (HMI display).
- **Suffix C** → instrument has **Control** (PID output).
- **Suffix A** → instrument generates an **Alarm**.
- **Suffix H** → the alarm is **High** alarm.
- **Suffix L** → (optional modifier) located on the **L**ocal panel (field-mounted).
- **200** → loop number 200.

**Therefore:** `TICAHL-200` = a **Temperature Indicating Controller with High Alarm**, **field-mounted** in loop **200**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Forgetting to put the **ISA bubble inside a square** for DCS-mounted instruments (vs. a plain circle for field-mounted) is a **2-mark deduction** every time it occurs.
> - Drawing a **single straight line for signal** instead of an **arrow-headed line** is a **1-mark deduction**.
> - Omitting the **loop number** at the end of a tag is a **1-mark deduction**.
> - In cascade questions, the student **must draw the inner loop first** in engineering practice; in the exam, **label the RSP arrow clearly**.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat labeled diagram, explain the **architecture of a Distributed Control System (DCS)**. List the function of each level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thermocouple (Type K) is connected to a DCS AI card via a temperature transmitter ranged **0–600 °C** to give **4–20 mA**. The current observed is **13.6 mA**. Calculate the **measured temperature** and the **percentage of range**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) DCS Architecture — see Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram above**

- **Level 0 — Field:** Sensors (RTD, TC, DPT) and final control elements (control valve, pump, motor).
- **Level 1 — Process Station:** Analog/Digital I/O cards, PID function blocks, scan engine, redundancy.
- **Level 2 — Supervisory:** Operator HMI (faceplates, trends, alarms), Engineering Station (configuration), Historian (database).
- **Level 3 — Operations Management:** Production scheduling, batch records, KPI dashboards (out of lab scope but mentioned for completeness).
- **Level 4 — ERP:** Business-level planning.

Each level communicates with the next through **standardized fieldbus / Ethernet**, enabling vertical integration.

**(b) Calculation**

**Step 1 — Apply the linearization formula**

$$
E = E_{min} + \dfrac{E_{max} - E_{min}}{16}\,(I - 4)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values**

$$
E = 0 + \dfrac{600 - 0}{16}\,(13.6 - 4)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the difference**

$$
I - 4 = 13.6 - 4 = 9.6 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by 16**

$$
\dfrac{9.6}{16} = 0.60
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by the engineering span**

$$
E = 0.60 \times 600 = 360\,^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the percentage of range**

$$
\%R = \dfrac{E - E_{min}}{E_{max} - E_{min}} \times 100 = \dfrac{360 - 0}{600 - 0} \times 100 = 60\,\%
$$

**Verification using the current formula directly:**

$$
\%R = \dfrac{I - 4}{16} \times 100 = \dfrac{9.6}{16} \times 100 = 60\,\% \;\checkmark
$$

**Final Answer:** $E = 360\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ at 60% of range.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Draw the **ISA-5.1 P&ID and the block diagram** for a **cascade temperature-flow control** of a heat exchanger, where the **reactor outlet temperature** is the primary variable and the **steam flow** is the secondary variable. Clearly mark the tags. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step procedure to implement PID control on a process station** (e.g., a level-control pilot plant) using a DCS. State the meaning of **$K_c$, $T_i$, $T_d$** and the effect of increasing each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) ISA-5.1 P&ID and Cascade Block Diagram — see Section 4.4 Mermaid diagram above**

The cascade loop is drawn as follows:

- **TT-200** (RTD) on reactor outlet → signal → **TIC-200** (DCS square-bubble, primary PID).
- Output (MV) of **TIC-200** becomes **RSP of FIC-201** (DCS square-bubble, secondary PID).
- **FT-201** (orifice + DPT on steam line) → signal → **FIC-201**.
- Output of **FIC-201** → **FCV-201** (pneumatic control valve on steam line).
- Steam enters exchanger → heats reactor outlet.

**Tag Summary Table:**

| Tag | Variable / Function |
|---|---|
| TT-200 | Temperature Transmit (sensor) |
| TIC-200 | Temperature Indicating Controller (primary) |
| FT-201 | Flow Transmit (sensor) |
| FIC-201 | Flow Indicating Controller (secondary) |
| FCV-201 | Flow Control Valve |

**(b) Procedure for PID Implementation on a Process Station**

1. **Identify the loop components** — sensor, transmitter, controller, final control element.
2. **Wire the I/O** — connect sensor to AI card, valve to AO card via I/P.
3. **Configure the I/O in DCS** — assign channel, range, units, linearization (e.g., square-root for DP flow, Callendar–Van Dusen for RTD).
4. **Create a PID function block** — link its `PV` to the AI channel; its `CV` to the AO channel.
5. **Set initial tuning** — use **open-loop step test** (process reaction curve) to find $K_p, L, T$ and then apply **Cohen–Coon** or **Ziegler–Nichols** rules.
6. **Tune manually** — start with $K_c$ small, $T_i$ very large, $T_d = 0$; then increase $K_c$ until acceptable setpoint tracking and disturbance rejection.
7. **Switch to AUTO** — enable bumpless transfer, observe trend, fine-tune.
8. **Save and back up** the configuration.

**Meaning and Effect of PID Parameters:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Effect of Increase |
|---|---|---|
| **Proportional gain** | $K_c$ | Faster response, smaller steady-state error, but risk of oscillation if too high |
| **Integral time** | $T_i$ (min/repeat) | **Eliminating** steady-state error; smaller $T_i$ ⇒ stronger integral action ⇒ faster removal of offset but risk of instability |
| **Derivative time** | $T_d$ (min) | Adds damping, anticipates future error; helpful for slow loops (temperature); harmful for noisy signals (flow) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard directions for tuning in KTU labs:**
> 1. Set $T_i = \infty$ (turn off integral), $T_d = 0$ (turn off derivative).
> 2. Increase $K_c$ until the response just starts to oscillate (ultimate gain $K_u$).
> 3. Set $K_c = 0.5 K_u$ (Ziegler–Nichols closed-loop tuning rule for PI/PID).
> 4. Set $T_i = 0.8 T_u$ (where $T_u$ = ultimate period of oscillation).
> 5. If using PID, set $T_d = 0.125 T_u$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callouts

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes KTU Students Make in This Module:**
> 1. **Drawing field transmitters as DCS square-bubbles** — wrong; field = circle, DCS = circle-in-square.
> 2. **Mixing solid and dashed signal lines** without reason — the standard says solid arrow = continuous signal; dashed = optional.
> 3. **Forgetting to label the RSP arrow** in cascade drawings — examiners deduct 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing `I/P` and `P/I` converters** — `I/P` converts current (4–20 mA) to pressure (3–15 psi) for pneumatic valve; reverse for `P/I`.
> 5. **Writing "DCS is just a big PLC"** — DCS is process-control-oriented (continuous + batch), with integrated redundancy and central historian; PLC is discrete-logic-oriented.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **DCS = Distributed Control System** — distributed controllers + central HMI over a digital fieldbus.
- **Purdue Model levels** — 0 (Field), 1 (Process Station), 2 (Supervisory), 3 (MES), 4 (ERP).
- **Process Station** = controller + I/O cards (AI, AO, DI, DO) + scan engine.
- **ISA-5.1** = the **standard for symbols, letters, and tag numbering** in process instrumentation.
- **Instrument bubble shapes:** circle = field; circle-in-square = DCS; diamond = manual valve; hexagon = math block; square with bar = PLC.
- **Line types:** solid arrow = continuous signal; dashed = optional/interlock; cross-hatched = process pipe; bubble-line = capillary/EM.
- **Tag format:** `XX-NNN-SS` where XX = function letters, NNN = loop number, SS = optional suffix.
- **First letter rule:** the **first letter denotes the measured variable**, not the function.
- **Loop components:** sensor → transmitter → controller (DCS) → final control element → process.
- **4–20 mA scaling formula:** $E = E_{min} + \dfrac{R}{16}(I - 4)$, where $R = E_{max} - E_{min}$.
- **3–15 psi pneumatic signal** corresponds to 4–20 mA via I/P converter.
- **PID continuous form** uses $K_c$, $T_i$, $T_d$; **DCS implements the velocity (incremental) form** to enable bumpless transfer.
- **Cascade control:** primary PID's MV becomes the secondary PID's RSP; tune inner loop first, then outer.
- **Scan time rule of thumb:** $T_s \le 0.1 \tau_p$ where $\tau_p$ is the dominant process time constant.
- **Communication speeds:** FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1 = 31.25 kbps; HSE / Ethernet = 100 Mbps – 1 Gbps.
- **Tag decoding order:** Function letters left-to-right; first letter = variable; last letter = function or modifier; loop number is the **loop identity**, not the instrument count.
- **ISA-5.1 vs. SAMA:** SAMA is the **functional loop diagram** (rectangles with letters); ISA-5.1 is the **process & instrumentation diagram** (circles + diamonds + lines). They are complementary, not interchangeable.
- **HMI = Operator Station**; **Historian = time-series data server**; **Engineering Station = configuration PC**.
- **DCS ≠ SCADA** — DCS is control-centric and plant-wide; SCADA is supervisory and geographically wide.
- **Redundancy codes:** 1oo2, 2oo2, 2oo3 — these are voting logic codes for safety-critical loops.
- **Standard control strategies:** Single-loop PID, Cascade, Feedforward, Ratio, Split-Range, Override (Auto-Select), Auto–Manual station.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute 30-Second Recall Mnemonic:** "**Bubbles Tell Everything**" — circle = field, square = DCS, diamond = valve, hexagon = math. Memorize this and the letter table; you can decode any ISA diagram in any KTU exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control of RC circuit using LabVIEW
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL607 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Control of RC circuit using LabVIEW

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**RC Circuit Control using LabVIEW** is the laboratory implementation of closed-loop (or open-loop) dynamic control of a first-order electrical system — a **Resistor-Capacitor (R-C) network** — using the **Laboratory Virtual Instrument Engineering Workbench (LabVIEW)** graphical programming environment. In this experiment, the capacitor voltage (or circuit output) is treated as the **Process Variable (PV)**, and the input voltage to the circuit is the **Manipulated Variable (MV)**, regulated through a software-implemented controller (P, PI, PD, or PID) inside the LabVIEW Virtual Instrument (VI).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this experiment validates the following:
- Identification of a **first-order lag (FOPDT) system** from step-response data.
- Implementation of an **analog/digital control strategy** using virtual instrumentation.
- Validation of **time-domain specifications** — rise time, settling time, steady-state error, and overshoot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment demonstrates the equivalence between a real physical process (RC network) and a first-order transfer function, and shows how LabVIEW can replicate classical analog control actions digitally with high fidelity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **leaking water bucket** connected to a tap:
- The **tap** is the manipulated variable (input voltage) — how much water you pour in.
- The **bucket with a hole at the bottom** is the RC circuit — it fills up but also leaks out (capacitor charges and discharges through the resistor).
- The **water level in the bucket** is the output (capacitor voltage).
- Your **eye + brain + hand adjusting the tap** is the controller (P/PI/PID in LabVIEW).

If you set a desired water level (**Setpoint**) and keep watching the bucket (**feedback**), adjusting the tap accordingly, you are essentially doing what LabVIEW does with an RC circuit — **closed-loop feedback control**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A simple RC circuit is the *electrical twin* of a thermal system (heater + thermometer) or a liquid level system. Mastering its control in LabVIEW builds the foundation for controlling real industrial processes.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (Highlighted)

- **Time Constant (τ = RC):** The time required for the capacitor voltage to reach **63.2 %** of its final value. Unit: **seconds (s)**.
- **DC Gain (K):** For a passive RC low-pass filter, K = 1 (dimensionless).
- **Cut-off Frequency (f_c = 1 / (2πRC)):** The frequency at which output power drops to half. Unit: **Hertz (Hz)**.
- **Sampling Time (T_s):** The discrete time-step used in the LabVIEW While Loop. Typical value: **0.01 s to 0.1 s**.
- **Reference Setpoint (r(t)):** The desired capacitor voltage, in **Volts (V)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The RC Circuit as a First-Order System

A series RC circuit driven by an input voltage **V_in(t)** with output taken across the capacitor **V_c(t)** is governed by Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):

$$V_{in}(t) = i(t) \cdot R + V_c(t)$$

Since the current through the capacitor is $i(t) = C \cdot \dfrac{dV_c(t)}{dt}$, the governing differential equation is:

$$RC \cdot \frac{dV_c(t)}{dt} + V_c(t) = V_{in}(t)$$

This is a **linear, first-order, ordinary differential equation** with constant coefficients. Taking the Laplace Transform (assuming zero initial conditions):

$$RC \cdot s \cdot V_c(s) + V_c(s) = V_{in}(s)$$

Hence the **Transfer Function** of the RC low-pass circuit is:

$$G(s) = \frac{V_c(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{1}{RCs + 1} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$$

where the static gain **K = 1** and the time constant **τ = RC**.

## 2.2 Why an RC Circuit Models a First-Order Process

Every real industrial process that has a single energy-storage element behaves like an RC circuit:
- **Thermal process** (heater + metal block) → Thermal capacitance (C) and resistance (R) to ambient.
- **Liquid tank** (inflow + outflow) → Tank capacitance (Area) and flow resistance (valve).
- **Pneumatic system** (air tank + leak) → Pneumatic capacitance and resistance.

## 2.3 Control Strategies Implemented in LabVIEW

LabVIEW allows the user to build a **PID controller** in software using built-in VIs from the *Control & Simulation Palette*. The standard PID control law is:

$$u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

In the **digital (discrete) domain** suitable for LabVIEW:

$$u(k) = K_c \left[ e(k) + \frac{T_s}{T_i}\sum_{i=0}^{k} e(i) + \frac{T_d}{T_s}\bigl(e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr) \right]$$

| Term | Symbol | Unit | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Proportional Gain | $K_c$ | dimensionless | Immediate corrective action |
| Integral Time | $T_i$ | seconds (s) | Eliminates steady-state error |
| Derivative Time | $T_d$ | seconds (s) | Anticipates future error |
| Sampling Period | $T_s$ | seconds (s) | LabVIEW loop iteration time |
| Error Signal | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Volts (V) | Deviation from setpoint |
| Process Variable | $y(t) = V_c(t)$ | Volts (V) | Measured capacitor voltage |
| Manipulated Variable | $u(t) = V_{in}(t)$ | Volts (V) | Controller output |
| Time Constant | $\tau = RC$ | seconds (s) | Speed of circuit response |
| Steady-state Error | $e_{ss}$ | Volts (V) | Final error after transients die out |
| Rise Time | $t_r$ | seconds (s) | Time to go from 10 % to 90 % of final value |
| Settling Time | $t_s$ | seconds (s) | Time to enter and stay within ±2 % of final value |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Software-based PID control of an RC plant is the prototype for **digital temperature controllers, motor speed controllers, and DCS (Distributed Control System) loops** in modern process plants (oil refineries, pharmaceutical batch reactors, power plants).

## 2.4 Hardware-Software Architecture

The experiment typically uses:
- **PCI/PXI/USB Data Acquisition (DAQ) card** (e.g., NI-DAQmx supported device, NI ELVIS II+, or myDAQ).
- **Analog Input (AI) channel** — to sense the capacitor voltage (scaled to 0–10 V or ±10 V).
- **Analog Output (AO) channel** — to feed the controller output back into the RC circuit input.
- **LabVIEW Real-Time Module** or base LabVIEW with *PID and Fuzzy Logic Toolkit*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Transfer Function of the RC Circuit

**Given:** Series RC circuit with input $V_{in}(t)$ across the series combination, output $V_c(t)$ across the capacitor.

**Step 1 — Apply KVL around the loop.**
The sum of voltage drops equals the source voltage:

$$V_{in}(t) = V_R(t) + V_c(t)$$

**Step 2 — Express V_R in terms of current.**
Ohm's law for the resistor gives:

$$V_R(t) = R \cdot i(t)$$

**Step 3 — Express the capacitor current.**
The fundamental capacitor equation is:

$$i(t) = C \cdot \frac{dV_c(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and rearrange.**

$$V_{in}(t) = RC \cdot \frac{dV_c(t)}{dt} + V_c(t)$$

**Step 5 — Take the Laplace Transform (zero initial conditions).**
Using $\mathcal{L}\{\dfrac{dV_c}{dt}\} = sV_c(s)$ and $\mathcal{L}\{V_c(t)\} = V_c(s)$:

$$V_{in}(s) = RC \cdot s \cdot V_c(s) + V_c(s)$$

**Step 6 — Factor V_c(s) and isolate the ratio.**

$$V_{in}(s) = V_c(s) \cdot (RCs + 1)$$

$$\boxed{G(s) = \frac{V_c(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{1}{RCs + 1} = \frac{1}{\tau s + 1}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Unit Step Response

**Given:** A step input $V_{in}(t) = A \cdot u(t)$ (amplitude $A$ in Volts), with zero initial capacitor voltage.

**Step 1 — Laplace transform of the step input.**

$$V_{in}(s) = \frac{A}{s}$$

**Step 2 — Output in s-domain.**

$$V_c(s) = G(s) \cdot V_{in}(s) = \frac{1}{\tau s + 1} \cdot \frac{A}{s}$$

**Step 3 — Partial fraction expansion.**

$$V_c(s) = \frac{A}{s(\tau s + 1)} = \frac{A}{s} - \frac{A\tau}{\tau s + 1}$$

Rewriting the second term in standard form:

$$V_c(s) = \frac{A}{s} - \frac{A}{s + \frac{1}{\tau}}$$

**Step 4 — Inverse Laplace transform.**

$$\boxed{V_c(t) = A \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right) \quad \text{Volts}}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate at key time instants to validate the time constant definition.**
- At $t = 0$: $V_c(0) = A(1 - e^0) = 0$ (capacitor initially uncharged).
- At $t = \tau$: $V_c(\tau) = A(1 - e^{-1}) = 0.632 \, A$ → **63.2 % rule**.
- At $t = 5\tau$: $V_c(5\tau) = A(1 - e^{-5}) \approx 0.9933 \, A$ → effectively at steady state.
- As $t \to \infty$: $V_c(\infty) = A$ → **DC steady-state value**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Open-Loop Steady-State Error for a P-Controller

**Given:** A proportional-only controller with gain $K_c$ feeding the RC plant, and a step setpoint of magnitude $R$.

**Step 1 — Forward path transfer function with P-controller.**

$$G_{OL}(s) = K_c \cdot \frac{1}{\tau s + 1}$$

**Step 2 — Closed-loop transfer function (unity feedback).**

$$G_{CL}(s) = \frac{G_{OL}(s)}{1 + G_{OL}(s)} = \frac{K_c}{(\tau s + 1) + K_c} = \frac{K_c}{1 + K_c} \cdot \frac{1}{\frac{\tau}{1+K_c} s + 1}$$

**Step 3 — Final value via Final Value Theorem.**

$$y_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G_{CL}(s) \cdot \frac{R}{s} = \frac{K_c}{1 + K_c} \cdot R$$

**Step 4 — Steady-state error.**

$$e_{ss} = R - y_{ss} = R - \frac{K_c \, R}{1 + K_c} = \frac{R}{1 + K_c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A pure P-controller can *never* give zero steady-state error on a first-order plant. To eliminate the offset, one **must** add the integral action (i.e., use a PI or PID controller).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Tuning a PI Controller for Zero Steady-State Error

**Given:** Setpoint $R$, PI controller $G_c(s) = K_c \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s}\right)$, plant $G_p(s) = \dfrac{1}{\tau s + 1}$.

**Step 1 — Open-loop transfer function.**

$$G_{OL}(s) = K_c \left(1 + \frac{1}{T_i s}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\tau s + 1} = \frac{K_c(T_i s + 1)}{T_i s (\tau s + 1)}$$

**Step 2 — Closed-loop transfer function.**

$$G_{CL}(s) = \frac{K_c(T_i s + 1)}{T_i s (\tau s + 1) + K_c(T_i s + 1)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Final Value Theorem for a step setpoint.**

$$y_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G_{CL}(s) \cdot \frac{R}{s} = \frac{K_c \cdot 1}{0 + K_c \cdot 1} \cdot R = R$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**

$$\boxed{e_{ss} = R - y_{ss} = 0}$$

The integral action guarantees **zero steady-state error** for a step setpoint — this is the central reason PI/PID controllers are the workhorse of process control.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Controller Performance

| Controller Type | Rise Time $t_r$ | Overshoot \% | Settling Time $t_s$ | Steady-State Error $e_{ss}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| P-only | Fast (decreases with $K_c$) | Small to Moderate | Moderate | $\dfrac{R}{1+K_c} \neq 0$ |
| PI | Slightly slower | Moderate | Longer (integrator windup risk) | **0** |
| PD | Very fast | Increased | Shortest | $\dfrac{R}{1+K_c} \neq 0$ |
| PID | Fast | Moderate to High | Short (well-tuned) | **0** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Closed-Loop LabVIEW Control of RC Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint Reference r_k] --> SUMA[Summer Node]
    FBK[Feedback Signal y_k from AI Channel] --> SUMA
    SUMA --> ERR[Error e_k equals r_k minus y_k]
    ERR --> PID[LabVIEW PID VI Block]
    PIDG[Controller Gains Kc Ti Td] --> PID
    PID --> MVO[Manipulated Variable u_k]
    MVO --> AOC[Analog Output AO Channel]
    AOC --> RCPLANT[RC Circuit Plant Hardware]
    RCPLANT --> PV[Process Variable y_k equals Vc_t]
    PV --> AIC[Analog Input AI Channel]
    AIC --> FBK
    PV --> DISP[Front Panel Waveform Chart Display]
    ERR --> ERRLOG[Error Logging to TDMS File]
    PIDG --> TUNE[Manual or Ziegler Nichols Tuning Panel]
    TUNE --> PIDG
```

## 4.2 LabVIEW VI Architecture (Front Panel + Block Diagram Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FP[FRONT PANEL User Interface Layer]
        F1[Setpoint Numeric Control Volts]
        F2[Kc Proportional Gain Knob]
        F3[Ti Integral Time Slider seconds]
        F4[Td Derivative Time Slider seconds]
        F5[Waveform Chart for PV vs Time]
        F6[Waveform Chart for Error vs Time]
        F7[Waveform Chart for MV vs Time]
        F8[STOP Boolean Button]
    end

    subgraph BD[BLOCK DIAGRAM Functional Layer]
        B1[While Loop with Wait ms Timer]
        B2[DAQmx Read VI AI Channel]
        B3[Subtract Function e equals r minus y]
        B4[PID VI from Control Simulation Palette]
        B5[Signal Saturation Block 0 to 10 V]
        B6[DAQmx Write VI AO Channel]
        B7[Shift Register for Previous Error]
        B8[Build Waveform Function for Chart]
    end

    subgraph HW[PHYSICAL HARDWARE Layer]
        H1[NI DAQ Card USB or PCI]
        H2[RC Circuit Board R equals 10k C equals 100uF]
        H3[Power Supply plus minus 12 V]
    end

    F1 --> B3
    F2 --> B4
    F3 --> B4
    F4 --> B4
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> B6
    B6 --> H1
    H1 --> H2
    H2 --> H1
    H1 --> B2
    B4 --> B7
    B7 --> B4
    B6 --> B8
    B2 --> B8
    B8 --> F5
    B3 --> F6
    B5 --> F7
    F8 --> B1
    H3 --> H2
```

## 4.3 Open-Loop Step Response (Conceptual Time-Domain Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Time t equals 0] -->|Step Input of A Volts| B[Capacitor Charging Phase]
    B -->|t equals tau| C[63.2 percent of A reached]
    C -->|t equals 2 tau| D[86.5 percent of A]
    D -->|t equals 3 tau| E[95.0 percent of A]
    E -->|t equals 4 tau| F[98.2 percent of A]
    F -->|t equals 5 tau| G[99.3 percent of A steady state]
    G -->|t greater than 5 tau| H[Output remains at A Volts]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above flowchart represents the **time-domain charging profile** of a first-order RC system in response to a unit step input. It is the canonical curve students should reproduce on the LabVIEW waveform chart to confirm the plant identification step.

## 4.4 Data Flow Inside the LabVIEW While Loop (Sequential)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ST[Start Iteration k] --> RD[Step 1 DAQ Read AI Channel Vc_k]
    RD --> CMP[Step 2 Compute Error e_k equals SP minus Vc_k]
    CMP --> CALC[Step 3 PID Algorithm Compute u_k]
    CALC --> SAT[Step 4 Saturate u_k to 0 to 10 Volts]
    SAT --> WR[Step 5 DAQ Write AO Channel Vc_in]
    WR --> DSP[Step 6 Update Front Panel Charts]
    DSP --> WAT[Step 7 Wait T_s milliseconds]
    WAT --> CHK{Step 8 STOP Button Pressed}
    CHK -->|No| ST
    CHK -->|Yes| END[Exit While Loop and Close VIs]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define the transfer function of an RC circuit and identify the poles. Mention the significance of the time constant.*

**Model Answer:**
The transfer function of a series RC low-pass circuit (output across the capacitor) is given by:

$$G(s) = \frac{V_c(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{1}{RCs + 1} = \frac{1}{\tau s + 1}$$

where $\tau = RC$ is the **time constant** (in seconds) and the static gain $K = 1$.
The system has a **single real pole** at:

$$s = -\frac{1}{\tau} = -\frac{1}{RC}$$

The pole lies in the **left-half s-plane**, confirming that the RC circuit is a **stable, first-order, minimum-phase system** with no oscillation. The time constant $\tau$ quantifies the *speed of response*: a smaller $\tau$ (smaller R or C) gives a faster charging transient. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Why is an integral action essential in a controller for an RC circuit, even when a proportional controller reduces the error significantly?*

**Model Answer:**
A pure proportional (P) controller applied to a first-order RC plant yields a closed-loop transfer function:

$$G_{CL}(s) = \frac{K_c}{1 + K_c} \cdot \frac{1}{\frac{\tau}{1+K_c} s + 1}$$

Applying the Final Value Theorem for a step setpoint of magnitude $R$ gives the steady-state output $y_{ss} = \dfrac{K_c R}{1 + K_c}$ and a non-zero steady-state error $e_{ss} = \dfrac{R}{1 + K_c}$. Even if $K_c$ is large, the error reduces but **never becomes zero**.
Adding the integral term (PI or PID controller) introduces a pole at the origin in the controller transfer function, making the loop **Type-1**. The new $e_{ss} = 0$ for a step input. Hence the integral action is **essential to eliminate the offset** that a P-controller cannot remove. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** Derive the transfer function of a series RC circuit and obtain its unit step response. Show that the response reaches 63.2 % of the final value at $t = \tau$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat LabVIEW block-diagram, explain how a PID controller is implemented in software to control the output voltage of an RC circuit in real time. List any two precautions to be taken while tuning. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Step 1 — KVL on the series RC circuit:**

$$V_{in}(t) = R \cdot i(t) + V_c(t)$$

**Step 2 — Capacitor constitutive relation:**

$$i(t) = C \cdot \frac{dV_c(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Governing ODE:**

$$RC \cdot \frac{dV_c(t)}{dt} + V_c(t) = V_{in}(t)$$

**Step 4 — Laplace transform (zero IC):**

$$V_c(s) (RC s + 1) = V_{in}(s) \implies G(s) = \frac{1}{RC s + 1}$$

**Step 5 — Unit step input** $V_{in}(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$:

$$V_c(s) = \frac{1}{s(\tau s + 1)} = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{1}{s + 1/\tau}$$

**Step 6 — Inverse Laplace transform:**

$$V_c(t) = 1 - e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate at $t = \tau$:**

$$V_c(\tau) = 1 - e^{-1} = 1 - 0.3679 = 0.6321 \quad (\text{i.e., } 63.2 \%)$$

**Conclusion:** The transfer function is $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{\tau s + 1}$ and at one time constant the output reaches 63.2 % of the final value. **(7 marks — full derivation above)**

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**LabVIEW Implementation Steps (Block Diagram Flow):**

1. **Front Panel Design:**
   - Numeric control for Setpoint (Volts).
   - Knobs/sliders for $K_c$, $T_i$, $T_d$.
   - Three Waveform Charts: PV vs Time, Error vs Time, MV vs Time.
   - A STOP button.

2. **Block Diagram Construction (Inside a While Loop):**
   - Place **DAQmx Read VI** to acquire capacitor voltage $V_c(t)$ on AI channel.
   - Compute error $e(k) = r(k) - y(k)$ using a Subtract function.
   - Feed $e(k)$, $K_c$, $T_i$, $T_d$ into the **PID VI** (located in *Control Design & Simulation → PID*).
   - Apply a **Saturation block** to limit output between 0 V and 10 V (to protect DAQ hardware).
   - Send the saturated signal to **DAQmx Write VI** (AO channel) — this is the new $V_{in}(t)$.
   - Use a **Shift Register** to store previous error $e(k-1)$ for the derivative term.
   - Use **Build Waveform** and wire to charts.
   - Place a **Wait (ms)** function with the desired $T_s$ (e.g., 100 ms).

3. **Two Tuning Precautions:**
   - **Anti-windup:** Clamp the integrator when the actuator saturates; otherwise large overshoots occur.
   - **Derivative kick:** Filter the derivative term (low-pass) or compute derivative on PV only, not on error, to avoid sudden setpoint-induced spikes.
   - *(Acceptable: Start with $K_c$ small, $T_i$ large, $T_d = 0$; increase $K_c$ gradually. Use proper signal grounding to suppress 50 Hz noise.)*

**(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – July 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** For a step input of magnitude 5 V applied to an RC circuit with $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 100 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$, compute (i) the time constant, (ii) the time to reach 4 V, and (iii) the steady-state capacitor voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the open-loop and closed-loop step responses of the RC circuit with a P-controller having $K_c = 4$. Comment on the steady-state error. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Given:** $R = 10 \times 10^3 \, \Omega$, $C = 100 \times 10^{-6} \, F$, step amplitude $A = 5 \, V$.

**(i) Time constant:**

$$\tau = RC = (10 \times 10^3)(100 \times 10^{-6}) = 10^4 \times 10^{-4} = 1 \text{ s}$$

**(ii) Time to reach $V_c = 4 \, V$:**

Using the step response formula $V_c(t) = A(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$:

$$4 = 5(1 - e^{-t/1})$$

$$\frac{4}{5} = 1 - e^{-t}$$

$$e^{-t} = 0.2$$

$$t = -\ln(0.2) = 1.6094 \text{ s} \approx 1.61 \text{ s}$$

**(iii) Steady-state capacitor voltage:**

$$V_c(\infty) = A = 5 \text{ V}$$

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Open-loop response (no controller, $K_c = 0$):**

$$V_c(t) = 5(1 - e^{-t})$$

Steady-state value: $5 \, V$. Steady-state error vs a $5 \, V$ setpoint: $e_{ss} = 0$ (open-loop matches setpoint by design when input is the setpoint itself).

**Closed-loop response with P-controller $K_c = 4$:**

Closed-loop transfer function (from Section 3.3 with $R \to 5$, $\tau = 1$ s, $K_c = 4$):

$$G_{CL}(s) = \frac{4}{s + 5}$$

Time-domain response to a $5 \, V$ step setpoint:

$$y(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{ \frac{4}{s+5} \cdot \frac{5}{s} \right\} = 4 \left( 1 - e^{-5t} \right)$$

Steady-state value:

$$y_{ss} = 4 \, V$$

Steady-state error:

$$e_{ss} = 5 - 4 = 1 \, V$$

Using the formula derived in Section 3.3:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{R}{1 + K_c} = \frac{5}{1+4} = 1 \, V \quad \checkmark$$

**Comment:** The closed-loop system is **faster** (effective time constant $1/5 = 0.2$ s versus $1$ s open-loop) but it suffers a **steady-state offset of 1 V (20 %)**. To eliminate this offset, an integral action (PI/PID) must be added. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make — and where they lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting zero initial conditions** in the Laplace transform of the RC circuit — KVL should be applied to the *loop*, not the *capacitor alone*.
> 2. **Writing the transfer function as $\frac{1}{RCs+1}$ without identifying $\tau$ and $K$** — examiners expect the standard first-order form $K / (\tau s + 1)$ explicitly.
> 3. **Confusing the time constant of the plant with the controller's integral time $T_i$** — they are completely different parameters. $T_i$ is a *controller* tuning parameter, while $\tau = RC$ is a *plant* parameter.
> 4. **Stating "P-controller gives zero steady-state error"** — this is **false** for a step setpoint on a Type-0 plant like the RC circuit. Examiners deduct full marks.
> 5. **In LabVIEW block-diagram questions**, students often describe the Front Panel only and skip the While Loop, DAQmx Read/Write VIs, and Shift Register — these are **mandatory** elements.
> 6. **Not mentioning anti-windup** when describing PI/PID — this is a frequently asked 2-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The RC circuit is the **canonical first-order plant** with transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{\tau s + 1}$, where $\tau = RC$.
- The **unit step response** is $V_c(t) = A(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$, reaching 63.2 % of $A$ at $t = \tau$, 86.5 % at $2\tau$, 95 % at $3\tau$, and effectively steady at $5\tau$.
- The system has a **single real pole at $s = -1/\tau$** (strictly in the left-half s-plane → stable).
- LabVIEW implements digital PID using the equation $u(k) = K_c \left[ e(k) + \dfrac{T_s}{T_i}\sum e(i) + \dfrac{T_d}{T_s}(e(k) - e(k-1)) \right]$ inside a **While Loop** with a **Wait (ms)** timer.
- Hardware interface is via **DAQmx Read (AI)** and **DAQmx Write (AO)** VIs.
- **P-only** control always has a steady-state error $e_{ss} = R/(1+K_c)$ for a Type-0 plant.
- **PI/PID** control **eliminates the offset** for a step input because the integrator introduces a pole at the origin, making the loop Type-1.
- The derivative term $T_d$ improves transient response (faster, less overshoot) but should be **filtered** to avoid noise amplification.
- Always apply **anti-windup** logic to the integrator when the actuator saturates.
- Ziegler-Nichols (open-loop reaction curve or closed-loop ultimate-gain) is the standard tuning method taught in KTU 2024 Scheme.
- The Front Panel must include setpoint, controller gains, charts for PV/Error/MV, and a STOP button.
- The Block Diagram must include a **While Loop**, **DAQmx Read**, **DAQmx Write**, **Subtract (error)**, **PID VI**, **Shift Register**, and **Wait (ms)** functions.
- Bandwidth of the closed-loop system is limited to roughly $1/\tau_{CL}$ where $\tau_{CL} = \tau/(1+K_c)$ for a P-controller.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control of a process station using LabVIEW
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL607 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Control of a process station using LabVIEW

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**LabVIEW (Laboratory Virtual Instrument Engineering Workbench)** is a graphical programming environment developed by National Instruments (NI) that uses a dataflow model (G-language) instead of sequential text-based code. In the context of process control, LabVIEW is used to design **Virtual Instruments (VIs)** that emulate the functionality of physical hardware such as PID controllers, oscilloscopes, data loggers, and signal generators.

A **Process Station** in process control is a physical or simulated system comprising a **plant** (e.g., a tank, heater, motor, or pressure vessel) equipped with sensors (for measurement) and actuators (for control). When interfaced with LabVIEW through a **DAQ (Data Acquisition) card** or a serial/ethernet communication protocol, it forms a closed-loop control system in which the software VI acts as the digital controller.

**Control of a process station using LabVIEW** therefore refers to the implementation of real-time control algorithms (commonly PID) within a LabVIEW VI to manipulate the process variable (PV) of a physical plant such that it tracks a desired setpoint (SP) with minimal error, overshoot, and steady-state deviation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCICL607):** Students are expected to design, develop, and execute a LabVIEW-based control VI, demonstrate closed-loop response to step changes, record transient performance metrics (rise time, settling time, overshoot, steady-state error), and interpret the Bode/stability characteristics of the controlled process.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway and you want to maintain a constant speed of **80 km/h** (this is your *setpoint*). Your eyes observe the speedometer (the *sensor*), your brain compares the current speed with 80 km/h and decides to either press the accelerator harder or ease off (the *controller*), and the accelerator/brake pedals act on the engine (the *actuator*) which propels the car (the *plant*).

In this analogy:
- **The car + road conditions = Process Station (Plant)**
- **Speedometer = Sensor (transducer + signal conditioning)**
- **Your brain running a control rule = LabVIEW VI with a PID algorithm**
- **Accelerator/Brake = Actuator (heater, pump, valve, motor drive)**
- **Steady cruise at 80 km/h = Setpoint tracking with zero error**

LabVIEW replaces the "human brain" with a deterministic, real-time software loop executing tens to thousands of times per second, far more precise and tireless than a human. The **Front Panel** is the dashboard (knobs, graphs, indicators), and the **Block Diagram** is the wiring behind the dashboard showing how data flows from sensor → algorithm → actuator.

## 1.3 Key Physical / Engineering Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally evaluated by KTU examiners and practicing control engineers:

- **Setpoint (SP):** Desired target value of the process variable (units: °C, kPa, L/min, RPM).
- **Process Variable (PV):** Measured instantaneous value fed back to the controller.
- **Error Signal:** $e(t) = SP - PV$ (units same as PV).
- **Controller Output (CO) / Manipulated Variable (MV):** Signal sent to the actuator, typically **0–10 V, 4–20 mA, or PWM**.
- **Rise Time ($t_r$):** Time to go from 10% to 90% of final value.
- **Settling Time ($t_s$):** Time to remain within ±2% (or ±5%) of final value.
- **Overshoot ($M_p$):** Peak value above setpoint, expressed as a percentage.
- **Steady-State Error ($e_{ss}$):** Final persistent error after transients die out.
- **Sampling Period ($T_s$):** Time between two successive controller executions; for industrial process control, **$T_s$ = 0.1 s to 1 s** is typical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Tuning Rule (KTU-referenced):** Ziegler–Nichols is the most commonly tested tuning method. The ultimate gain $K_u$ and ultimate period $P_u$ are first found experimentally by increasing proportional gain until sustained oscillations occur, after which PID parameters are computed from a published table.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 LabVIEW Architecture: The Two Windows

Every LabVIEW program is called a **Virtual Instrument (VI)** and contains three primary components:

1. **Front Panel (FP):** The user interface. Contains **Controls** (inputs — knobs, switches, numeric inputs) and **Indicators** (outputs — graphs, LEDs, numeric displays, charts).
2. **Block Diagram (BD):** The source code. Contains terminals, functions, structures (While Loop, For Loop, Case Structure, Flat Sequence, Timed Loop), and wires that represent data flow.
3. **Connector Pane & Icon:** Allows a VI to be used as a sub-VI (analogous to a function call in text-based languages).

**Data Flow Programming Principle:** A node executes only when **all** its input terminals have received data. This is fundamentally different from C/Python where execution is sequential. Data flows along wires, not along lines of code.

## 2.2 Components of a Process Station

A typical KTU-lab process station (e.g., the **Feedback PC-Controlled Heat Exchanger, Process Station 37-100, or a coupled-tank rig**) consists of:

- **Plant:** Tank, heater, or motor whose dynamics are described by a first-order or second-order transfer function $G_p(s)$.
- **Sensor:** RTD/PT-100 (temperature), differential pressure transducer (level), tachogenerator (speed), strain gauge (force).
- **Signal Conditioning:** Amplifier, filter, cold-junction compensation, linearization.
- **Actuator:** Heater coil, control valve, variable-frequency drive (VFD), pneumatic positioner.
- **Interface Hardware:** NI DAQ card (e.g., **NI USB-6008/6009, NI PCI-6221**) or **RS-232/485/USB** link to PLC/microcontroller.
- **Software Controller:** The LabVIEW VI containing the PID algorithm.

## 2.3 PID Control Theory in Continuous and Discrete Forms

The **Proportional–Integral–Derivative (PID) controller** is the workhorse of industrial process control. Its continuous-time output is:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

where:
- $K_p$ — Proportional gain (dimensionless or in controller-output units per error unit)
- $K_i = K_p / T_i$ — Integral gain
- $K_d = K_p \cdot T_d$ — Derivative gain
- $T_i$ — Integral time (reset time), in **seconds**
- $T_d$ — Derivative time (rate time), in **seconds**

For **digital implementation in LabVIEW**, the integral is approximated by a rectangular sum and the derivative by a backward difference, yielding the **velocity (incremental) form**:

$$u[k] = u[k-1] + K_p\bigl(e[k] - e[k-1]\bigr) + K_i \, T_s \, e[k] + \frac{K_d}{T_s}\bigl(e[k] - 2e[k-1] + e[k-2]\bigr)$$

This form is preferred because it eliminates **integral windup** issues through the use of conditional integration (anti-windup via clamping or back-calculation).

## 2.4 First-Order Plus Dead Time (FOPDT) Plant Model

Most process stations used in KTU labs are well-modelled by a **First-Order Plus Dead Time (FOPDT)** transfer function, identified via an open-loop step test:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K \, e^{-L s}}{\tau s + 1}$$

where $K$ = process gain, $\tau$ = time constant, $L$ = dead time (transport delay).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Description | Units / Range |
|---|-------------------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Error Signal | $e(t) = SP - PV$ | Same as PV |
| 2 | Continuous PID Output | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot e$ | 0–100% or 4–20 mA |
| 3 | Discrete PID (Position Form) | $u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i T_s \sum e[j] + \dfrac{K_d}{T_s}(e[k]-e[k-1])$ | — |
| 4 | Discrete PID (Velocity Form) | $\Delta u[k] = K_p(e[k]-e[k-1]) + K_i T_s e[k] + \dfrac{K_d}{T_s}(e[k]-2e[k-1]+e[k-2])$ | — |
| 5 | FOPDT Plant Model | $G_p(s) = K e^{-Ls}/(\tau s+1)$ | $K$: gain, $\tau$: s, $L$: s |
| 6 | Closed-Loop Transfer Function | $T(s) = \dfrac{C(s) G_p(s)}{1 + C(s) G_p(s) H(s)}$ | — |
| 7 | Ziegler–Nichols P-only | $K_p = 0.5 K_u$ | — |
| 8 | Ziegler–Nichols PI | $K_p = 0.45 K_u, \; T_i = P_u/1.2$ | $T_i$ in s |
| 9 | Ziegler–Nichols PID | $K_p = 0.60 K_u, \; T_i = 0.5 P_u, \; T_d = 0.125 P_u$ | $T_i, T_d$ in s |
| 10 | Cohen–Coon PID | $K_p = \dfrac{1.35}{K}\bigl(\tau/L + 0.25\bigr)$ | — |
| 11 | ITAE Index (goodness of tuning) | $J = \int_0^\infty t \vert e(t) \vert \, dt$ | Minimise for optimum |
| 12 | Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist) | Hz |
| 13 | Anti-aliasing Filter | $f_c < f_s/2$ | Hz |
| 14 | DAQ Resolution | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n} - 1}$ | Volts / LSB |
| 15 | Steady-state error (step, P-only) | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{A}{1+K_p K}$ | Same as PV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-aliasing Note for KTU:** The sampling frequency $f_s$ must be at least twice the highest frequency component of the signal (Nyquist criterion). In LabVIEW, this is enforced by placing a low-pass filter before the ADC and choosing $T_s$ small enough (typically $T_s \le 0.1 \tau$).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

PID-based LabVIEW control of process stations is used extensively in:
- **Petrochemical refineries:** Temperature, flow, and pressure control of distillation columns.
- **Pharmaceutical batch reactors:** Precise temperature ramps for reaction kinetics.
- **Power plants:** Boiler drum level and steam temperature control.
- **Water treatment plants:** pH neutralisation, chlorine dosing, dissolved oxygen control.
- **Automotive test rigs:** Engine dynamometer speed/load control.
- **HVAC and Building Automation:** Chilled water temperature, zone pressure.

The advantages of using LabVIEW over a proprietary digital controller include: **rapid prototyping, graphical visualization, easy data logging, network connectivity, and the ability to implement advanced algorithms (fuzzy, model-predictive, adaptive) without changing hardware.**

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Discrete PID (Velocity Form) from Continuous PID

**Starting point — Continuous PID:**

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 1 — Discretize the integral using the rectangular (backward-Euler) rule:**

The integral is approximated by a cumulative sum of error samples multiplied by the sampling period $T_s$:

$$\int_0^{kT_s} e(\tau)\, d\tau \;\approx\; T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j]$$

**Step 2 — Discretize the derivative using the backward difference:**

The first derivative of a discrete signal at sample $k$ is approximated by the change over one interval:

$$\frac{de(t)}{dt}\bigg|_{t=kT_s} \;\approx\; \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{T_s}$$

**Step 3 — Assemble the discrete (position-form) PID equation:**

$$u[k] = K_p \, e[k] + K_i \, T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j] + K_d \, \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{T_s}$$

This is the **absolute (position) form**. Each time the controller fires, it recomputes the *total* control output from the running integral.

**Step 4 — Convert to velocity (incremental) form by subtracting consecutive outputs:**

$$u[k] - u[k-1] = K_p \bigl(e[k] - e[k-1]\bigr) + K_i \, T_s \, e[k] + \frac{K_d}{T_s}\bigl(e[k] - 2e[k-1] + e[k-2]\bigr)$$

**Why velocity form?** It eliminates the cumulative sum (saves memory), naturally provides **bumpless transfer** when the operator switches between manual and auto modes, and simplifies **anti-windup** (we just clamp $\Delta u$ and the integral cannot runaway).

## 3.2 Closed-Loop Transfer Function Derivation

Consider a unity-feedback system with controller $C(s)$ and plant $G_p(s)$. The forward path is $L(s) = C(s) G_p(s)$. The closed-loop transfer function from setpoint $R(s)$ to output $C(s)$ is obtained from the block-diagram reduction rule:

$$T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{L(s)}{1 + L(s)} = \frac{C(s)\, G_p(s)}{1 + C(s)\, G_p(s)}$$

**Step-by-step for a P-only controller and first-order plant:**

Let $C(s) = K_p$ and $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$.

Substitute:

$$T(s) = \frac{K_p \cdot \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}}{1 + K_p \cdot \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $(\tau s + 1)$:

$$T(s) = \frac{K_p K}{\tau s + 1 + K_p K} = \frac{K_p K / (1 + K_p K)}{\bigl[\tau / (1 + K_p K)\bigr] s + 1}$$

This is still first-order with **reduced effective time constant** $\tau_{cl} = \dfrac{\tau}{1+K_p K}$ and **reduced steady-state gain** $\dfrac{K_p K}{1+K_p K}$. As $K_p$ increases, the system becomes faster but the steady-state error to a step input approaches a non-zero limit — the classical reason for adding the **integral** term.

## 3.3 Ziegler–Nichols Ultimate-Cycle Tuning Derivation

**Step 1:** Set the controller to **P-only** (i.e., $K_i = 0, K_d = 0$).

**Step 2:** With the loop closed, slowly increase $K_p$ from zero until the process variable exhibits **sustained, constant-amplitude oscillations** in response to a small setpoint perturbation. Record the gain at which this first occurs: $K_u$ (ultimate gain). Record the period of oscillation: $P_u$ (ultimate period).

**Step 3:** Apply the Z–N recommended values:

| Controller | $K_p$ | $T_i$ | $T_d$ |
|------------|-------|-------|-------|
| P | $0.5 K_u$ | — | — |
| PI | $0.45 K_u$ | $P_u / 1.2$ | — |
| PID | $0.60 K_u$ | $0.5 P_u$ | $0.125 P_u$ |

**Step 4:** Convert to actual $K_i$ and $K_d$ for LabVIEW:

$$K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i}, \qquad K_d = K_p \cdot T_d$$

## 3.4 Open-Loop Step-Test Identification (FOPDT)

For the FOPDT model $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K e^{-Ls}}{\tau s + 1}$:

1. Apply a step change $\Delta u$ in controller output (with loop in **manual**).
2. Record the PV response; identify the **initial slope** $R = \dfrac{dPV}{dt}\big|_{t=0}$.
3. Steady-state PV change: $\Delta PV_{ss}$.
4. Process gain: $K = \dfrac{\Delta PV_{ss}}{\Delta u}$.
5. Draw a tangent at the inflection point; the time at which it crosses the initial value is the **dead time** $L$.
6. The time at which it crosses $\Delta PV_{ss}$ is $L + \tau$, hence $\tau = (L + \tau)_{tangent} - L$.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: LabVIEW Implementation of a PID Loop

| LabVIEW Block | Mathematical Function | Purpose |
|---------------|-----------------------|---------|
| DAQ Assistant / `AI Read` | Read analog input voltage | Acquire sensor signal $PV$ |
| Subtract block | $e = SP - PV$ | Compute error |
| `PID.vi` from Control Toolkit | Implements $u[k] = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dot e$ | Compute control output |
| Conditional Integration (inside `PID.vi`) | Stop integrating if $u$ saturates | Anti-windup |
| `AO Write` | Write $u$ to analog output | Drive actuator |
| Waveform Chart | Plot $PV$, $SP$, $u$ vs time | Real-time visualization |
| Write to Spreadsheet / TDMS | Save data to file | Post-experiment analysis |
| While Loop with Wait (ms) | Set $T_s$ | Establish sampling period |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A KTU lab process station has an FOPDT model with $K = 2.0$ °C/%, $\tau = 30$ s, $L = 5$ s. The controller output is 0–100% and PV is temperature in °C. Design a PID controller using Cohen–Coon tuning for a setpoint of 75 °C and compute the steady-state error expected.

**Step 1 — Cohen–Coon formulas:**

$$K_p = \frac{1}{K}\left(\frac{\tau}{L}\right)\left(1 + \frac{L}{3\tau}\right) = \frac{1}{2.0}\left(\frac{30}{5}\right)\left(1 + \frac{5}{90}\right)$$

$$K_p = 0.5 \times 6 \times 1.0556 = 3.167 \, \%/°C$$

**Step 2 — Integral time:**

$$T_i = L \cdot \frac{30 + 3(L/\tau)}{9 + 20(L/\tau)} = 5 \cdot \frac{30 + 3(5/30)}{9 + 20(5/30)} = 5 \cdot \frac{30 + 0.5}{9 + 3.333}$$

$$T_i = 5 \cdot \frac{30.5}{12.333} = 5 \cdot 2.473 = 12.36 \, s$$

**Step 3 — Derivative time:**

$$T_d = L \cdot \frac{4}{11 + 2(L/\tau)} = 5 \cdot \frac{4}{11 + 2(5/30)} = 5 \cdot \frac{4}{11.333} = 1.765 \, s$$

**Step 4 — Compute $K_i$ and $K_d$:**

$$K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{3.167}{12.36} = 0.2563 \, \%/(°C \cdot s)$$

$$K_d = K_p \cdot T_d = 3.167 \times 1.765 = 5.589 \, \% \cdot s / °C$$

**Step 5 — Steady-state error (for a step setpoint change to 75 °C with integral action present):**

Because the controller includes an **integral** term, the closed-loop system is **Type 1**, and the steady-state error to a step input is exactly:

$$e_{ss} = 0 \, °C$$

This is one of the principal reasons integral action is included in nearly every industrial PID controller.

# SECTION 4 (Cont. — Step-by-Step Numerical Worked Examples)

## 4.1 Worked Example: Ziegler–Nichols Tuning from Ultimate Cycle Data

**Problem:** During the ultimate-cycle test on a KTU temperature process station, the technician observed sustained oscillations at a proportional gain of $K_u = 8.0$ and an oscillation period of $P_u = 24$ s. Compute the recommended Z–N PID parameters and predict the expected overshoot for a step setpoint change.

**Step 1 — Z–N PID settings:**

$$K_p = 0.60 \, K_u = 0.60 \times 8.0 = 4.8$$

$$T_i = 0.5 \, P_u = 0.5 \times 24 = 12 \, s$$

$$T_d = 0.125 \, P_u = 0.125 \times 24 = 3.0 \, s$$

**Step 2 — Convert to discrete gains (assume $T_s = 0.5$ s):**

$$K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{4.8}{12} = 0.40 \, s^{-1}$$

$$K_d = K_p \cdot T_d = 4.8 \times 3.0 = 14.4 \, s$$

**Step 3 — Predict overshoot using empirical Z–N rule of thumb:**

Z–N tuning typically produces **20%–30% overshoot** for a unit step input on a second-order-dominant process. The expected range is therefore:

$$M_p \approx 25\% \pm 5\%$$

If the overshoot is unacceptable, the integral time $T_i$ is increased by a factor of 1.5–2.0 to reduce it.

## 4.2 Worked Example: Steady-State Error of P-Only Control

**Problem:** A KTU level-control rig has $K = 0.5$ cm/% (i.e., 1% valve opening produces 0.5 cm level rise at steady state). With $K_p = 4$ and a setpoint step of 10 cm, what is the steady-state error?

**Step 1 — Position the closed-loop gain:**

For a Type-0 plant (no integrator) and P-only controller, the steady-state error to a step of magnitude $A$ is:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{A}{1 + K_p K}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$e_{ss} = \frac{10}{1 + 4 \times 0.5} = \frac{10}{1 + 2} = \frac{10}{3} \approx 3.33 \, cm$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

The PV will settle at $10 - 3.33 = 6.67$ cm, which is **33% below the setpoint**. This non-zero offset is the *raison d'être* for the integral term — adding $K_i$ forces $e_{ss} \to 0$ for a step input.

## 4.3 Worked Example: Sampling Period Selection

**Problem:** A temperature process has dominant time constant $\tau = 30$ s. Choose a sampling period $T_s$ that gives a resolution of approximately 1% of the time constant.

**Step 1 — Empirical rule of thumb:**

For first-order-dominated processes:

$$T_s \approx \frac{\tau}{10} \text{ to } \frac{\tau}{20}$$

**Step 2 — Compute:**

$$T_s = \frac{30}{10} = 3.0 \, s \quad \text{(lower bound, faster response)}$$

$$T_s = \frac{30}{20} = 1.5 \, s \quad \text{(upper bound, more accurate tracking)}$$

A safe choice: $T_s = 1.0$ s, giving $f_s = 1.0$ Hz — easily handled by a USB-DAQ card and a typical LabVIEW timed loop.

**Step 3 — Anti-aliasing filter cutoff:**

Set $f_c = 0.4 \, f_s = 0.4$ Hz (4-pole Butterworth recommended). This is configured in the DAQ Assistant's input channel settings in LabVIEW.

## 4.4 Analytical Matrix — Common LabVIEW PID Tuning Mistakes

| # | Mistake | Symptom in LabVIEW Trace | Corrective Action |
|---|---------|-------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $K_p$ too high | Large overshoot, sustained oscillation | Reduce $K_p$ by 30% |
| 2 | $K_i$ too high | Slow oscillation, integrator windup | Increase $T_i$ or enable anti-windup clamp |
| 3 | $K_d$ too high | Noisy $u(t)$, chattering actuator | Increase derivative filter time; reduce $K_d$ |
| 4 | $T_s$ too large | Phase lag, sluggish response | Decrease $T_s$ (subject to sensor bandwidth) |
| 5 | Sensor not calibrated | Persistent offset even with integral action | Re-calibrate using 2-point (zero + span) method |
| 6 | Actuator saturation ignored | PV cannot reach SP, integral keeps winding | Enable output clamping & conditional integration |
| 7 | Derivative kick on SP change | Sudden $u$ spike when SP changes | Compute derivative on PV only, not on error |
| 8 | Wrong units for $K_p$ | No control action or runaway | Verify that $K_p$ uses consistent PV/MV units |

## 5.1 LabVIEW VI Architecture for Process Control

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the logical architecture of a LabVIEW VI used to control a process station. It mirrors the **Front Panel + Block Diagram** paradigm and shows the closed-loop interaction with the physical process.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FP["FRONT PANEL  User Interface"]
        SP_Knob["Numeric Control: Setpoint SP"]
        Kp_Slider["Knob: Proportional Gain Kp"]
        Ki_Slider["Knob: Integral Gain Ki"]
        Kd_Slider["Knob: Derivative Gain Kd"]
        Ts_Dial["Numeric Input: Sampling Period Ts"]
        PV_Chart["Waveform Chart: PV vs Time"]
        SP_Chart["Waveform Chart: SP vs Time"]
        U_Chart["Waveform Chart: u vs Time"]
        StopBtn["Boolean: STOP"]
    end

    subgraph BD["BLOCK DIAGRAM  Data Flow Code"]
        WhileLoop["WHILE LOOP  Iteration Terminal i"]
        Wait["Wait ms  Ts"]
        DAQRead["DAQ Assistant AI Read  PV voltage from sensor"]
        ScalePV["Scaling & Linearization  V to Engineering Units"]
        ErrorCalc["Subtract Block  e = SP - PV"]
        PIDAlgo["PID Algorithm Sub-VI  P + I + D terms + Anti-windup"]
        SatClamp["Saturation Clamp  0 to 100 percent"]
        DAQWrite["DAQ Assistant AO Write  u to actuator"]
        Logger["Write to Measurement File  TDMS format"]

    end

    end
```
