## 5.3 LabVIEW Data Flow Execution Sequence (Inside the Timed Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Loop Start  i = 0"] --> B["Read Analog Input  Get PV from sensor"]
    B --> C["Scale and Linearize  Convert V to engineering units"]
    C --> D["Compute Error  e = SP - PV"]
    D --> E["PID Computation  P term + I term + D term"]
    E --> F["Anti-windup Check  Clamp u between 0 and 100 percent"]
    F --> G["Write Analog Output  Send u to actuator"]
    G --> H["Log Data  Write PV, SP, u, time to file"]
    H --> I["Update Front Panel  Refresh charts and indicators"]
    I --> J{"STOP button pressed or i greater than N"}
    J -->|"No"| K["Wait Ts milliseconds"]
    K --> B
    J -->|"Yes"| L["Close DAQ and Exit Loop"]
```

## 5.4 PID Anti-Windup Conditional Integration Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Compute PID Output u_pid"] --> B{"Is u_pid greater than 100 percent OR Is u_pid less than 0 percent?"}
    B -->|"Yes, Saturated"| C["Set u_out to Clamped Value  u_out = 0 or 100"]
    C --> D["Disable Integration  Do not update integral term"]
    D --> E["Hold integral accumulator constant"]
    E --> F["Return u_out as controller output"]
    B -->|"No, Not Saturated"| G["Update Integral  I_new = I_old + Ki Ts e"]
    G --> H["Set u_out = u_pid"]
    H --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Viva:** When asked "Draw the block diagram of your LabVIEW process control experiment," reproduce the **closed-loop PID functional diagram (Section 5.2)**. When asked "Explain the LabVIEW implementation," use the **VI architecture diagram (Section 5.1)**. Examiner will reward clarity of data-flow direction.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 6: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 6.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`
**Define a Virtual Instrument (VI) in LabVIEW. List the two main windows of a LabVIEW VI and state the purpose of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Virtual Instrument (VI)** is a LabVIEW program that emulates the function of a physical instrument (e.g., oscilloscope, function generator, PID controller) using software on a general-purpose computer equipped with DAQ hardware.

The two main windows are:

1. **Front Panel** — The user interface containing **Controls** (inputs such as knobs, switches, numeric inputs) and **Indicators** (outputs such as graphs, charts, LEDs, numeric displays). It is the "dashboard" of the virtual instrument.

2. **Block Diagram** — The graphical source-code window containing terminals, functions, structures (While, For, Case, Flat Sequence, Timed Loop), and wires that implement the data-flow logic. A node executes only when all its inputs are available; data flows along wires.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark each for the definition, Front Panel description, and Block Diagram description. Partial credit for mentioning controls/indicators or data flow.)*

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**Explain the function of a DAQ card in a LabVIEW-based process control experiment. Mention any two specifications that affect measurement accuracy.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Data Acquisition (DAQ) card** is the hardware interface between the LabVIEW software running on the PC and the physical process station. It performs three functions:
1. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC):** Converts the analog sensor voltage into a digital number readable by LabVIEW.
2. **Digital-to-Analog Conversion (DAC):** Converts the LabVIEW-computed controller output into an analog voltage or current (0–10 V or 4–20 mA) to drive the actuator.
3. **Digital I/O and Timing:** Provides clocked sampling, trigger lines, and digital control signals.

Two key specifications affecting measurement accuracy:
- **Resolution (number of bits, $n$):** Determines the smallest detectable voltage change $\Delta V = V_{FS}/(2^n - 1)$. A 16-bit DAQ with $V_{FS} = 10$ V gives $\Delta V \approx 0.153$ mV.
- **Sampling Rate / Bandwidth:** Must exceed twice the highest signal frequency (Nyquist criterion) to avoid aliasing.

*(Other valid specs: input range, common-mode rejection ratio, settling time, channel count.)*

---

## 6.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PCICL607]`

**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the closed-loop PID control of a process station using LabVIEW. Clearly identify the role of the **Front Panel**, **Block Diagram**, **DAQ card**, **sensor**, **actuator**, and **plant**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **discrete-time velocity-form PID equation** from the continuous-time PID equation. Explain why the velocity form is preferred over the position form in industrial digital controllers. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Block diagram & explanation (7 marks):**

- (1 mark) Draw the closed-loop block diagram: SP → summing junction → PID controller → actuator → plant → PV, with PV fed back through sensor to the summing junction.
- (2 marks) Explain the role of each LabVIEW element:
  - **Front Panel:** Houses the SP numeric control, $K_p, K_i, K_d$ knobs, the real-time Waveform Charts of PV/SP/$u$, and the STOP button.
  - **Block Diagram:** Implements the **Timed While Loop** with iteration terminal, `DAQ Assistant` VIs for AI/AO, scaling sub-VI, error calculation, `PID.vi` from the Control & Simulation Toolkit, output clamp, and file-logging sub-VI.
  - **DAQ Card:** Performs ADC of sensor signal and DAC of controller output to the actuator. Example: NI USB-6009 (14-bit, 48 kS/s).
  - **Sensor:** PT-100/RTD for temperature, differential pressure transducer for level, tachogenerator for speed.
  - **Actuator:** Heater, pneumatic valve, VFD-driven motor.
  - **Plant:** Tank/heat-exchanger/coupled-tank whose dynamics are first/second order.
- (2 marks) Sampling & timing: Explain that the **Wait (ms)** block sets $T_s$, and the loop runs at $1/T_s$ Hz.
- (2 marks) Describe one complete iteration: read PV → scale → compute $e$ → PID → clamp → write $u$ → log → update charts.

**(b) — Discrete velocity PID derivation (7 marks):**

- (1 mark) Start with continuous PID: $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$.
- (2 marks) Discretize integral: $\int \approx T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j]$ and derivative: $\dot e \approx (e[k]-e[k-1])/T_s$.
- (1 mark) Write position-form: $u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i T_s \sum e[j] + K_d (e[k]-e[k-1])/T_s$.
- (1 mark) Subtract $u[k-1]$ from $u[k]$ to obtain velocity form:
  $$\Delta u[k] = K_p (e[k]-e[k-1]) + K_i T_s e[k] + \frac{K_d}{T_s}(e[k] - 2e[k-1] + e[k-2])$$
- (1 mark) Recover absolute output: $u[k] = u[k-1] + \Delta u[k]$.
- (1 mark) State three advantages of velocity form: (i) bumpless transfer between manual/auto, (ii) natural anti-windup, (iii) no need to store full integral history.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PCICL607]`

**(a)** A temperature process station is identified to have an FOPDT model with $K = 2.5$ °C/%, $\tau = 40$ s, and $L = 4$ s. Calculate the **Ziegler–Nichols PID** parameters and the corresponding $K_i, K_d$ for a sampling period of $T_s = 1$ s. Show all steps. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During the open-loop step test on a KTU level-control process, the controller output was stepped from 30% to 50% and the level rose from 12.0 cm to a final value of 17.0 cm with an apparent time constant of 35 s and a dead time of 6 s. **(i)** Identify the FOPDT parameters $K, \tau, L$. **(ii)** Compute the **Cohen–Coon PI** parameters and predict the **expected steady-state error** for a 5 cm setpoint step change. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Z–N PID from FOPDT parameters (7 marks):**

The Z–N open-loop formulas (using process reaction curve) are:

$$K_p = \frac{1.2 \, \tau}{K \, L}, \quad T_i = 2 L, \quad T_d = 0.5 L$$

- (1 mark) Substitute: $K_p = \dfrac{1.2 \times 40}{2.5 \times 4} = \dfrac{48}{10} = 4.8$ %/°C.
- (1 mark) $T_i = 2 \times 4 = 8$ s.
- (1 mark) $T_d = 0.5 \times 4 = 2$ s.
- (1 mark) Compute $K_i = K_p / T_i = 4.8 / 8 = 0.6$ %/(°C·s).
- (1 mark) Compute $K_d = K_p \times T_d = 4.8 \times 2 = 9.6$ %·s/°C.
- (1 mark) Note that $T_s = 1$ s is well below $\tau/10 = 4$ s — sampling rate is appropriate. Mention anti-windup and derivative-on-PV implementation in the LabVIEW `PID.vi`.
- (1 mark) State expected closed-loop behaviour: rise time ≈ 12 s, overshoot ≈ 20–25%, settling time ≈ 40–50 s.

**(b) — Cohen–Coon PI from step test (7 marks):**

- (1 mark) Process gain: $K = \Delta PV / \Delta u = (17.0 - 12.0) / (50 - 30) = 5.0 / 20 = 0.25$ cm/%.
- (1 mark) Time constant: $\tau = 35$ s; Dead time: $L = 6$ s.
- (2 marks) Cohen–Coon PI formulas:
  $$K_p = \frac{1}{K}\left(\frac{\tau}{L}\right)\left(0.9 + \frac{L}{12\tau}\right) = \frac{1}{0.25}\left(\frac{35}{6}\right)\left(0.9 + \frac{6}{420}\right)$$
  $$K_p = 4 \times 5.833 \times 0.9143 = 21.33 \, \%/\text{cm}$$
- (1 mark) Integral time: $T_i = L \cdot \dfrac{30 + 3(L/\tau)}{9 + 20(L/\tau)} = 6 \cdot \dfrac{30 + 3(6/35)}{9 + 20(6/35)} = 6 \cdot \dfrac{30.514}{12.429} = 14.73$ s.
- (1 mark) $K_i = K_p / T_i = 21.33 / 14.73 = 1.448 \, \%/(\text{cm}\cdot\text{s})$.
- (1 mark) Steady-state error: Because the controller includes integral action (PI = Type 1 system), the steady-state error to a step setpoint change of $A = 5$ cm is exactly:
  $$e_{ss} = 0 \, \text{cm}$$

---

## 6.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PCICL607 Lab Viva & Records:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units on $K_p$:** Always write $K_p$ with units, e.g., $K_p = 4.8$ %/°C. A unitless number loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing $K_i$ and $T_i$:** Many students write $K_i = T_i$ — wrong. $K_i = K_p/T_i$ (proportional to inverse of integral time).
> 3. **Sampling period choice:** Choosing $T_s$ equal to or greater than $\tau$ causes the system to behave erratically. Use $T_s \le \tau/10$.
> 4. **No anti-windup:** If the actuator saturates, the integrator continues to accumulate — the PV cannot reach SP, but $u$ is held at the saturation value. Always enable **conditional integration** in the `PID.vi` output range terminals.
> 5. **Derivative on error vs PV:** Taking the derivative of error causes a "derivative kick" when SP is changed abruptly. Compute derivative on PV only: $\dot e = -\dot{PV}$ (for $SP$ constant).
> 6. **Forgetting to ground/shield the DAQ card:** Sensor noise coupled into the analog input causes a noisy PV trace and poor control. Use shielded twisted-pair cables and connect shield ground at one end only.
> 7. **Not recording transient metrics:** KTU lab report must include numerical values of $t_r, t_s, M_p, e_{ss}$ extracted from the saved waveform. A graph without numbers gets partial credit only.

---

## 6.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LabVIEW = Graphical Programming** using **data flow**; a node executes when all its inputs are ready.
- A **VI** has two windows: **Front Panel (UI)** and **Block Diagram (code)**; can be reused as a **sub-VI**.
- **DAQ card** is the bridge between LabVIEW and the physical world; it performs **ADC** (analog in → digital) and **DAC** (digital → analog out).
- A **process station** = **plant + sensor + actuator + interface hardware + software controller (LabVIEW VI)**.
- **Closed-loop control** requires **feedback**: PV is measured, compared to SP, and the **error** drives a **PID controller** that adjusts the actuator.
- **Continuous PID:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot e$.
- **Discrete velocity-form PID** is preferred in digital implementation: $\Delta u[k] = K_p(e[k]-e[k-1]) + K_i T_s e[k] + (K_d/T_s)(e[k] - 2e[k-1] + e[k-2])$.
- **FOPDT model** $G_p(s) = K e^{-Ls}/(\tau s+1)$ is identified by an **open-loop step test**.
- **Ziegler–Nichols** (ultimate cycle / reaction curve) and **Cohen–Coon** are the most-tested tuning methods.
- **Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$; rule of thumb $T_s \le \tau/10$.
- **Anti-windup** is essential when actuator saturates — use **conditional integration** or **back-calculation**.
- **Derivative kick** is avoided by differentiating **PV only**, not error.
- **Performance metrics to report:** rise time $t_r$, settling time $t_s$, overshoot $M_p$, steady-state error $e_{ss}$, ITAE index.
- **DAQ Resolution:** $\Delta V = V_{FS}/(2^n - 1)$; 16-bit on a 10 V range gives **~0.153 mV/LSB**.
- **Stability check:** If sustained oscillation is observed at $K_u$, the system is at the stability limit; reduce $K_p$ by 50% for P-only or use Z–N values.
- **LabVIEW-specific good practice:** Place a **Wait (ms)** inside the While Loop; use a **Timed Loop** for hard real-time scheduling; use **TDMS file format** for efficient data logging.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of proportional, PI, and PID control modes for DC motor in LabVIEW
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCICL607 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Design of proportional, PI, and PID control modes for DC motor in LabVIEW # MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: Design of P, PI, and PID Control Modes for a DC Motor in LabVIEW ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) In the context of **PCICL607 – Process Control Instrumentation Lab**, Experiment 2 is the **software-based (virtual) design and analysis of closed-loop speed/position control of a separately-excited DC servo motor** using the three classical error-driven controllers — **Proportional (P)**, **Proportional-Integral (PI)**, and **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID)** — implemented and simulated entirely inside the **Laboratory Virtual Instrument Engineering Workbench (LabVIEW)** graphical programming environment. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus-Aligned Definition:** > *"Design, simulation, and performance evaluation of continuous-time P, PI, and PID controllers applied to a DC motor plant model using the LabVIEW Control Design and Simulation Module, with the objective of analysing transient response, steady-state error, and disturbance rejection for typical set-point tracking tasks."* ### 1.2 The Plant Under Control — The DC Motor A **separately excited DC motor** is the *plant* (process) whose angular speed (or shaft position) we wish to regulate. Its behaviour is described by a first-order linear time-invariant (LTI) transfer function when armature inductance $L_a$ is neglected, or by a second-order transfer function when $L_a$ is retained. The standard **armature-controlled** DC motor transfer function relating the armature voltage $V_a(s)$ to the angular displacement $\theta(s)$ is: $$ G_{motor}(s) = \frac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s \, (J s + B)(L_a s + R_a) + K_b K_m} $$ For the **speed-control** configuration used most commonly in this experiment, the transfer function between applied voltage $V_a(s)$ and angular speed $\omega(s)$ simplifies to: $$ G_{motor}(s) = \frac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_m}{(J s + B)(L_a s + R_a) + K_b K_m} $$ When $L_a$ is very small (typical for the lab-scale motor), it reduces to a **first-order lag**: $$ G_{motor}(s) \approx \frac{K}{\tau s + 1} $$ where $K$ is the steady-state gain, and $\tau$ is the mechanical time constant. ### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **The Cruise-Control Analogy:** > Imagine driving a car on a flat highway. You want to maintain a constant speed of **60 km/h** despite small road inclines and wind gusts. > - **Your eyes** are the **error detector** (subtractor) — they compare 60 km/h (set-point) with the actual speed (process variable). > - **Your right foot on the accelerator** is the **controller**. The harder you press, the more you correct. > - *Press harder in proportion to the speed difference* → **Proportional (P) controller**. > - *Keep pressing a little extra until the error is fully gone over time* → **Integral (I) action**. > - *Anticipate hills by pressing briefly when you see the road slope up* → **Derivative (D) action**. > - **The car's speed** is the **process variable** (output of the DC motor). > - **The dashboard speedometer** is the **sensor/feedback** path. > > LabVIEW is simply the *virtual driver's cabin* — a software screen where you build these blocks graphically instead of using physical wires and op-amps. ### 1.4 Physical Parameters & Standard Metrics Used in the Lab | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Lab Value (for small PMDC servo motor) | SI Unit | |:------:|:---------|:----------------------------------------------:|:-------:| | $R_a$ | Armature resistance | **1.0 – 2.5 Ω** | Ω | | $L_a$ | Armature inductance | **0.5 – 5.0 mH** | H | | $K_b$ | Back-EMF constant | **0.05 – 0.1 V·s/rad** | V·s/rad | | $K_m$ | Motor torque constant | **0.05 – 0.1 N·m/A** | N·m/A | | $J$ | Rotor inertia | **1 × 10⁻⁵ – 5 × 10⁻⁵ kg·m²** | kg·m² | | $B$ | Viscous friction coefficient | **1 × 10⁻⁴ – 1 × 10⁻⁶ N·m·s/rad** | N·m·s/rad | > [!TIP] > **For board exam purposes**, the exact numerical values are *not* as important as the *form* of the transfer function. Memorize the structure shown in Section 2. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mathematical Model of the Armature-Controlled DC Motor The DC motor is governed by two coupled first-order differential equations derived from **Newton's second law for rotation** and **Kirchhoff's voltage law for the armature circuit**. **Mechanical Equation (Newton's Law):** $$ J \frac{d\omega}{dt} + B\,\omega = K_m \, i_a - T_L $$ **Electrical Equation (KVL on Armature Loop):** $$ L_a \frac{di_a}{dt} + R_a \, i_a + K_b \, \omega = v_a $$ Where: - $\omega(t)$ = angular velocity of the motor shaft (rad/s) - $i_a(t)$ = armature current (A) - $v_a(t)$ = applied armature voltage (V) - $T_L$ = load torque disturbance (N·m) > The **back-EMF term $K_b \omega$** is the feedback that couples the mechanical and electrical subsystems — it is *the* reason a DC motor is naturally a stable plant. ### 2.2 The Three Controllers — P, PI, PID A controller takes the **error signal** $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ (set-point minus measured output) and generates a **control signal** $u(t)$ that drives the plant. #### 2.2.1 Proportional (P) Controller $$ u(t) = K_p \, e(t) \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad C_P(s) = K_p $$ - **Why it works:** Output is directly proportional to the instantaneous error. - **Limitation:** Cannot drive the steady-state error to zero for a step input on a type-0 plant (residual **offset**). - **Effect of increasing $K_p$:** Faster response, smaller (but not zero) steady-state error, but increased **overshoot** and possible **oscillation/instability**. #### 2.2.2 Proportional-Integral (PI) Controller $$ u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad C_{PI}(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} = \frac{K_p s + K_i}{s} $$ - **Why it works:** The integral term *accumulates* past errors. As long as *any* error exists, the integral grows and pushes the actuator until the error is exactly zero. - **Engineering truth:** This is the **most widely used controller in industry (~90% of loops)** because it eliminates steady-state error for step inputs while remaining simple to tune. - **Drawback:** Introduces a pole at the origin in the open-loop transfer function → reduces **phase margin** → can cause oscillation if $K_i$ is too large. - **Practical tuning relationship:** $K_i = \dfrac{K_p}{T_i}$ where $T_i$ is the **integral time (reset time)**. #### 2.2.3 Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) Controller $$ u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} $$ $$ C_{PID}(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s} $$ - **Why it works:** Adds an *anticipatory* term based on the rate of change of error. - **Effect:** Increases **damping**, reduces **overshoot**, and improves **transient response** without necessarily worsening steady-state error. - **Caveat:** The pure derivative amplifies high-frequency measurement noise; in LabVIEW, the implementation usually uses a **filtered derivative** with a first-order pole: $$ C_{PID,\,filtered}(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + \frac{K_d s}{1 + \tau_f s}, \quad \tau_f \approx 0.01\text{–}0.1\,T_i $$ ### 2.3 The Closed-Loop Transfer Function With controller $C(s)$ in the forward path and unity feedback, the closed-loop transfer function becomes: $$ T(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{C(s)\,G(s)}{1 + C(s)\,G(s)} $$ **For a P controller with a first-order plant** $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$: $$ T_P(s) = \frac{K K_p}{\tau s + (1 + K K_p)} = \frac{K_{cl}}{\tau_{cl} s + 1} $$ where $K_{cl} = \dfrac{K K_p}{1 + K K_p}$ and $\tau_{cl} = \dfrac{\tau}{1 + K K_p}$ — the closed-loop gain is **less than 1 (offset!)** and the time constant **shrinks** as $K_p$ grows. **For a PI controller** the closed-loop system has order two — the integral action forces $T_{PI}(0) = 1$, eliminating the offset. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Unit / Notes | |:-:|:------------------|:--------|:-------------| | 1 | DC motor speed TF (neglecting $L_a$) | $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ | $K = K_m/(B R_a + K_b K_m)$ | | 2 | Mechanical time constant | $\tau_m = J R_a / (B R_a + K_b K_m)$ | seconds | | 3 | Electrical time constant | $\tau_e = L_a / R_a$ | seconds (usually $\ll \tau_m$) | | 4 | P controller TF | $C_P(s) = K_p$ | dimensionless | | 5 | PI controller TF | $C_{PI}(s) = K_p + K_i/s$ | — | | 6 | PID controller TF | $C_{PID}(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$ | — | | 7 | Open-loop TF | $L(s) = C(s) G(s)$ | — | | 8 | Closed-loop TF (unity FB) | $T(s) = L(s) / [1 + L(s)]$ | must be **stable** | | 9 | Steady-state error (step input) | $e_{ss} = 1 / (1 + K_p)$ for P-controller | non-zero! | | 10 | Integral-removed offset | $e_{ss} \rightarrow 0$ for PI / PID on type-0 plant | proof via Final Value Theorem | | 11 | Rise time (approx.) | $t_r \approx 1.8 / \omega_n$ | first-order approximation | | 12 | % Overshoot | $\%M_p = 100 \, e^{-\zeta \pi / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | depends on damping ratio $\zeta$ | | 13 | Ziegler–Nichols $K_u$ method | $K_p = 0.5 K_u, \; T_i = 0.8 T_u, \; T_d = 0.125 T_u$ | $K_u$ = ultimate gain, $T_u$ = ultimate period | | 14 | Ziegler–Nichols reaction-curve | $K_p = \tau / (K \cdot L), \; T_i = 2L, \; T_d = 0.5 L$ | from process reaction curve | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board-exam mantra:** "P control **never** eliminates steady-state error for a step input on a type-0 plant. PI or higher **does**." Examiners test this single point more than any other in this experiment. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The DC motor is the *workhorse* of industrial motion control: - **CNC machines, robotic arms, conveyor belts, hard-disk drives, electric vehicles, drone propellers, camera gimbals** — every one of these uses a closed-loop speed/position controller. - The **tuning methodology** you learn on a simulated DC motor in LabVIEW is **directly applicable** to temperature loops, pressure loops, and flow loops in chemical plants. - LabVIEW's **Control Design and Simulation Module** is a standard tool in industrial R&D for rapid prototyping before committing to physical hardware (saving lakhs of rupees in prototype cost). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: DC Motor Transfer Function $G(s) = \omega(s)/V_a(s)$ We start with the two governing ODEs in the **Laplace domain** (assuming zero initial conditions and zero load torque $T_L = 0$). **Step 1 — Mechanical equation in Laplace domain:** $$ J s \, \Omega(s) + B \, \Omega(s) = K_m \, I_a(s) $$ Grouping terms: $$ \Omega(s)\,(J s + B) = K_m \, I_a(s) \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_a(s) = \frac{(J s + B)}{K_m}\,\Omega(s) $$ **Step 2 — Electrical equation in Laplace domain:** $$ L_a s \, I_a(s) + R_a \, I_a(s) + K_b \, \Omega(s) = V_a(s) $$ Grouping terms: $$ I_a(s)\,(L_a s + R_a) = V_a(s) - K_b \, \Omega(s) \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_a(s) = \frac{V_a(s) - K_b\,\Omega(s)}{L_a s + R_a} $$ **Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $I_a(s)$:** $$ \frac{(J s + B)}{K_m}\,\Omega(s) = \frac{V_a(s) - K_b\,\Omega(s)}{L_a s + R_a} $$ **Step 4 — Cross-multiply:** $$ (J s + B)(L_a s + R_a)\,\Omega(s) = K_m\bigl[V_a(s) - K_b\,\Omega(s)\bigr] $$ **Step 5 — Expand the left-hand side and collect the $\Omega(s)$ terms:** $$ (J s + B)(L_a s + R_a)\,\Omega(s) + K_m K_b\,\Omega(s) = K_m \, V_a(s) $$ $$ \bigl[(J s + B)(L_a s + R_a) + K_b K_m\bigr]\,\Omega(s) = K_m\,V_a(s) $$ **Step 6 — Final transfer function:** $$ G_{motor}(s) = \frac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_m}{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_b K_m} $$ **Step 7 — Simplification when $L_a \to 0$:** Setting $L_a = 0$: $$ G(s) = \frac{K_m}{R_a(J s + B) + K_b K_m} = \frac{K_m}{R_a J\, s + (R_a B + K_b K_m)} $$ Dividing numerator and denominator by $(R_a B + K_b K_m)$: $$ G(s) = \frac{K_m / (R_a B + K_b K_m)}{\dfrac{R_a J}{R_a B + K_b K_m}\,s + 1} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1} $$ with $$ K = \frac{K_m}{R_a B + K_b K_m}, \qquad \tau = \frac{R_a J}{R_a B + K_b K_m} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical meaning of $K$:** It is the no-load steady-state speed per volt applied. **Physical meaning of $\tau$:** It is the time taken for the motor to reach $63.2\%$ of its final speed in response to a step voltage. ## 3.2 Derivation: Steady-State Error for a P-Controller on a First-Order Plant **Step 1 — Plant:** $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$, **Controller:** $C(s) = K_p$, **Feedback:** $H(s) = 1$. **Step 2 — Open-loop TF:** $L(s) = C(s)G(s) = \dfrac{K K_p}{\tau s + 1}$. **Step 3 — Closed-loop TF:** $$ T(s) = \frac{L(s)}{1 + L(s)} = \frac{K K_p}{\tau s + 1 + K K_p} $$ **Step 4 — Apply a unit step input:** $R(s) = 1/s$. **Step 5 — Apply the Final Value Theorem** to get the steady-state output: $$ y_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \, Y(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{K K_p}{\tau s + 1 + K K_p} \cdot \frac{1}{s} = \frac{K K_p}{1 + K K_p} $$ **Step 6 — Steady-state error:** $$ e_{ss} = 1 - y_{ss} = 1 - \frac{K K_p}{1 + K K_p} = \frac{1}{1 + K K_p} $$ **Conclusion:** $e_{ss} \neq 0$ for any finite $K_p$. Only as $K_p \to \infty$ does the error go to zero — but at the cost of instability (denominator $\tau s + 1 + K K_p$ has its pole at $s = -(1 + K K_p)/\tau$, which is *always stable* for this first-order plant, but the plant becomes very fast and aggressive; in higher-order plants, very high $K_p$ *does* destabilize). ## 3.3 Derivation: PI-Controller Eliminates Steady-State Error **Step 1 — Controller:** $C_{PI}(s) = K_p + K_i/s$, **Plant:** $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$. **Step 2 — Open-loop TF:** $$ L(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}\right) \frac{K}{\tau s + 1} = \frac{K(K_p s + K_i)}{s(\tau s + 1)} $$ **Step 3 — Position constant** (since the open-loop now has a pole at the origin, the system is **Type 1**): $$ K_p^{pos} = \lim_{s \to 0} L(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{K(K_p s + K_i)}{s(\tau s + 1)} = \infty $$ **Step 4 — Apply Final Value Theorem for unit step:** $$ e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p^{pos}} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0 $$ **Conclusion:** The integral action has made the system **Type 1**, which guarantees zero steady-state error for a step input. ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (For Board Exam Practice) > **Given:** > $R_a = 2 \,\Omega$, $L_a = 0.5$ H, $K_b = 0.05$ V·s/rad, $K_m = 0.05$ N·m/A, $J = 5\times 10^{-5}$ kg·m², $B = 1\times 10^{-4}$ N·m·s/rad. > > **Required:** Find the plant TF, the steady-state error with a P-controller having $K_p = 10$, and design a PI-controller that gives zero steady-state error and a closed-loop natural frequency $\omega_n \approx 30$ rad/s. **Step 1 — Compute the steady-state gain $K$:** Denominator constant: $R_a B + K_b K_m = (2)(1\times 10^{-4}) + (0.05)(0.05) = 2\times 10^{-4} + 2.5\times 10^{-3} = 2.7\times 10^{-3}$. $$ K = \frac{K_m}{R_a B + K_b K_m} = \frac{0.05}{2.7 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 18.52 \text{ rad/(s·V)} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the time constant $\tau$:** $$ \tau = \frac{R_a J}{R_a B + K_b K_m} = \frac{(2)(5\times 10^{-5})}{2.7\times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1\times 10^{-4}}{2.7\times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.0370 \text{ s} $$ **Step 3 — Plant TF (neglecting $L_a$):** $$ G(s) = \frac{18.52}{0.0370\,s + 1} $$ **Step 4 — Steady-state error with P-controller $K_p = 10$:** $$ e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K K_p} = \frac{1}{1 + (18.52)(10)} = \frac{1}{186.2} \approx 0.00537 \;(\approx 0.54\%) $$ **Step 5 — Design a PI-controller for the second-order target** $\omega_n = 30$ rad/s, $\zeta = 0.7$: The desired closed-loop characteristic polynomial is: $$ s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = s^2 + 42\,s + 900 $$ The actual closed-loop characteristic polynomial (with $G(s) = K/(\tau s + 1)$ and $C_{PI} = K_p + K_i/s$) is: $$ 1 + \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}\right)\frac{K}{\tau s + 1} = 0 $$ Multiplying by $s(\tau s + 1)$: $$ s(\tau s + 1) + K(K_p s + K_i) = 0 $$ $$ \tau s^2 + s + K K_p s + K K_i = 0 $$ $$ \tau s^2 + (1 + K K_p) s + K K_i = 0 $$ Dividing by $\tau$: $$ s^2 + \frac{(1 + K K_p)}{\tau} s + \frac{K K_i}{\tau} = 0 $$ **Step 6 — Match coefficients:** $$ \frac{1 + K K_p}{\tau} = 2\zeta\omega_n = 42 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 + K K_p = 42 \tau = 42(0.0370) = 1.555 $$ $$ K K_p = 0.555 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_p = \frac{0.555}{18.52} \approx 0.0300 $$ $$ \frac{K K_i}{\tau} = \omega_n^2 = 900 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K K_i = 900 \tau = 900(0.0370) = 33.33 $$ $$ K_i = \frac{33.33}{18.52} \approx 1.800 \text{ s}^{-1} $$ **Step 7 — Verification of zero steady-state error:** Because the system is now Type-1, $e_{ss} = 0$ for any step input, regardless of $K_p$ and $K_i$ (as long as the closed loop remains stable, which our damping ratio $\zeta = 0.7 > 0$ guarantees). > [!TIP] > **The numerical values here are not what you should memorize — the *method of coefficient matching* is what examiners test.** ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Behavioural Comparison of P, PI, PID | Performance Metric | P Controller | PI Controller | PID Controller | |:-------------------|:------------:|:-------------:|:--------------:| | Steady-state error (step) | **Non-zero** $= 1/(1+KK_p)$ | **Zero** | **Zero** | | Steady-state error (ramp) | **Infinite** | Non-zero, finite | Non-zero, finite | | Rise time | Fastest for given $K_p$ | Slightly slower than P | Fast, comparable to P | | Overshoot | Moderate–High | Reduced (if well-tuned) | **Lowest** (well-tuned) | | Settling time | Moderate | Often longer | **Shortest** | | Disturbance rejection | Fair | **Good** | **Best** | | Sensitivity to noise | Low | Low | **High** (derivative amplifies noise) | | Tuning complexity | 1 parameter | 2 parameters | 3 parameters | | Typical industrial use | Rare alone | **~90% of loops** | Where stability of fast loops matters | # 4. 4.4 Typical LabVIEW Front-Panel Layout (Textual Schematic) The **Front Panel** of the LabVIEW VI for this experiment typically contains the following user-interface elements: | Region | Control / Indicator | LabVIEW Palette Location | Purpose | |:------:|:-------------------|:------------------------|:--------| | Top-left | Numeric Control — *Set-Point* | Numeric palette | Set desired speed (rad/s or rpm) | | Top-left | Numeric Control — *Step Time* | Numeric palette | When the set-point is applied (s) | | Centre | Waveform Chart — *Speed Output* | Graph palette | Plots $\omega(t)$ live | | Centre | Waveform Chart — *Error Signal* | Graph palette | Plots $e(t)$ live | | Centre | Waveform Chart — *Control Signal* | Graph palette | Plots $u(t)$ live | | Right | Knob / Slider — *Kp* | Numeric → Dialog | Tune P gain | | Right | Knob / Slider — *Ki* | Numeric → Dialog | Tune I gain | | Right | Knob / Slider — *Kd* | Numeric → Dialog | Tune D gain | | Bottom | Boolean Button — *STOP* | Boolean palette | Halt simulation loop | > [!IMPORTANT] > The actual **Block Diagram** uses nodes from the **Control & Simulation → Simulation → Continuous Linear Systems** sub-palette, specifically: > - *CD Construct TF Model.vi* — to enter $G(s)$ as a symbolic transfer function. > - *CD Step Signal.vi* — to generate a step set-point. > - *CD Sum.vi* — to compute the error. > - *Simulation Loop* — to time-step the continuous simulation. > - *CD Draw Transfer Function Equation.vi* (optional) — to display $G(s)$ on the front panel. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A] **"Why does a pure Proportional (P) controller fail to eliminate the steady-state error for a step input applied to a first-order plant? Justify with the Final Value Theorem."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** For a first-order plant $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ and a P-controller $C(s) = K_p$, the open-loop transfer function is $L(s) = \dfrac{K K_p}{\tau s + 1}$, which is a **Type-0 system** (no integrator). The closed-loop step response has steady-state value: $$ y_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{L(s)}{1 + L(s)} \cdot \frac{1}{s} = \frac{K K_p}{1 + K K_p} $$ The steady-state error is therefore: $$ e_{ss} = 1 - y_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K K_p} \neq 0 $$ This non-zero offset exists because, in steady-state, the error $e$ must remain *non-zero* in order to sustain the control signal $u = K_p e$ that drives the plant to the desired output. As $K_p \to \infty$, $e_{ss} \to 0$, but practical limits on $K_p$ (actuator saturation, stability margins) prevent this. **(3 marks)** ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B] **"List any three advantages and one limitation of using a PI controller for DC motor speed control."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Advantages:** 1. **Eliminates steady-state error** for step inputs (system becomes Type-1). **(1 mark)** 2. **Simple two-parameter tuning** ($K_p$, $K_i$) — easy to implement on industrial PLCs and microcontrollers. **(1 mark)** 3. **Good disturbance rejection** for slow-varying load torques, since the integral term keeps accumulating error and forces compensation. **(1 mark — partial credit for naming any other correct advantage** **Limitation:** 1. The integrator introduces **phase lag**, which reduces the phase margin and can lead to **oscillatory transients** if $K_i$ is set too high. **(0 marks by itself but the question already allotted 3 marks for the three advantages — re-allocating** > **Corrected scoring:** The question is 3 marks and asks for 3 advantages + 1 limitation. Award 2 marks for the advantages and 1 mark for the limitation. *(Examiner's discretion.)* **Limitation:** The integrator introduces a pole at the origin, reducing the **phase margin**, and excessive $K_i$ can cause **sustained oscillations or instability**. **(1 mark)** --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** Derive the transfer function of an **armature-controlled separately excited DC motor** relating the armature voltage to the angular velocity, including the back-EMF and armature-inductance terms. Clearly state the assumptions. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** For the motor with parameters $R_a = 1\,\Omega$, $L_a \approx 0$, $K_b = K_m = 0.1$, $J = 1\times 10^{-4}$ kg·m², $B = 1\times 10^{-4}$ N·m·s/rad, design a **PI controller** such that the closed-loop system has a damping ratio $\zeta = 0.707$ and natural frequency $\omega_n = 50$ rad/s. Verify that the steady-state error for a unit step input is zero. **\[7 marks\]** #### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks **Step 1 — State the two governing equations:** *(1 mark)* $$ J \frac{d\omega}{dt} + B\omega = K_m i_a - T_L $$ $$ L_a \frac{di_a}{dt} + R_a i_a + K_b \omega = v_a $$ **Step 2 — State the assumptions:** *(1 mark)* - Field current is held constant (separately excited with constant field). - Load torque $T_L = 0$ (no-load condition, or absorbed into $B$). - Motor is unsaturated, linear, and parameters are constant. - Initial conditions are zero. **Step 3 — Take Laplace transforms:** *(1 mark)* $$ (J s + B)\Omega(s) = K_m I_a(s) $$ $$ (L_a s + R_a) I_a(s) + K_b \Omega(s) = V_a(s) $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $I_a(s)$ from the mechanical equation:** *(1 mark)* $$ I_a(s) = \frac{(J s + B)\Omega(s)}{K_m} $$ **Step 5 — Substitute into the electrical equation:** *(1 mark)* $$ (L_a s + R_a)\frac{(J s + B)\Omega(s)}{K_m} + K_b \Omega(s) = V_a(s) $$ **Step 6 — Solve for the transfer function $\Omega(s)/V_a(s)$:** *(2 marks)* $$ \Omega(s)\left[\frac{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B)}{K_m} + K_b\right] = V_a(s) $$ $$ \frac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_m}{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_b K_m} $$ **Expanding the denominator:** $$ G_{motor}(s) = \frac{K_m}{(L_a J) s^2 + (L_a B + R_a J) s + (R_a B + K_b K_m)} $$ #### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks **Step 1 — Compute plant parameters with $L_a = 0$:** *(1 mark)* $$ K = \frac{K_m}{R_a B + K_b K_m} = \frac{0.1}{(1)(1\times 10^{-4}) + (0.1)(0.1)} = \frac{0.1}{0.0101} \approx 9.901 $$ $$ \tau = \frac{R_a J}{R_a B + K_b K_m} = \frac{(1)(1\times 10^{-4})}{0.0101} \approx 9.901 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} $$ **Step 2 — Plant transfer function:** *(1 mark)* $$ G(s) = \frac{9.901}{0.009901\,s + 1} $$ **Step 3 — Desired closed-loop characteristic polynomial:** *(1 mark)* $$ s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = s^2 + 2(0.707)(50) s + 2500 = s^2 + 70.7\,s + 2500 $$ **Step 4 — Actual closed-loop characteristic polynomial with PI controller:** *(1 mark)* From the derivation in Section 3.4: $$ \tau s^2 + (1 + K K_p)s + K K_i = 0 $$ Dividing by $\tau$: $$ s^2 + \frac{(1 + K K_p)}{\tau} s + \frac{K K_i}{\tau} = 0 $$ **Step 5 — Match coefficients:** *(2 marks)* $$ \frac{1 + K K_p}{\tau} = 70.7 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 + K K_p = 70.7 \times 0.009901 = 0.700 $$ $$ K K_p = -0.300 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_p = \frac{-0.300}{9.901} \approx -0.0303 $$ *(A negative $K_p$ — physically this means the controller sign convention must be inverted; in LabVIEW this is a one-click polarity change. The mathematical design is valid.)* $$ \frac{K K_i}{\tau} = 2500 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K K_i = 2500 \times 0.009901 = 24.75 $$ $$ K_i = \frac{24.75}{9.901} \approx 2.50 \text{ s}^{-1} $$ **Step 6 — Verify zero steady-state error:** *(1 mark)* The open-loop transfer function with PI controller is: $$ L(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}\right) \frac{K}{\tau s + 1} = \frac{K(K_p s + K_i)}{s(\tau s + 1)} $$ This is a **Type-1 system** (one pole at the origin). For a unit step input, the position error constant is: $$ K_p^{pos} = \lim_{s \to 0} L(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{K(K_p s + K_i)}{s(\tau s + 1)} = \infty $$ Therefore: $$ e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p^{pos}} = \frac{1}{\infty} = 0 $$ **Zero steady-state error is verified.** ✓ --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** With a neat **LabVIEW block diagram**, explain how you would simulate a **closed-loop PID-controlled DC motor** in the Control & Simulation Module. Clearly label the **Simulation Loop**, the **plant transfer function block**, the **PID controller block**, the **error summer**, and the **feedback path**. List the front-panel controls you would use to vary $K_p$, $K_i$, and $K_d$ in real time. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** For the same plant $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{0.05\,s + 1}$, compare the **time-domain step response** of (i) a P-controller with $K_p = 1$, (ii) a PI-controller with $K_p = 1$, $K_i = 5$, and (iii) a PID-controller with $K_p = 1$, $K_i = 5$, $K_d = 0.05$. For each case, state (qualitatively) the expected **steady-state error, rise time, overshoot, and settling time**. **\[7 marks\]** #### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks **Step 1 — LabVIEW Block Diagram description:** *(4 marks)* The VI consists of: - A **Simulation Loop** (Functions → Control & Simulation → Simulation → Simulation Loop) that contains the entire continuous-time model. The loop period is set to e.g. **1 ms** and the solver to **Runge-Kutta 4 (RK4)** for accuracy. *(0.5 mark)* - A **Step Signal block** (CD Step Signal.vi) provides the set-point $r(t)$ — the user enters the *step time* and *step amplitude* on the front panel. *(0.5 mark)* - A **Sum block** (CD Sum.vi) configured with gains $(+1, -1)$ computes $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$. *(0.5 mark)* - A **PID block** (CD PID.vi) takes the error and outputs $u(t)$; its three terminals — *Kp*, *Ki*, *Kd* — are wired to front-panel controls. The internal implementation uses a parallel form with a derivative filter (default $\tau_f = 0.01 T_i$). *(1 mark)* - A **DC Motor transfer function block** (CD Construct TF Model.vi) with numerator $K_m$ and denominator $[(L_a J) \; (L_a B + R_a J) \; (R_a B + K_b K_m)]$; the block output is the motor speed $\omega(t)$. *(0.5 mark)* - A **feedback wire** carrying $\omega(t)$ back to the negative input of the Sum block (unity feedback). *(0.5 mark)* - **Probe indicators / waveform charts** to display $\omega(t)$, $e(t)$, and $u(t)$ live. *(0.5 mark)* **Step 2 — Front-panel controls for live tuning:** *(2 marks)* - Numeric Control (Knob or Slider) — **Kp**, range e.g. 0 to 100. *(0.5 mark)* - Numeric Control (Knob or Slider) — **Ki**, range e.g. 0 to 50. *(0.5 mark)* - Numeric Control (Knob or Slider) — **Kd**, range e.g. 0 to 5. *(0.5 mark)* - Boolean Toggle — **PID Bypass / Mode Select** (Optional) to switch between P, PI, PID for comparative study. *(0.5 mark)* **Step 3 — Procedure for the experiment:** *(1 mark)* 1. Enter motor parameters and run the VI with $K_i = K_d = 0$ (pure P-mode) — record the step response. 2. Enable integral action, sweep $K_i$ — observe steady-state error decreasing to zero. 3. Enable derivative action, sweep $K_d$ — observe reduction in overshoot. 4. Tabulate rise time, overshoot, settling time, and steady-state error for each combination. #### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks **Plant:** $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{0.05\,s + 1}$, so $K = 10$, $\tau = 0.05$ s. **Case (i) — P-controller with $K_p = 1$:** *(2 marks)* Closed-loop TF: $$ T_P(s) = \frac{K K_p}{\tau s + 1 + K K_p} = \frac{10}{0.05\,s + 11} $$ Steady-state gain: $\dfrac{10}{11} \approx 0.909$. - **Steady-state error:** $e_{ss} = 1 - 0.909 = 0.091$ (i.e. **9.1 % offset**). ✓ - **Time constant:** $\tau_{cl} = 0.05/11 = 0.00455$ s → very fast. - **Rise time:** $t_r \approx 2.2 \tau_{cl} \approx 0.010$ s. - **Overshoot:** **0 %** (first-order system). - **Settling time:** $t_s \approx 4 \tau_{cl} \approx 0.018$ s. **Case (ii) — PI-controller with $K_p = 1$, $K_i = 5$:** *(2.5 marks)* Closed-loop characteristic polynomial: $$ \tau s^2 + (1 + K K_p) s + K K_i = 0.05\,s^2 + 11\,s + 50 = 0 $$ $$ \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{50}{0.05}} = \sqrt{1000} \approx 31.62 \text{ rad/s} $$ $$ \zeta = \frac{11}{2 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 31.62} = \frac{11}{3.162} \approx 3.48 $$ Since $\zeta > 1$, the system is **overdamped**. - **Steady-state error:** $e_{ss} = 0$ (Type-1 system). ✓ - **Overshoot:** **0 %** (overdamped). - **Rise time:** slow compared to Case (i), roughly $t_r \approx (\pi - \cos^{-1}\zeta)/\omega_d \approx$ 0.04 s. - **Settling time:** $t_s \approx 4/(\zeta\omega_n) \approx 4/(3.48 \times 31.62) \approx 0.036$ s. **Case (iii) — PID-controller with $K_p = 1$, $K_i = 5$, $K_d = 0.05$:** *(2.5 marks)* Closed-loop characteristic polynomial (with derivative, neglect filter for simplicity): $$ \tau s^2 + (K K_d + 1 + K K_p) s + K K_i = 0 $$ Wait — this is the parallel form applied to a first-order plant, the closed-loop order remains **second-order** because the $s$ in the numerator of $K_d s$ cancels with the $s$ in the controller denominator? No, the PID denominator is $s$, and the plant is first-order, so the open-loop has a pole at origin (Type 1) and the plant pole — the open-loop order is two, hence the closed-loop order is two. With $K_d = 0.05$: $$ (K K_d) s^2 + (1 + K K_p) s + K K_i = 0 $$ $$ (10 \times 0.05) s^2 + (1 + 10 \times 1) s + 50 = 0 $$ $$ 0.5\,s^2 + 11\,s + 50 = 0 $$ $$ \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{50}{0.5}} = 10 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \zeta = \frac{11}{2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 10} = \frac{11}{10} = 1.1 $$ Still **overdamped** ($\zeta > 1$). - **Steady-state error:** $e_{ss} = 0$. ✓ - **Overshoot:** **0 %** (overdamped). - **Rise time:** the system is now *slower* than in Case (ii) because $K_d$ has added a *zero* in the open-loop (which is in the numerator of the closed-loop TF) that increases the natural frequency perceived by the system, but in this particular numerical case $\omega_n$ has *decreased* due to the leading coefficient. The rise time is roughly 0.05–0.08 s. - **Settling time:** $t_s \approx 4/(\zeta\omega_n) \approx 4/11 \approx 0.36$ s. **Summary Table for Lab Notebook:** | Controller | $e_{ss}$ | Rise time | Overshoot | Settling time | |:----------:|:--------:|:---------:|:---------:|:-------------:| | P ($K_p=1$) | 9.1 % | 0.010 s | 0 % | 0.018 s | | PI ($K_p=1, K_i=5$) | **0 %** | 0.04 s | 0 % | 0.036 s | | PID ($K_p=1, K_i=5, K_d=0.05$) | **0 %** | 0.06 s | 0 % | 0.36 s | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks** > 1. **Confusing *Plant Type* and *Controller Type*:** Examiners often ask "Why does a Type-0 plant with a P-controller have non-zero steady-state error for a step input?" Students who write "because the plant is open-loop" lose 2 marks. The correct answer: "Because the system has **no pole at the origin** in the open-loop transfer function, the position error constant is finite, not infinite." > 2. **Forgetting to negate the feedback:** In the block diagram, the summing junction must have $r(t)$ with a **+** sign and $y(t)$ with a **−** sign. Many students draw both with **+** and get a positive-feedback (unstable) system. Always check the sign. > 3. **Mismeasuring steady-state error:** $e_{ss} = r_{ss} - y_{ss}$, **not** $u_{ss} - y_{ss}$. The control signal is *not* part of the error. > 4. **Forgetting units:** $K_i$ has units of s⁻¹, $K_d$ has units of s. Writing $K_i = 5$ without units loses a mark. > 5. **Saying "PID has no offset" without proof:** A bare statement is worth 0.5 marks; show the Final Value Theorem or the Type-1 argument for full credit. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist) - [x] The **armature-controlled DC motor** has a transfer function with **one pole at the origin** (when output is **position**) or **two real poles** (when output is **speed**, with $L_a \neq 0$). For speed control with negligible $L_a$, the plant is **first-order**: $G(s) = K/(\tau s + 1)$. - [x] The **back-EMF term $K_b \omega$** is the internal feedback that makes the motor naturally stable. - [x] **P-controller** $C(s) = K_p$: gives offset on step input for Type-0 plants. Increases speed but causes oscillation if $K_p$ is too high. - [x] **PI-controller** $C(s) = K_p + K_i/s$: eliminates offset, makes the system Type-1, **industry's workhorse**. - [x] **PID-controller** $C(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$: adds damping, reduces overshoot, but amplifies measurement noise. Use a **derivative filter** $\tau_f$ in practice. - [x] **Steady-state error for unit step on P-controlled first-order plant:** $e_{ss} = 1/(1 + K K_p)$ — **not zero**. - [x] **Steady-state error for unit step on PI-controlled first-order plant:** $e_{ss} = 0$ (because the system is now Type-1). - [x] **Ziegler–Nichols tuning** (ultimate-gain method): set $K_i = K_d = 0$, increase $K_p$ until the system oscillates with constant amplitude. Read off $K_u$ and $T_u$. Then apply the formulas: $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 T_u$, $T_d = 0.125 T_u$ for a PID (or the P/PI versions as given in Section 2.4). - [x] **LabVIEW implementation essentials:** Simulation Loop, CD Construct TF Model, CD Sum, CD PID, CD Step Signal, Waveform Charts for output, Numeric Sliders for $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$, RK4 solver, loop period 1 ms. - [x] **Always set the solver** in the Simulation Loop's input node to **RK4 (4th-order Runge-Kutta)** for stiff or fast plants; use **RK1 (Euler)** only for very simple pedagogical examples. - [x] **Negative $K_p$ designs** can be a *sign of correct mathematical design* — it merely means your set-point polarity is opposite to the actuator polarity. **Invert the sign at the controller output** in LabVIEW using a $-1$ gain block. - [x] **Final value theorem** is the tool of choice for *all* steady-state error calculations in this experiment. - [x] **Always tabulate** $e_{ss}$, $t_r$, $M_p$, and $t_s$ for the three controller modes in the lab record — this is the standard expected by KTU evaluators. - [x] **Derivative kick** — when a step change is applied to the set-point, the derivative of the error contains a Dirac impulse. In LabVIEW, route the derivative through the **measured output** (i.e. $u = K_p e + K_i \int e + K_d (-dy/dt)$) to avoid this — LabVIEW's *CD PID.vi* has a configuration terminal for this. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Mantra for the Lab Exam:** > *"Plant first, then controller. Identify the plant order, identify the controller order, write the closed-loop characteristic polynomial, apply Final Value Theorem for $e_{ss}$, use Ziegler–Nichols for initial $K_p$/$K_i$/$K_d$ values, then iterate in LabVIEW using the waveform chart until the transient specifications are met."*

topics.title: Design of electronic proportional controller using OPAMP and monitoring both controlled variable and manipulating variable using LabVIEW
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCICL607 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Design of electronic proportional controller using OPAMP and monitoring both controlled variable and manipulating variable using LabVIEW # 🔬 Experiment 2: Electronic Proportional Controller Design & LabVIEW Monitoring ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Proportional Controller (P-Controller):** A linear feedback controller whose output is directly proportional to the error signal — defined as the difference between the **setpoint (SP)** and the **controlled variable / process variable (PV)**. Mathematically, the controller output is expressed as: > > $$m(t) = K_c \cdot e(t) + m_0$$ > > where $K_c$ is the **proportional gain (dimensionless)**, $e(t)$ is the instantaneous error, and $m_0$ is the **bias / controller output at zero error**. An **electronic P-controller** is the hardware implementation of this law using active analog components — specifically, an **operational amplifier (op-amp)** configured as an **inverting summing amplifier** with adjustable gain. The op-amp performs two simultaneous functions: 1. **Subtraction** of the measured PV from the setpoint (error generation). 2. **Amplification** of the resulting error by a tunable factor $K_c$ (proportional action). The **controlled variable (CV)** is the process variable being regulated (e.g., temperature, pressure, flow), and the **manipulating variable (MV)** is the signal the controller sends to the final control element (e.g., valve position, heater power). **LabVIEW** (Laboratory Virtual Instrument Engineering Workbench) — a graphical programming environment from **National Instruments (NI)** — is used for real-time digital monitoring, plotting, and data logging of both CV and MV. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cruise Control of Your Car > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: Driving on a Highway with Cruise Control** > > Imagine you set your car's cruise control to **100 km/h** (this is the **setpoint, SP**). Your current speed is **80 km/h** (this is the **process variable, PV**). The error is $e = SP - PV = 20$ km/h. The cruise control system applies more fuel (the **manipulating variable, MV**) proportional to this error. The larger the gap, the harder it presses the accelerator. This is **proportional action**. > > - The car's speedometer reading → **Controlled Variable (CV/PV)** > - The accelerator pedal position → **Manipulating Variable (MV)** > - The set speed dial → **Setpoint (SP)** > - The Engine Control Unit (ECU) → **The op-amp P-controller** > - The dashboard display showing speed & throttle % → **LabVIEW Virtual Instrument (VI)** The op-amp is the "ECU" of the control loop. It looks at the gap, multiplies it by a tunable knob (the gain $K_c$), and outputs a corrective signal — all in **microseconds**, since op-amps have bandwidths in the **MHz** range. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants > [!TIP] > **Key Standard Metrics Used in This Experiment:** > > | Metric | Symbol | Unit | Typical Range | > |---|---|---|---| > | Proportional Gain | $K_c$ | dimensionless (or %/% ) | **0.1 to 100** | > | Proportional Band | $PB$ | % | $PB = \dfrac{100}{K_c} \times 100\,\%$ | > | Error Signal | $e(t)$ | V (volts) | **0 – 10 V** (standard industrial) | > | Controller Output (MV) | $m(t)$ | V or mA | **0 – 10 V** or **4 – 20 mA** | > | Bias / Reset | $m_0$ | V | **0 – 10 V** | > | Op-amp Supply | $V_{CC}, -V_{EE}$ | V | **±15 V** (typical) | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Block Diagram of a Closed-Loop Control System A complete feedback control loop consists of four cardinal elements: 1. **Process** — the plant whose variable is to be controlled. 2. **Sensor / Transmitter** — measures the CV and converts it to a standardized electrical signal. 3. **Controller** — compares SP and PV, computes the error, and applies the control law (here, P-action). 4. **Final Control Element (FCE)** — typically a control valve, variable-speed drive, or heater, actuated by the MV. The **signal flow** is: $$\text{SP} \rightarrow \text{Comparator} \rightarrow \text{Controller (Op-amp P)} \rightarrow \text{MV} \rightarrow \text{FCE} \rightarrow \text{Process} \rightarrow \text{CV} \rightarrow \text{Transmitter} \rightarrow \text{Comparator}$$ ## 2.2 Mathematical Foundation of Proportional Control The classical continuous-time proportional control law: $$m(t) = K_c \, e(t) + m_0$$ In the **Laplace domain** (assuming $m_0 = 0$ for simplicity, i.e., zero bias): $$M(s) = K_c \cdot E(s)$$ Hence the **transfer function of a pure P-controller** is: $$G_c(s) = \frac{M(s)}{E(s)} = K_c$$ This is a **pure gain block** — no dynamics, no memory, no time constant. This is the *fastest* controller mode, but it inherently produces a **steady-state offset** (also called **droop** or **bias error**). ## 2.3 Operational Amplifier as a Proportional Controller The op-amp is configured as an **inverting summing amplifier**, which elegantly performs: - **Summing** of setpoint and measured PV (with appropriate signs to yield error). - **Multiplication by a tunable gain** $K_c = R_f / R_{in}$. The output of the inverting amplifier is: $$V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}} \cdot V_{in}$$ For a **summing configuration** with two inputs ($V_{SP}$ and $V_{PV}$): $$V_{out} = -\left[\frac{R_f}{R_1} V_{SP} + \frac{R_f}{R_2} V_{PV}\right]$$ To get a true P-controller, we connect the **measured PV to the non-inverting input** (or invert it again) so that: $$V_{MV} = \frac{R_f}{R_{in}} (V_{SP} - V_{PV}) = K_c \cdot e$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why an Inverting Configuration?** > The inverting amplifier is preferred because: > 1. The virtual ground at the inverting input makes the summing operation mathematically clean. > 2. Gain is set by a **single resistor ratio**, making it easy to tune. > 3. Negative feedback ensures **linear, stable operation** within the op-amp's bandwidth. ## 2.4 Proportional Band (PB) — The Industrial Twin of $K_c$ In process industries, controllers are often characterized by **Proportional Band** rather than gain: $$PB\,(\%) = \frac{100}{K_c} \times 100 = \frac{10000}{K_c}$$ Interpretation: PB is the **range of error** (as a percentage of the transmitter's calibrated span) required to drive the controller output through its **full range**. A small PB → high gain → aggressive (possibly oscillatory) control. A large PB → low gain → sluggish control. ## 2.5 Steady-State Offset — The Inherent Flaw of Pure P-Control When a load disturbance enters the process, a pure P-controller will settle to a new steady state where the error is **NOT zero**. This residual error is called **offset**: $$e_{ss} = \frac{\text{Disturbance}}{1 + K_c \cdot G_p(0) \cdot G_v(0) \cdot G_m(0)}$$ As $K_c \to \infty$, $e_{ss} \to 0$ — but in practice, very high $K_c$ causes **sustained oscillations** and **instability**. This is the fundamental trade-off that motivates the addition of **Integral (I)** action in PI/PID controllers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Examiners frequently ask: *"Why does a pure proportional controller produce offset?"* The answer is: *For the controller output to settle to a new steady value after a load change, the error must be non-zero (since MV ∝ error). Hence PV ≠ SP at steady state.* ## 2.6 LabVIEW-Based Monitoring — Architecture LabVIEW programs are called **Virtual Instruments (VIs)**. Each VI has two panels: | Panel | Purpose | |---|---| | **Front Panel** | User interface — knobs, switches, charts, numeric indicators (mimics a physical instrument). | | **Block Diagram** | Graphical source code — wires, icons, structures (the actual logic). | For our experiment, LabVIEW is used to: 1. **Acquire analog voltage signals** from the op-amp circuit corresponding to $V_{PV}$ (CV) and $V_{MV}$ (MV) via an **NI DAQ (Data Acquisition) card** (e.g., NI USB-6008/6009) or a **serial-port-connected ADC** (e.g., Arduino with voltage divider). 2. **Display real-time waveforms** on a **Waveform Chart** or **Graph** indicator. 3. **Log data** to a `.tdms` or `.lvm` file for offline analysis. 4. **Compute metrics** such as offset, peak, and rise time using built-in functions. ### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master Table — Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $m(t) = K_c \, e(t) + m_0$ | Proportional control law | V | | 2 | $e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)$ | Error definition | V (or eng. units) | | 3 | $G_c(s) = M(s)/E(s) = K_c$ | P-controller transfer function | dimensionless | | 4 | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}} V_{in}$ | Inverting op-amp gain | V | | 5 | $K_c = R_f / R_{in}$ | Op-amp-based $K_c$ | dimensionless | | 6 | $PB\,(\%) = 100 / K_c \times 100$ | Proportional band | % | | 7 | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{D}{1+K_c G_{loop}(0)}$ | Steady-state offset | V or eng. units | | 8 | $f_{BW} \approx \dfrac{GBW}{K_c}$ | Closed-loop bandwidth (unity-gain stable op-amp) | Hz | | 9 | $V_{MV,\max} = V_{sat} - 2\,\text{V}$ | Practical op-amp output swing | V | | 10 | $\tau = R_{in} C_{in}$ | Input time constant (if filter added) | s | > **Where:** > $D$ = load disturbance magnitude, > $G_{loop}(0)$ = loop DC gain, > $GBW$ = gain-bandwidth product of the op-amp (e.g., **1 MHz** for $\mu$A741), > $V_{sat}$ = saturation voltage (e.g., **±13.5 V** for ±15 V supply). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Op-Amp Inverting Amplifier Output We start from **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the inverting node (let's call it node A). For an ideal op-amp, the inverting input draws **no current**, and the **virtual short** forces $V_A = V_+ = 0\,\text{V}$ (since the non-inverting input is grounded). **Step 1:** Apply KCL at node A. $$\sum I_{into\,A} = 0$$ The currents entering node A are: - From the input source through $R_{in}$: $I_{in} = \dfrac{V_{in} - V_A}{R_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{in} - 0}{R_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_{in}}$ - From the output through the feedback resistor $R_f$: $I_f = \dfrac{V_{out} - V_A}{R_f} = \dfrac{V_{out} - 0}{R_f} = \dfrac{V_{out}}{R_f}$ **Step 2:** Both currents flow **into** node A (input current flows in, feedback current flows in because $V_{out}$ is negative for positive $V_{in}$), and the op-amp draws zero current, so: $$\frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}} + \frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$$ **Step 3:** Solve for $V_{out}$. $$\frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = -\frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}}$$ $$\boxed{V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}} V_{in}}$$ The negative sign indicates a **180° phase inversion** — hence the name "inverting amplifier." For our P-controller, the **second inversion** (or routing PV to the non-inverting input) restores the correct error sign. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Steady-State Offset Consider a standard unity-feedback loop with a P-controller. Let the process transfer function be $G_p(s)$ and a step disturbance $D(s) = D_0 / s$ enter at the process input. **Step 1:** The closed-loop transfer function from disturbance to error is: $$\frac{E(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{-G_p(s)}{1 + K_c G_p(s)}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the **Final Value Theorem** (FVT) to find steady-state error due to disturbance: $$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{-G_p(s)}{1 + K_c G_p(s)} \cdot \frac{D_0}{s}$$ **Step 3:** Cancel $s$: $$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{-D_0 \, G_p(s)}{1 + K_c G_p(s)}$$ **Step 4:** Evaluate at $s = 0$, where $G_p(0) = K_p$ (the process DC gain): $$e_{ss} = \frac{-D_0 \, K_p}{1 + K_c K_p}$$ Taking magnitudes (sign depends on convention): $$\boxed{\vert e_{ss} \vert = \frac{\vert D_0 \vert \, K_p}{1 + K_c K_p}}$$ > **Engineering Interpretation:** As $K_c$ increases, $e_{ss} \to D_0 / K_c \to 0$ (ideally), but large $K_c$ reduces phase margin and may destabilize the loop. The **optimum $K_c$** is the largest value that still yields acceptable damping (e.g., **damping ratio $\zeta \geq 0.4$**). ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Op-Amp Component Selection **Problem (KTU-style):** Design an electronic P-controller using a $\mu$A741 op-amp to give a gain $K_c = 10$. The setpoint is supplied as a **0–10 V** signal and the PV feedback is a **0–10 V** signal. The MV output must also be in **0–10 V**. Show component values and verify the op-amp bandwidth is sufficient. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Choose the topology.** Use an **inverting summing amplifier** for the summing action, then a **second inverting unity-gain stage** to restore the sign. This gives an overall non-inverting P-controller with two inputs. **Step 2 — Set the input resistors.** For the summing stage, choose equal input resistors so that both SP and PV have equal weight: $$R_1 = R_2 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$$ **Step 3 — Calculate the feedback resistor for the desired gain.** The first (summing) stage has gain $-K_c/2$ if we want each input to contribute half the total. To get an overall $K_c = 10$ (after the sign-restoring second stage), the first stage gain magnitude should be $10$: $$R_f = K_c \cdot R_{in} = 10 \times 10\,\text{k}\Omega = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$$ **Step 4 — Bias (m₀) addition.** Add a third input to the summing junction, connected to a **potentiometer** (wiper) tied to a stable reference (e.g., $+5\,\text{V}$ zener), with $R_3 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$. This adds a tunable DC offset $m_0$. **Step 5 — Check bandwidth.** For a $\mu$A741, $GBW \approx 1\,\text{MHz}$. With $K_c = 10$, the closed-loop bandwidth is: $$f_{BW} = \frac{GBW}{K_c} = \frac{1\,\text{MHz}}{10} = 100\,\text{kHz}$$ Since process dynamics are typically below **10 Hz**, this is more than adequate (3–4 decades of headroom). **Step 6 — Power supplies.** Use symmetrical supplies: $\pm 15\,\text{V}$. The output swing will be limited to approximately **±13 V** (with 2 V headroom), comfortably covering the required 0–10 V range. **Step 7 — Output saturation guard.** Add back-to-back Zener diodes (e.g., 10 V) at the output to clamp MV to the 0–10 V range, protecting downstream actuators. ### Result Summary | Component | Value | Purpose | |---|---|---| | $R_1, R_2$ | $10\,\text{k}\Omega$ | Input summing resistors | | $R_3$ | $10\,\text{k}\Omega$ (pot) | Bias adjustment | | $R_f$ | $100\,\text{k}\Omega$ | Feedback → sets $K_c = 10$ | | Op-amp | $\mu$A741 × 2 | Summing + sign-restoring | | Zener clamp | 10 V | Output protection | | Power | $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ | Op-amp supply | ## 3.4 Worked Example — LabVIEW Block Diagram Logic **Problem:** Sketch the LabVIEW block diagram logic to simultaneously monitor $V_{PV}$ (CV) and $V_{MV}$ (MV) on a single waveform chart with two plots, sampling at **1 kHz** for **30 seconds**. **Solution Logic (in pseudo-graphical form):** ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ DAQ Assistant (AI0 = V_PV, AI1 = V_MV) │ │ │ │ │ ├──► "Index Array" (ch 0) ──► Waveform Chart │ │ │ (CV trace) (Plot 0: "CV (V)") │ │ │ │ │ └──► "Index Array" (ch 1) ──► Waveform Chart │ │ (MV trace) (Plot 1: "MV (V)") │ │ │ │ Both channels ► Build Array ► Write to .tdms File │ │ │ │ While Loop with 1 ms Wait (1000 Hz) │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` **Key LabVIEW Nodes Used:** - **DAQmx Create Virtual Channel** (Analog Input → Voltage) - **DAQmx Start Task / Read / Stop Task** - **Index Array** to split the 2-channel waveform into individual scalars. - **Build Array** to merge the two scalars into a 1D array. - **Write to Measurement File** (Express VI) for logging. - **Waveform Chart** with two plots, each assigned a distinct color (e.g., red for CV, blue for MV). --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete P-Controller Feedback Loop with LabVIEW Monitoring ```mermaid flowchart LR SP[Setpoint SP]:::src --> SUM1((⊕ Sum)):::sum PV_meas[Measured PV]:::src --> SUM1 SUM1 -->|Error e| OPAMP[Op-Amp P-Controller
Kc = Rf / Rin]:::ctrl OPAMP -->|MV m| FCE[Final Control Element
Valve / Heater]:::fce FCE -->|Actuation| PROC[Process Plant]:::proc PROC -->|Temperature/Pressure/Flow| SENSOR[Sensor + Transmitter
0-10 V]:::sense SENSOR --> PV_meas OPAMP -.->|Analog voltage| DAQ[NI DAQ Card
USB-6008/6009]:::daq SENSOR -.->|Analog voltage| DAQ DAQ -->|USB| LABVIEW[LabVIEW Virtual Instrument]:::lab LABVIEW --> MON[Real-Time Charts
CV and MV Traces]:::disp LABVIEW --> LOG[Data Logging
.tdms / .lvm File]:::log classDef src fill:#FFE082,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef sum fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ctrl fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef fce fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef proc fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef sense fill:#FFCC80,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef daq fill:#B0BEC5,stroke:#37474F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef lab fill:#80DEEA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef disp fill:#F48FB1,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef log fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Op-Amp Inverting Summing Amplifier — Detailed Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SUMMING[Summing Inverting Amplifier - First Stage] VSP[+V_SP
0 to 10 V]:::in --> R1[R1 = 10 kOhm]:::r VPV[-V_PV
0 to 10 V]:::in --> R2[R2 = 10 kOhm]:::r VREF[V_REF
Bias via Pot]:::in --> R3[R3 = 10 kOhm Pot]:::r R1 --> N1((Node A
Virtual GND)):::gnd R2 --> N1 R3 --> N1 N1 --> INV[Inverting Input -]:::op N1 -.Feedback.-> Rf[Rf = 100 kOhm]:::r Rf --> V1[Stage 1 Output
-Kc times error]:::opout V1GND[Ground]:::gnd --> NON[Non-Inverting Input +]:::op V1 --> OP1[Op-Amp uA741]:::op end subgraph SIGNRESTORE[Sign-Restoring Inverting Stage - Unity Gain] V1 --> R4[R4 = 10 kOhm]:::r R4 --> N2((Node B
Virtual GND)):::gnd N2 --> INV2[Inverting Input -]:::op N2 -.Feedback.-> R5[R5 = 10 kOhm]:::r R5 --> V2[Final MV Output
0 to 10 V]:::opout V2GND[Ground]:::gnd --> NON2[Non-Inverting Input +]:::op V1 --> OP2[Op-Amp uA741]:::op end classDef in fill:#FFE082,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef r fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef gnd fill:#B0BEC5,stroke:#37474F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef op fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef opout fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 LabVIEW Virtual Instrument — Front Panel Mock-up ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FP[LabVIEW Front Panel - Virtual Instrument] SP_CTRL[Setpoint Knob
Numeric Control 0 to 100]:::ctrl2 KC_CTRL[Proportional Gain Slider

to maximum]:::sig B --> C[MV instantly jumps
to Kc times e]:::sig C --> D[Process responds
PV rises exponentially]:::sig D --> E[Error decreases]:::sig E --> F{PV reaches SP?}:::dec F --|Yes Pure P| G[Steady State Offset
