final offset value]:::out classDef event fill:#FFE082,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef sig fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef dec fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef warn fill:#EF9A9A,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef out fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define proportional band. How is it related to proportional gain? [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model QP] **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Proportional Band (PB)** is defined as the *range of error (expressed as a percentage of the transmitter's calibrated span) over which the controller output travels through its full operating range*. > > The relationship with proportional gain $K_c$ is **inversely proportional**: > > $$PB\,(\%) = \frac{100}{K_c} \times 100 = \frac{10000}{K_c}$$ > > **Example:** If $K_c = 4$, then $PB = 10000 / 4 = 2500\,\%$ — meaning a 25% error change drives the output through its full range. Conversely, if $K_c = 50$, then $PB = 200\,\%$ — only a 2% error change is needed. > > **Industrial significance:** A small PB (high gain) gives tight, aggressive control but risks oscillation. A large PB (low gain) gives smooth, sluggish control. The choice is a trade-off based on process dynamics and desired performance. --- ### Q2. What is steady-state offset in a proportional controller? Why does it occur? [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model QP] **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Steady-state offset** is the **non-zero error** that persists between the setpoint and the controlled variable after a load disturbance or setpoint change, when the loop has settled. > > **Why it occurs:** For a pure P-controller, $m = K_c \cdot e + m_0$. At steady state, the controller output $m$ must reach a *new* constant value to counteract the disturbance. Since $m \propto e$, the error $e$ must be non-zero to sustain this new $m$. Hence, $PV \neq SP$ at steady state. > > **Remedy:** Add **integral (I) action** — a PI controller integrates the error over time, driving it eventually to zero. The integral term keeps changing the output as long as any error exists, eliminating offset (at the cost of slower response and possible overshoot). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) **Q A. (a)** Design an electronic proportional controller using an op-amp inverting summing configuration to realize a gain $K_c = 25$. The setpoint and PV signals are both in the range 0–10 V. Specify all component values, draw the circuit diagram, and derive the output voltage expression. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Explain how LabVIEW can be used to monitor both the controlled variable (PV) and the manipulating variable (MV) in real time. List the key hardware and software components required, and describe the front-panel layout. **[7 marks]** #### Model Solution **(a) Op-Amp P-Controller Design [7 marks]** **Step 1 — Topology choice [1 mark]:** We use a **two-stage op-amp circuit**: - **Stage 1:** Inverting summing amplifier (computes the negative of the error). - **Stage 2:** Unity-gain inverter (restores the sign, giving overall non-inverting P-action). **Step 2 — Choose input resistors [1 mark]:** Set $R_1 = R_2 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ (equal weights for SP and PV). **Step 3 — Feedback resistor for $K_c = 25$ [2 marks]:** The first stage must produce a gain of $-25$ between its input and output: $$R_f = K_c \cdot R_1 = 25 \times 10\,\text{k}\Omega = 250\,\text{k}\Omega$$ **Step 4 — Output equation [2 marks]:** Stage 1 output: $$V_1 = -\left[\frac{R_f}{R_1} V_{SP} + \frac{R_f}{R_2} V_{PV}\right] = -25\,(V_{SP} + V_{PV})$$ Stage 2 (unity-gain inverting) output: $$V_{MV} = -V_1 = 25\,(V_{SP} - V_{PV}) = 25\,e$$ **Step 5 — Verification [1 mark]:** For $V_{SP} = 5\,\text{V}$, $V_{PV} = 3\,\text{V}$: $V_{MV} = 25 \times 2 = 50\,\text{V}$. ⚠️ **This exceeds the op-amp supply! The output will saturate at $\sim 13.5\,\text{V}$.** This illustrates that **practical op-amps cannot realize arbitrarily high $K_c$** without output saturation; industrial controllers add saturation limits and anti-windup. **(b) LabVIEW Monitoring [7 marks]** **Hardware components [2 marks]:** - **NI DAQ card** (e.g., NI USB-6008/6009) — 8 or 16 analog inputs, 12 or 14-bit resolution, ±10 V range. - **Signal conditioning** — voltage dividers or instrumentation amplifiers to scale op-amp outputs to DAQ-safe levels. - **PC** running LabVIEW (any version from 2018 onwards). **Software components [2 marks]:** - **NI-DAQmx driver** — driver-level interface. - **LabVIEW VIs** — DAQ Assistant Express VI for acquisition, Waveform Chart for display, Write-to-File for logging. - **VISA drivers** — for serial/USB communication if using Arduino-based ADC. **Front Panel layout [2 marks]:** - Two **numeric indicators** for CV and MV. - A **dual-plot waveform chart** showing CV (red) and MV (blue) vs. time. - **Start/Stop** Boolean buttons. - **Setpoint and $K_c$** numeric controls. - A **status LED** indicating DAQ status. - An **offset numeric indicator** (SP − PV). **Procedure [1 mark]:** 1. Connect op-amp outputs to DAQ AI0 (PV) and AI1 (MV). 2. Configure DAQmx task for 2-channel analog input at 1 kHz. 3. Run the While Loop, acquire, split, display, and log data. 4. After the experiment, analyze the .tdms file in DIAdem or Excel. --- ### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **Q B. (a)** Derive the expression for steady-state offset in a unity-feedback system with a proportional controller subjected to a step load disturbance. Discuss the role of controller gain in reducing offset and the practical limit beyond which the system becomes unstable. **[7 marks]** **(b)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the complete experimental setup for designing a P-controller and monitoring its performance. List at least **five precautions** to be taken during the experiment. **[7 marks]** #### Model Solution **(a) Steady-State Offset Derivation [7 marks]** **Step 1 — System block diagram and equations [1 mark]:** Let the process be $G_p(s)$ and the disturbance enter as $D(s)$ at the process input. The error equation is: $$E(s) = SP(s) - PV(s) = SP(s) - G_p(s)\,[M(s) + D(s)]$$ With $M(s) = K_c E(s)$: $$E(s) = SP(s) - G_p(s)\,[K_c E(s) + D(s)]$$ **Step 2 — Solve for E(s) [2 marks]:** $$E(s)\,[1 + K_c G_p(s)] = SP(s) - G_p(s) D(s)$$ $$E(s) = \frac{SP(s)}{1 + K_c G_p(s)} - \frac{G_p(s) D(s)}{1 + K_c G_p(s)}$$ **Step 3 — Apply Final Value Theorem for step disturbance $D(s) = D_0 / s$, with $SP = 0$ [2 marks]:** $$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \left[\frac{-G_p(s) D_0/s}{1 + K_c G_p(s)}\right] = \frac{-D_0 \, G_p(0)}{1 + K_c G_p(0)}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $G_p(0) = K_p$ [1 mark]:** $$\boxed{\vert e_{ss} \vert = \frac{\vert D_0 \vert \, K_p}{1 + K_c K_p}}$$ **Step 5 — Discussion [1 mark]:** - As $K_c \to \infty$, $e_{ss} \to 0$. - However, large $K_c$ reduces **phase margin** (the Nyquist plot crosses the critical $-1$ point) and the system **oscillates or becomes unstable**. - The **practical optimum $K_c$** corresponds to a **damping ratio $\zeta \approx 0.7$** (Ziegler-Nichols ultimate gain method). **(b) Experimental Setup and Precautions [7 marks]** **Block diagram [3 marks]:** (Refer to Section 4.1 — Complete feedback loop with LabVIEW monitoring) The setup includes: - Setpoint signal generator (potentiometer tied to a stable 10 V reference). - Sensor + transmitter (e.g., RTD + bridge, or LVDT for position). - Op-amp P-controller circuit on a breadboard. - Final control element (heater for thermal process, or motor-pump assembly). - Process (water bath, tank, or position-control rig). - NI DAQ card → PC with LabVIEW. **Five Precautions [2 marks each, total 4 marks]:** 1. **Power supply sequencing:** Always connect op-amp power supplies (±15 V) *before* applying input signals to prevent latch-up damage. 2. **Input voltage limits:** Ensure all op-amp inputs stay within the common-mode range (typically $\pm 12$ V for $\pm 15$ V supply) to avoid phase reversal. 3. **Output clamping:** Use Zener diode clamps to protect actuators from over-voltage during large transients. 4. **Shielded cables:** Use shielded twisted pairs for sensor and MV signal lines to reject EMI from lab equipment (motors, fluorescent lights). 5. **Single-point grounding:** Ground the op-amp circuit and the DAQ card at a *single common point* to avoid ground loops that introduce 50 Hz noise. 6. **Calibration:** Calibrate the DAQ analog input channels with a precision voltmeter before starting the experiment. 7. **Sample-rate selection:** Choose a sample rate at least **10× faster** than the fastest process dynamics to avoid aliasing. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:** > > 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in op-amp gain.** Students often write $V_{out} = (R_f/R_{in})V_{in}$ instead of $V_{out} = -(R_f/R_{in})V_{in}$. This is an **instant 1-mark deduction**. > > 2. **Confusing "controlled variable" and "manipulating variable."** CV/PV is the *output of the process* (what you measure); MV is the *output of the controller* (what you command). Examiners specifically test this vocabulary. > > 3. **Saying "LabVIEW is a programming language."** It is a **graphical programming environment / dataflow language** with VIs as the unit of code. Mark penalty for imprecise terminology. > > 4. **Skipping units in numerical answers.** Always state the unit (V, kHz, %, °C) explicitly. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for unit omissions. > > 5. **Not mentioning the existence of steady-state offset.** Almost any question on P-controllers implicitly asks about offset. *Always include a sentence on this limitation in your answer.* > > 6. **Drawing the op-amp with feedback on the wrong terminal.** Feedback MUST go to the *inverting* (−) input for negative feedback. Drawing it to the non-inverting (+) input will be marked wrong. > > 7. **Confusing Proportional Band and Proportional Gain.** PB is **inversely** related to $K_c$, not directly. Writing $PB = K_c$ loses a mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before the Exam** > > ✅ **Core Equation:** $m(t) = K_c \cdot e(t) + m_0$ > > ✅ **Error Definition:** $e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)$ > > ✅ **Op-Amp Gain Formula:** $V_{out} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}} V_{in}$ (inverting) > > ✅ **Proportional Band Relation:** $PB\,(\%) = \dfrac{10000}{K_c}$ > > ✅ **Steady-State Offset:** $\vert e_{ss} \vert = \dfrac{\vert D_0 \vert \, K_p}{1 + K_c K_p}$ → offset is **inversely proportional** to $K_c$. > > ✅ **Key Limitation of P-Control:** Cannot eliminate offset → motivates PI/PID. > > ✅ **LabVIEW = NI product**, graphical dataflow language, programs are called **VIs** (Virtual Instruments) with **Front Panel + Block Diagram**. > > ✅ **DAQ Hardware:** NI USB-6008/6009 typical, ±10 V range, 12–14-bit ADC, ≥1 kHz sampling for process labs. > > ✅ **Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$; in practice, $f_s \geq 10 f_{max}$. > > ✅ **Standard I/O Ranges:** 0–10 V, 4–20 mA (industrial), ±10 V (lab). > > ✅ **Op-Amp Standard:** $\mu$A741 (GBW ≈ 1 MHz, ±15 V supply, output swing ≈ ±13 V). > > ✅ **Practical Component Values:** $R_{in} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ is the textbook standard; $R_f = K_c \cdot R_{in}$. > > ✅ **Two-Stage Design:** Summing inverter → Unity-gain inverter (sign restore). > > ✅ **Precautions Checklist:** Power sequencing, common-mode limits, output clamping, shielding, single-point grounding. > > ✅ **Typical LabVIEW Display:** Dual-plot waveform chart — **CV in red, MV in blue** (memorize this convention). > > ✅ **Exam Mantra:** *Pure P-control = fast but offset.* *PI = slower but zero offset.* *PID = optimal but tuning-sensitive.*

topics.title: Design of electronic proportional plus integral controller using OPAMP and monitoring both controlled variable and manipulating variable using LabVIEW
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL607 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design of electronic proportional plus integral controller using OPAMP and monitoring both controlled variable and manipulating variable using LabVIEW

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PI Controller (Proportional-Integral Controller):** A PI controller is a feedback control mechanism that computes a control action as a linear combination of the **error signal** (proportional action) and the **time integral of the error signal** (integral action). The electronic realization using **operational amplifiers (Op-Amps)** yields a compact, low-cost analog computing element widely used in process control loops before the advent of digital controllers.

The PI controller output in the **time domain** is mathematically expressed as:

$$m(t) = K_c \, e(t) + \frac{K_c}{T_i} \int_0^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau$$

In the **Laplace / s-domain**, the transfer function of an ideal PI controller is:

$$G_c(s) = \frac{M(s)}{E(s)} = K_c \left( 1 + \frac{1}{T_i \, s} \right)$$

Where:
- $K_c$ = Proportional Gain (dimensionless, also called controller gain)
- $T_i$ = Integral Time / Reset Time (seconds)
- $e(t)$ = Error signal = Set Point (SP) − Process Variable (PV)

## 1.2 The Three Signals in a Control Loop

| Signal | Symbol | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Set Point** | SP / r(t) | Desired value (reference) |
| **Process Variable** | PV / y(t) | Controlled variable (measured output) |
| **Manipulated Variable** | MV / m(t) | Controller output → final control element |

> [!NOTE]
> In the context of KTU Lab Experiment 2, the **controlled variable** is the process output being regulated (e.g., temperature, level, voltage across a plant), and the **manipulated variable** is the **op-amp controller output voltage** that drives the plant.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Cruise Control Analogy:** Imagine you are driving a car and want to maintain exactly **60 km/h**.
> - **Proportional Action (P):** If you are at 65 km/h, you lift the accelerator slightly; if at 55 km/h, you press it harder. The harder you press (larger error), the more you compensate. But pressing proportional to error alone leaves a steady **offset** (you may settle at 58 km/h instead of 60).
> - **Integral Action (I):** You keep "remembering" the past error. If 58 km/h persisted for too long, you gradually press the accelerator more and more until the offset is eliminated. **The integral term eliminates steady-state error.**
> - **The Op-Amp PI circuit** is the electronic "driver" that mathematically computes this P+I action using resistors and a capacitor in a feedback network.

## 1.4 The Role of LabVIEW in This Experiment

**LabVIEW (Laboratory Virtual Instrument Engineering Workbench)** by **National Instruments (NI)** is a graphical programming environment used to:
- **Acquire** the controlled variable (PV) through a **DAQ (Data Acquisition) card** or **USB-compatible ADC module**.
- **Acquire** the manipulating variable (MV = op-amp controller output).
- **Display** both signals in real time using **waveform charts** on a virtual front panel.
- **Log** data to file for offline analysis (calculation of peak overshoot, settling time, steady-state error, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme - PCICL607, Module 2):** The student must *design, build, test, and demonstrate* a working op-amp PI controller and *interface* the controlled variable and manipulating variable to a PC running LabVIEW for online monitoring. The lab record must include the circuit diagram, component calculation, observed waveforms/responses, and inferred performance metrics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why PI and not P or PID?

| Controller | Transfer Function | Steady-State Error | Drawback |
|---|---|---|---|
| P only | $K_c$ | Non-zero (offset) | Cannot eliminate offset |
| **PI** | $K_c\left(1+\frac{1}{T_i s}\right)$ | **Zero for step input** | Slower response |
| PID | $K_c\left(1+\frac{1}{T_i s}+T_d s\right)$ | Zero | Noise amplification by D term |

> [!NOTE]
> The **PI controller is the workhorse of process industries** (flow, level, temperature loops) because it eliminates offset and does not suffer from the noise sensitivity of the derivative term.

## 2.2 Op-Amp Realization of an Inverting PI Controller

The most common implementation is the **inverting PI configuration** using a single op-amp (e.g., **µA741, LM741, TL081, LM358**).

### 2.2.1 Circuit Topology

- **Input branch:** Resistor $R_1$ from input voltage $V_{in}(t)$ to the inverting terminal.
- **Feedback branch:** Resistor $R_f$ in **parallel** with capacitor $C_f$ from output to inverting terminal.
- **Non-inverting input:** Grounded.

### 2.2.2 Derivation of Output Equation

Applying the **ideal op-amp assumptions**:
- No current into input terminals ($i^+ = i^- = 0$)
- Virtual short: $V^+ = V^-$ ⇒ $V^- = 0$ (virtual ground)

The current through $R_1$ is:

$$i_1 = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

The current through the feedback network is:

$$i_f = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{Z_f}$$

where $Z_f$ is the parallel combination of $R_f$ and $C_f$ in the s-domain:

$$Z_f(s) = R_f \parallel \frac{1}{C_f s} = \frac{R_f}{1 + R_f C_f s}$$

By **KCL at the inverting node** ($i_1 = i_f$):

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = -\frac{V_{out}}{Z_f(s)}$$

Solving for $V_{out}/V_{in}$:

$$G_c(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = -\frac{Z_f(s)}{R_1} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + R_f C_f s}$$

This is the transfer function of a **first-order lag + proportional** action (real PI). Substituting $\tau_f = R_f C_f$:

$$G_c(s) = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \tau_f s}$$

### 2.2.3 Conversion to Standard PI Form

To obtain a true **PI form** $K_c(1 + 1/(T_i s))$, the above must be rewritten. Expanding:

$$G_c(s) = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + R_f C_f s} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1 \cdot R_f C_f} \cdot \frac{1}{s + 1/(R_f C_f)}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator appropriately, the standard **ideal PI form** is obtained when a **true integrator** is realized using the configuration with $R_f$ removed and only $C_f$ in feedback (pure I action), or using the **Miller integrator with input resistor** for the I term in parallel with a P-gain block.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU lab purposes, the "Pseudo-PI" form** $G_c(s) = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}\cdot\frac{1}{1+R_f C_f s}$ is acceptable and is the most commonly tested circuit. The low-frequency gain (which determines the integral action) is $K_c = R_f/R_1$, and the integral time constant is approximated as $T_i \approx R_f C_f$ for $\omega \gg 1/T_i$.

## 2.3 Step Response of a Pure PI Controller

For a step input of magnitude $V_{in}$ applied at $t=0$:

$$V_{out}(t) = -K_c V_{in} \left[ 1 - e^{-t/(R_f C_f)} \right] \;\; \text{(pseudo-PI lag form)}$$

For an **ideal PI** (capacitor only in feedback, no $R_f$):

$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{V_{in}}{R_1 C_f} \cdot t - \frac{R_f}{R_1} V_{in}$$

The output is a **linear ramp** superimposed on a proportional jump — the classical "PI step response" with no steady-state limit (saturates at supply rails).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Exam — Memorize This Table**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PI Output (time) | $m(t) = K_c e(t) + \frac{K_c}{T_i}\int e\, dt$ | V or % | Standard form |
| 2 | PI Transfer Function | $G_c(s) = K_c\left(1 + \frac{1}{T_i s}\right)$ | — | Ideal PI |
| 3 | Zero Frequency Gain | $K_c = R_f / R_1$ | dimensionless | Op-amp design |
| 4 | Integral Time | $T_i = R_f \cdot C_f$ | seconds | Op-amp design |
| 5 | Reset Rate | $1/T_i$ | 1/s | Repeats per minute ⇒ $1/T_i \times 60$ |
| 6 | Error Signal | $e(t) = SP - PV$ | V or engineering unit | Signed convention |
| 7 | Closed-Loop Steady-State Error (PI, step) | $e_{ss} = 0$ | — | Integral action eliminates offset |
| 8 | Closed-Loop Steady-State Error (PI, ramp) | $e_{ss} = \text{const}/K_i$ | — | Non-zero for ramp |
| 9 | Closed-Loop Char. Eq. (unity feedback) | $1 + G_c(s)G_p(s) = 0$ | — | For stability analysis |
| 10 | DAQ Sampling Rate (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz | LabVIEW data logging |

> [!WARNING]
> In the inverting op-amp PI, the output is **inverted** (180° phase shift). For non-inverting PI (used when polarity matters), add a unity-gain inverter at the output or use the non-inverting PI topology (advanced topic — usually out of KTU syllabus scope).

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial applications of op-amp PI controllers** (still taught and used):
> - Analog temperature controllers (legacy systems, lab trainers)
> - Motor speed control loops
> - Op-amp-based function generators (PI networks set envelope)
> - Active filters where the PI transfer function defines the low-frequency gain
> - Foundation for understanding **electronic analog computing** of differential equations (used in early 1960s flight simulators)

**LabVIEW-based monitoring** bridges analog and digital domains — it is the de-facto standard in modern labs for online visualization, data logging, and supervisory control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Design Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numerical)

> **Given:** Design an electronic PI controller using op-amp µA741 with:
> - Proportional gain $K_c = 4$
> - Integral time $T_i = 1 \text{ second}$
> - Input voltage range: ±10 V
> - Output voltage range: ±12 V (op-amp supply)
>
> **Required:** Calculate $R_1$, $R_f$, $C_f$. Verify the transfer function. Sketch the step response. Suggest a LabVIEW DAQ channel mapping.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Component Calculation

### Step 1: Choose $R_1$ based on input bias current and loading

A standard design choice is to keep input resistance in the range **10 kΩ to 100 kΩ** to limit bias-current errors but not load the source. Let us pick:

$$R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$$

### Step 2: Compute $R_f$ from $K_c$

Using the design equation:

$$K_c = \frac{R_f}{R_1} \implies R_f = K_c \cdot R_1 = 4 \times 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 40 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Use the nearest standard E12 value: $R_f = 39 \text{ k}\Omega$ (acceptable tolerance).

### Step 3: Compute $C_f$ from $T_i$

Using the design equation:

$$T_i = R_f \cdot C_f \implies C_f = \frac{T_i}{R_f} = \frac{1}{40 \times 10^3} = 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F} = 25 \, \mu\text{F}$$

Use the nearest standard value: $C_f = 22 \, \mu\text{F}$ (tantalum or electrolytic, polarized — observe polarity with op-amp output polarity).

### Step 4: Verify Transfer Function

$$G_c(s) = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + R_f C_f s} = -\frac{40\text{k}}{10\text{k}} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + (40\text{k})(25\mu)s}$$

Simplifying:

$$G_c(s) = -4 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + 1.0 \, s} = \frac{-4}{1 + s}$$

The low-frequency gain is **−4** (matches $K_c$) and the time constant is **1 s** (matches $T_i$).

### Step 5: Step Response Verification

For a unit step input $V_{in}(s) = 1/s$:

$$V_{out}(s) = G_c(s) \cdot V_{in}(s) = \frac{-4}{1+s} \cdot \frac{1}{s}$$

Performing partial fractions:

$$V_{out}(s) = -4 \left( \frac{1}{s} - \frac{1}{s+1} \right)$$

Inverse Laplace transform:

$$V_{out}(t) = -4 \left( 1 - e^{-t} \right) \cdot u(t)$$

At $t=0$: $V_{out}(0) = 0$ (capacitor initially uncharged).
At $t \to \infty$: $V_{out} \to -4$ V (steady-state DC gain).
Time to reach 63.2% of final value: $t = T_i = 1$ s.

```
V_out (V)
  0 |..  ___________________ -4 V
    |  /
-2  | /
    |/
    +------------------- t (s)
   0   1   2   3
```

### Step 6: LabVIEW Channel Mapping

| Signal | Physical Origin | DAQ Channel (NI USB-6009 example) | LabVIEW Indicator |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Controlled Variable (PV)** | Plant output / sensor (e.g., LM35 temperature) | **AI0** (Analog Input, single-ended) | Waveform Chart "PV" |
| **Manipulating Variable (MV)** | Op-amp PI controller output | **AI1** (Analog Input, single-ended) | Waveform Chart "MV" |
| **Set Point (SP)** | User-entered numeric on front panel | (Internal, no DAQ needed) | Numeric Control "SP" |
| **Error Display** | Computed inside LabVIEW: $e = \text{SP} - \text{PV}$ | (Internal) | Numeric Indicator "Error" |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing Op-Amp

| Parameter | µA741 | LM358 | TL081 | OP07 | Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Supply | ±18 V max | ±16 V / +32 V single | ±18 V | ±18 V | — |
| Slew Rate | 0.5 V/µs | 0.3 V/µs | 13 V/µs | 0.3 V/µs | TL081 fastest |
| Input Impedance | 2 MΩ | 10 MΩ | $10^{12}$ Ω | 60 MΩ | TL081 best |
| Offset Voltage | 6 mV | 7 mV | 15 mV | 0.15 mV | OP07 lowest |
| Cost | Low | Very Low | Low | Medium | — |
| **KTU Lab Typical** | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ (precision) | **µA741 or LM358** |

> [!NOTE]
> For DC lab work (low-frequency temperature, level, slow voltage control), the **µA741** is sufficient. The slew rate of 0.5 V/µs means it can swing 5 V in 10 µs — adequate for time constants of 0.1 s and above.

## 3.4 Procedure Summary (for Lab Record)

1. **Breadboard** the inverting PI circuit with computed $R_1$, $R_f$, $C_f$.
2. **Power** the op-amp with ±12 V (or ±15 V) dual supply; verify the output offset is near zero with input grounded.
3. **Apply** a step input (e.g., +1 V from a function generator or DC source) and observe the op-amp output on an oscilloscope or DMM. Note the rising exponential, confirm $K_c$ and $T_i$.
4. **Connect** the controlled variable and controller output to **two analog input channels** of a DAQ device (e.g., NI USB-6008/6009, or a low-cost USB ADC like the **Pmod AD2** or **USB-1608FS**).
5. **Build a LabVIEW VI** with:
   - **Front Panel:** Two Waveform Charts (PV vs time, MV vs time), numeric controls for SP, $K_c$, $T_i$.
   - **Block Diagram:** DAQ Assistant → Split Signals → Display; optional **Simulate Signal** for the plant if no real plant is connected.
6. **Run the closed loop** (manual or simulated plant) and observe the **tracking, overshoot, settling time, and steady-state error** on LabVIEW charts.
7. **Save** the front panel screenshot and exported data file as `.lvm` or `.csv` for the lab record.

## 4.4 Typical Closed-Loop Step Response Curves

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0["t = 0 step SP applied"] --> T1["t < T_i MV rises exponentially MV = Kc e 1 minus e power minus t over Ti"]
    T1 --> T2["t approx T_i PV begins to track SP"]
    T2 --> T3["PV approaches SP with small overshoot less than 20 percent"]
    T3 --> T4["t = t_s settling time PV within 2 percent of SP"]
    T4 --> T5["t > t_s steady state PV = SP and ess = 0"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Response:** The **integral action is the "long memory"** — the controller keeps pushing the MV as long as the error is non-zero. This is what drives the **steady-state error to zero**. If you observe a persistent offset between SP and PV in your experiment, the **integral action is not strong enough** (increase $1/T_i$ by reducing $C_f$ or $R_f$).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]

**State the transfer function of a Proportional-Integral (PI) controller and explain the physical significance of the two terms.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The PI controller transfer function is:

$$G_c(s) = K_c \left( 1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} \right) = K_c + \frac{K_c}{T_i s}$$

The **proportional term** $K_c$ provides an output proportional to the **instantaneous error** — gives fast response but cannot eliminate steady-state error. The **integral term** $\frac{K_c}{T_i s}$ provides an output proportional to the **accumulated (time-integrated) error** — eliminates steady-state error but can cause overshoot and slow response. (1 mark for equation, 1 mark for P term, 1 mark for I term.)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]

**List the components required to realize a PI controller using a single op-amp and draw its circuit.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Components: One op-amp (e.g., µA741), one input resistor $R_1$, one feedback resistor $R_f$, one feedback capacitor $C_f$, and a dual DC power supply (±12 V or ±15 V). (1 mark) The inverting input receives the error signal through $R_1$; the non-inverting input is grounded. Feedback network is $R_f$ in **parallel** with $C_f$ between output and inverting input. (1 mark for topology, 1 mark for formula $K_c = R_f/R_1$ and $T_i = R_f C_f$.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**(a)** Derive the transfer function of an **inverting PI controller** realized using one op-amp, with input resistor $R_1$ and feedback network $R_f \parallel C_f$. (7 marks)

**(b)** For a process control trainer, design the PI controller with $K_c = 5$ and $T_i = 2$ s. Take $R_1 = 10$ kΩ. Calculate $R_f$ and $C_f$ and sketch the unit step response. Explain how you would connect the **controlled variable** and **manipulating variable** to a LabVIEW-monitored DAQ system. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

For the inverting PI op-amp, the inverting input is a **virtual ground** ($V^- = 0$). Apply **KCL** at this node:

$$i_1 + i_f = 0$$

Current through $R_1$ (from input to virtual ground):

$$i_1 = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

Current through feedback network (from virtual ground to output):

$$i_f = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{Z_f(s)}$$

where $Z_f(s)$ is the parallel combination:

$$Z_f(s) = R_f \parallel \frac{1}{C_f s} = \frac{R_f \cdot \frac{1}{C_f s}}{R_f + \frac{1}{C_f s}} = \frac{R_f}{1 + R_f C_f s}$$

Substituting KCL:

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} + \frac{-V_{out}}{Z_f(s)} = 0 \implies V_{out} = -\frac{Z_f(s)}{R_1} \cdot V_{in}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{G_c(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + R_f C_f s}}$$

Identifying $K_c = R_f/R_1$ and the pole at $s = -1/(R_f C_f)$ (or equivalently $T_i \approx R_f C_f$ for low frequencies), this realizes the lag-compensated (pseudo) PI action. (2 marks for KCL setup, 2 marks for $Z_f$ derivation, 1 mark for final $G_c(s)$, 1 mark for identification of $K_c$ and $T_i$, 1 mark for correct sign / inversion.)

---

**(b) Design (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $R_f$:**

$$K_c = \frac{R_f}{R_1} \implies R_f = K_c \cdot R_1 = 5 \times 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Use standard $R_f = 47$ kΩ (1 mark).

**Step 2 — Compute $C_f$:**

$$T_i = R_f \cdot C_f \implies C_f = \frac{T_i}{R_f} = \frac{2}{50 \times 10^3} = 40 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F} = 40 \, \mu\text{F}$$

Use standard $C_f = 47 \, \mu\text{F}$ (tantalum, observe polarity) (1 mark).

**Step 3 — Verify Transfer Function:**

$$G_c(s) = -\frac{50\text{k}}{10\text{k}} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + (50\text{k})(40\mu) s} = \frac{-5}{1 + 2s}$$

(1 mark for verification.)

**Step 4 — Sketch the Step Response:**

For unit step input, $V_{out}(t) = -5(1 - e^{-t/2})$ V.

| t (s) | $V_{out}$ (V) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0 |
| 1 | −1.97 |
| 2 | −3.16 |
| 4 | −4.32 |
| $\infty$ | −5.00 |

(1 mark for sketch with correct time constant and final value.)

**Step 5 — LabVIEW Interfacing:**

- Connect the **plant output (controlled variable, PV)** to **DAQ AI0** (single-ended analog input) using shielded twisted pair; scale using the DAQ Assistant "Custom Scaling" function (e.g., 0–10 V → 0–100 °C for a temperature plant).
- Connect the **op-amp PI output (manipulating variable, MV)** to **DAQ AI1**.
- Build a LabVIEW VI with two **Waveform Charts** — one for PV vs time, one for MV vs time.
- Add a **Numeric Control** for the Set Point and a **Numeric Indicator** for the computed error $e = \text{SP} - \text{PV}$.
- Use a **While Loop** with a **Wait (ms)** function to set the sampling rate (e.g., 100 ms for $f_s = 10$ Hz).
- Use **Write to Measurement File** to save data as `.lvm` for offline analysis (settling time, overshoot, steady-state error). (2 marks for block diagram description, 1 mark for DAQ channel selection.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional operation of a closed-loop process control system** with a PI controller. Identify the controlled variable, manipulated variable, and error signal. (7 marks)

**(b)** A temperature control loop uses a PI controller with $K_c = 2$ and $T_i = 5$ min. The set point is changed from 80 °C to 100 °C. Explain the **role of integral action** in eliminating the steady-state error. Also describe how you would use **LabVIEW** to monitor the temperature PV and the controller MV in real time, including the typical front-panel indicators you would include. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) (7 marks):**

A closed-loop control system has four primary elements: **(i) Controller (PI op-amp circuit), (ii) Final Control Element (heater, valve, motor), (iii) Plant (process whose temperature/level/flow is being controlled), and (iv) Sensor (LM35, RTD, LVDT, etc.)**. (1 mark for block diagram, 1 mark for each block description = 4 marks, 2 marks for signal identification.)

**Signal Flow:**
1. **Set Point (SP)** — desired value, entered by the user (e.g., 100 °C).
2. **Error (e)** = SP − PV, computed in the summing junction.
3. **Controller Output (MV)** = op-amp PI output voltage (e.g., 0–10 V).
4. **Final Control Element** converts MV to process energy (heater wattage, valve opening).
5. **Plant** — physical process (water tank, furnace).
6. **Process Variable (PV)** — measured output (temperature, level, flow).
7. **Sensor** — converts PV to an electrical signal (voltage) and feeds it back to the summing junction.

The loop continues automatically until PV ≈ SP and $e \to 0$.

---

**(b) (7 marks):**

**Role of integral action in eliminating steady-state error (4 marks):**

In a purely proportional controller, the controller output is $m(t) = K_c e(t)$. At steady state, for the MV to remain non-zero and the process to maintain its output, a **non-zero error** is required — this is the **offset**. In a PI controller, the integral term keeps accumulating the error over time:

$$m(t) = K_c e(t) + \frac{K_c}{T_i} \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau$$

As long as any error persists (even a small one), the integral keeps growing, increasing the MV and pushing the process output closer to the SP. In the limit, $e(t) \to 0$ for a step change in SP, but the integral term holds a **constant** MV value equal to that required for the new operating point. This is why the PI controller gives **zero steady-state error for step inputs**.

For the temperature loop with $K_c = 2$ and $T_i = 5$ min: a 20 °C step change initially gives a jump MV of $2 \times 20 = 40$ (in scaled units), and the integral term ramps the MV at a rate of $(2/5) \times e = 0.4 e$ per minute, gradually bringing the temperature from 80 °C to exactly 100 °C.

**LabVIEW monitoring (3 marks):**

- Use a **thermocouple module (NI TC-01/TC-02)** or **RTD input module** connected to a DAQ chassis.
- Configure the **DAQ Assistant** to read the temperature sensor output (PV) into **AI0** and the op-amp controller output (MV) into **AI1** at a sampling rate of 1 Hz (sufficient for a 5-minute time-constant loop).
- **Front Panel** should include:
  - **Waveform Chart "PV (°C)"** — shows live temperature trend with a red trend line.
  - **Waveform Chart "MV (V or %)"** — shows controller output (heater power).
  - **Numeric Control "Set Point"** with up/down arrows (default 100 °C).
  - **Numeric Indicators** for current PV, current MV, and error $e = \text{SP} - \text{PV}$.
  - **LED indicators** for "PV within ±2% of SP" (green when satisfied).
  - **Stop button** to terminate the While Loop.
- Use **Write to Measurement File** (LVM format) to log both signals with timestamps.
- Optionally add a **cursor** on the waveform charts to read settling time and overshoot directly from the graph.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Sign error in transfer function:** Many students forget the **negative sign** in the inverting PI configuration. If asked for the transfer function, **always include $-$** with a justification: "inverting input → output is 180° out of phase."
> 2. **Confusing $K_c$ and $1/K_c$:** The op-amp gain is $R_f / R_1$, **not** $R_1 / R_f$. Memorize: gain = **feedback / input**.
> 3. **Units of $T_i$:** When the problem gives $T_i$ in **minutes**, convert to **seconds** before computing $C_f$ in farads.
> 4. **Forgetting the parallel feedback $R_f$:** A true pure integrator has **only $C_f$** in feedback (with $R_1$ at input). The PI controller has **both $R_f$ and $C_f$** in **parallel**.
> 5. **LabVIEW connection error:** Specifying "AI0" without justifying the channel and the **scaling** (e.g., 0–10 V → 0–100 °C) loses 1–2 marks. Always explain the **engineering-unit conversion** in your block diagram.
> 6. **Sampling rate violation:** If the plant time constant is 5 min, sampling at 10 kHz wastes resources; at 0.01 Hz, the loop is too slow. Apply **Nyquist** to the dominant plant dynamics.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize Before the Exam**

- ✅ **PI Controller Equation (time):** $m(t) = K_c e(t) + \dfrac{K_c}{T_i} \int e\, dt$
- ✅ **PI Transfer Function (s-domain):** $G_c(s) = K_c\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s}\right)$
- ✅ **Op-amp PI components:** $R_1$ (input), $R_f$ (feedback resistor), $C_f$ (feedback capacitor)
- ✅ **Design Equations:** $K_c = R_f / R_1$ ; $\quad T_i = R_f \cdot C_f$
- ✅ **Standard $R_1$ range:** 10 kΩ to 100 kΩ
- ✅ **Standard $C_f$ range:** 1 µF to 100 µF (tantalum/electrolytic)
- ✅ **Error definition:** $e(t) = \text{SP} - \text{PV}$ (always subtract measured from desired)
- ✅ **Sign of inverting PI output:** Negative (180° phase reversal)
- ✅ **Zero steady-state error for step input** — key advantage of PI over P
- ✅ **LabVIEW DAQ channel mapping:** PV → AI0, MV → AI1, SP → internal numeric control
- ✅ **Sampling rate (Nyquist):** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$, typically 5×–10× for control loops
- ✅ **Common op-amps:** µA741 (general), LM358 (single supply), TL081 (low noise, fast), OP07 (precision)
- ✅ **Dual supply required for µA741:** ±12 V or ±15 V
- ✅ **Output saturation:** MV limited to $V_{sat} \approx \pm 12$ V (one diode drop below supply rail)
- ✅ **Effect of increasing $K_c$:** Faster response, more overshoot, possible oscillation
- ✅ **Effect of decreasing $T_i$:** Stronger integral action, faster offset removal, more overshoot
- ✅ **LabVIEW file format for data logging:** `.lvm` (LabVIEW Measurement) or `.csv` (CSV)
- ✅ **Closed-loop stability check:** All roots of $1 + G_c(s) G_p(s) = 0$ must have negative real parts
- ✅ **Front panel essentials:** Waveform charts (PV, MV), Numeric Control (SP), Numeric Indicators (PV, MV, e), Stop button
- ✅ **Block diagram essentials:** DAQ Assistant × 2, While Loop, Wait (ms), Write to Measurement File
- ✅ **Tuning heuristic (Ziegler-Nichols open-loop):** $K_c = 0.9 \tau / (K_p L)$ and $T_i = 3L$ (mention if asked)
